topics.title: Nickel Metal Hydride Battery
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT501 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nickel Metal Hydride Battery

# 🔋 Nickel Metal Hydride (NiMH) Battery — KTU Premier Study Notes

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Nickel Metal Hydride (NiMH) Battery:** A rechargeable alkaline secondary battery that employs a **nickel oxyhydroxide (NiOOH) positive electrode**, a **hydrogen-absorbing metal alloy negative electrode**, and a **potassium hydroxide (KOH) aqueous alkaline electrolyte**. During charge/discharge, hydrogen ions are shuttled between electrodes via the electrolyte while the metal alloy reversibly absorbs/releases hydrogen to store/release electrical energy.

The NiMH cell is classified under the family of **alkaline secondary batteries** and was developed as an environmentally superior successor to the Nickel Cadmium (NiCd) cell, eliminating the use of toxic Cadmium (Cd) while delivering roughly **1.2 V nominal cell voltage** and **30–40% higher specific energy** than NiCd.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hydrogen Sponge" Model

Imagine a **sponge soaked in water** sitting inside a sealed water tank connected to a pressurized pump:

- **The sponge (Metal Alloy Anode)** = Absorbs and releases hydrogen atoms reversibly during charge/discharge.
- **The pressurized tank (NiOOH Cathode)** = Provides the chemical potential difference.
- **The water in between (KOH Electrolyte)** = Acts as the ionic highway for H⁺/OH⁻ transport.
- **The pump (External Circuit)** = Pushes electrons through your car's headlamp or ECU when discharging.

When you charge the system (apply external voltage), hydrogen is "squeezed out" of the sponge back into the tank. When you discharge (load connected), the sponge re-absorbs hydrogen, releasing electrons that power your load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** NiMH was the *dominant* battery chemistry in **first-generation hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs)** such as the **Toyota Prius (1997–2015 generations)** before Lithium-ion took over post-2015 due to higher specific energy.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| **Nominal Cell Voltage** | **1.2 V** |
| **Theoretical Specific Energy** | **≈ 240 Wh/kg** |
| **Practical Specific Energy** | **60 – 120 Wh/kg** |
| **Energy Density (Volumetric)** | **140 – 300 Wh/L** |
| **Cycle Life** | **500 – 2000 cycles** |
| **Self-Discharge Rate** | **~20 – 30 % per month** |
| **Operating Temperature** | **−20 °C to +45 °C** |
| **Memory Effect** | **Negligible (vs. NiCd)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cell Architecture — Component Breakdown

A single NiMH cell is composed of the following functional layers (from anode to cathode):

1. **Negative Electrode (Anode):** A hydrogen-storage intermetallic alloy — typically **AB₅-type** (LaNi₅) or **AB₂-type** (TiNi₂ / ZrNi₂) Misch-metal based alloys. Modern variants use rare-earth-rich compositions such as **La₀.₆Ce₀.₄Ni₃.₆Co₀.₇Mn₀.₄Al₀.₃**.
2. **Separator:** A porous, non-woven polyolefin membrane soaked in electrolyte — provides ionic conduction while preventing electronic short.
3. **Electrolyte:** **~30 % KOH aqueous solution** (alkaline) with small additions of LiOH for improved cycle life.
4. **Positive Electrode (Cathode):** **Nickel hydroxide Ni(OH)₂** impregnated in a nickel foam substrate; charged state corresponds to **NiOOH**.
5. **Current Collectors:** Nickel-plated steel tabs for both electrodes.
6. **Cell Casing:** Hermetically sealed cylindrical (e.g., AA, D) or prismatic metal case with a **safety vent** for H₂ gas release under overpressure.

### 2.2 Electrochemistry — Charge & Discharge Reactions

**During DISCHARGE (battery delivers current — vehicle accessories ON):**

- **Anode (Oxidation):**
$$\text{MH} + \text{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^{-}$$

- **Cathode (Reduction):**
$$\text{NiOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^{-}$$

- **Net Cell Reaction:**
$$\text{MH} + \text{NiOOH} \longrightarrow \text{M} + \text{Ni(OH)}_2$$

**During CHARGE (regenerative braking / external charger):**
$$\text{M} + \text{Ni(OH)}_2 \longrightarrow \text{MH} + \text{NiOOH}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic for Anode Half-Cell:** *"M-H bond BREAKS to release e⁻"* — Metal hydride releases hydrogen as electron during discharge.

### 2.3 Why NiMH over NiCd? — Engineering Rationale

- **No toxic Cd** → RoHS compliant.
- **Higher specific energy** → ~ 80 Wh/kg vs. NiCd's ~ 50 Wh/kg.
- **Lower memory effect** → Random partial discharge is acceptable.
- **Hydrogen shuttle mechanism** → No metallic ion transport, reducing dendrite formation.
- **Drawback:** Higher self-discharge and heat generation during fast charging.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nernst Cell EMF** | $E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | V (Volts) | Open-circuit voltage vs. SoC |
| 2 | **Specific Energy** | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}}{m_{cell}}$ | Wh/kg | Range estimation per kg |
| 3 | **Energy Density (Volumetric)** | $E_{vol} = \dfrac{V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}}{V_{cell}}$ | Wh/L | Packaging constraints |
| 4 | **State of Charge (SoC)** | $\text{SoC} = \dfrac{C_{remaining}}{C_{rated}} \times 100$ | % | Battery Management System |
| 5 | **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** | $\text{DoD} = 100 - \text{SoC}$ | % | Cycle life modeling |
| 6 | **C-Rate Definition** | $I = C_{rate} \times C_{rated}$ | A | Charging/discharging current |
| 7 | **Hydrogen Storage Reaction (P-C-T)** | $\ln P_{H_2} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{RT} - \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}$ | atm | Alloy thermodynamics |
| 8 | **Charge Efficiency (Coulombic)** | $\eta_C = \dfrac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100$ | % | Round-trip loss |
| 9 | **Pack Energy (Series-Parallel)** | $E_{pack} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot V_{cell} \cdot C_{cell}$ | Wh | HV battery sizing |
| 10 | **Internal Heat Generation** | $Q = I^{2} \cdot R_{int}$ | W | Thermal management |

> Where: $E^{\circ}_{cell} \approx \mathbf{1.32\ V}$, $n = \mathbf{1\ electron/H\ atom}$, $F = \mathbf{96485\ C/mol}$, $R = \mathbf{8.314\ J/(mol\cdot K)}$.

### 2.5 Real-World Automotive Utility

- **Toyota Prius Gen II & III (2003–2015):** NiMH pack rated **1.31 kWh, 201.6 V nominal**, 28 modules of 6 cells each.
- **Honda Insight (1999):** First mass-market hybrid using NiMH D-cells.
- **Ford Escape Hybrid (2005):** 330 V NiMH traction pack.
- **Non-automotive:** Power tools, two-way radios, medical devices, emergency lighting.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Open-Circuit Voltage from Nernst Equation

**Objective:** Derive the equilibrium cell potential of a NiMH cell as a function of state of charge using thermodynamics.

**Step 1 — Gibbs Free Energy & Cell Potential Relationship:**

The maximum reversible electrical work obtainable from an electrochemical cell equals the change in Gibbs free energy of the cell reaction:

$$\Delta G = -n \cdot F \cdot E_{cell}$$

**Step 2 — Reaction Quotient at Arbitrary SoC:**

Let the extent of reaction $\xi$ represent the fractional discharge. The reaction quotient for the net reaction $\text{MH} + \text{NiOOH} \longrightarrow \text{M} + \text{Ni(OH)}_2$ is:

$$Q = \dfrac{a_{M} \cdot a_{Ni(OH)_2}}{a_{MH} \cdot a_{NiOOH}} = \dfrac{1 - \xi}{\xi}$$

(since activities of pure solids = 1, and the concentration ratios simplify with the extent of reaction).

**Step 3 — Apply the Nernst Equation:**

For any general electrochemical cell:

$$E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} - \dfrac{R \cdot T}{n \cdot F} \cdot \ln(Q)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Q and Isolate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{cell} &= E^{\circ}_{cell} - \dfrac{R \cdot T}{n \cdot F} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{1-\xi}{\xi}\right) \\
E_{cell} &= E^{\circ}_{cell} + \dfrac{R \cdot T}{n \cdot F} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{\xi}{1-\xi}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at Standard Conditions (T = 298 K):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{R \cdot T}{F} &= \dfrac{8.314 \times 298}{96485} \\
\dfrac{R \cdot T}{F} &= \dfrac{2477.572}{96485} \\
\dfrac{R \cdot T}{F} &= 0.02569\ \text{V} \\
\dfrac{R \cdot T}{n \cdot F} &= \dfrac{0.02569}{1} = 0.02569\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Final Cell Voltage Expression:**

$$\boxed{\,E_{cell}(\xi) = 1.32 + 0.02569 \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{\xi}{1-\xi}\right)\ \text{V}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Validation at $\xi = 0.5$ (50% SoC):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{cell} &= 1.32 + 0.02569 \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{0.5}{0.5}\right) \\
E_{cell} &= 1.32 + 0.02569 \cdot \ln(1) \\
E_{cell} &= 1.32 + 0 \\
E_{cell} &= 1.32\ \text{V} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms $E^{\circ}_{cell}$ is the mid-SoC voltage — an important KTU takeaway.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Specific Energy of a NiMH Cell

**Objective:** Compute the theoretical specific energy of an AB₅ NiMH cell based on atomic masses.

**Given Molecular Masses:**
- M(NiOOH) = 58.69 + 16.00 + 1.008 = **91.7 g/mol**
- M(MH) ≈ **92.5 g/mol** (LaNi₅H₆ averaged)

**Total mass of reactants per electron transferred:**

$$m_{eq} = \dfrac{M_{NiOOH} + M_{MH}}{n} = \dfrac{91.7 + 92.5}{1} = 184.2\ \text{g/eq}$$

**Faraday Constant Conversion (Coulombs → Ah):**

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= 96485\ \text{C/mol} \\
F_{Ah} &= \dfrac{96485}{3600} = 26.801\ \text{Ah/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Capacity per kg of reactants:**

$$Q_{sp} = \dfrac{1000}{184.2} \times 26.801 = 145.5\ \text{Ah/kg}$$

**Theoretical Specific Energy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{sp}^{theo} &= V_{nom} \times Q_{sp} \\
E_{sp}^{theo} &= 1.2\ \text{V} \times 145.5\ \text{Ah/kg} \\
E_{sp}^{theo} &= 174.6\ \text{Wh/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Note:** The textbook value of ~240 Wh/kg arises when using the lower equivalent weight of pure $\text{H}_2$ (1 g/eq → 26,801 Ah/kg → × 1.32 V ≈ 35,377 Wh/kg theoretical hydrogen limit), but for practical NiMH alloys the **realistic theoretical ceiling is ~ 200 Wh/kg**, and practical is **60–120 Wh/kg** due to inactive components (current collectors, electrolyte, casing, separator).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — NiMH vs Competing Chemistries

| Parameter | NiMH | NiCd | Li-ion (NMC) | Lead-Acid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal Voltage | 1.2 V | 1.2 V | 3.7 V | 2.0 V |
| Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | 60–120 | 40–60 | 150–250 | 30–50 |
| Cycle Life | 500–2000 | 1000–2000 | 1000–5000 | 300–500 |
| Self-Discharge (%/month) | 20–30 | 10–15 | 2–5 | 4–6 |
| Memory Effect | Low | High | None | None |
| Toxicity | Low (no Cd) | High (Cd) | Low | Medium (Pb) |
| Cost (per kWh) | Moderate | Moderate | High | Low |
| HEV Suitability | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — NiMH Cell Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Load or Charger] --> B[Cell Terminals and Current Collectors]
    B --> C[Positive Electrode NiOOH Cathode]
    B --> D[Negative Electrode Metal Hydride MH Anode]
    C <--> E[Porous Separator KOH Electrolyte Path]
    D <--> E
    C --> F[Ni Foam Substrate]
    D --> G[AB5 Alloy Powder LaNi5 Based]
    H[Safety Vent and Pressure Relief] -.-> B
    I[Thermal Sensor] -.-> B
    J[Battery Management System BMS] --> A
    K[State of Charge Estimator] --> J
    L[Temperature Controller] --> J
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence — Charge/Discharge Hydrogen Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CHARGER as Charger or Regenerative Braking
    participant ANODE as MH Anode
    participant SEP as KOH Electrolyte
    participant CATHODE as NiOOH Cathode
    participant LOAD as Vehicle Load

    Note over CHARGER,LOAD: DISCHARGE MODE
    LOAD->>CATHODE: Accepts electrons
    CATHODE->>SEP: Releases OH minus ions
    SEP->>ANODE: Transports OH minus to anode
    ANODE->>LOAD: Sends electrons through external circuit
    Note right of ANODE: MH plus OH minus produces M plus H2O plus electron

    Note over CHARGER,LOAD: CHARGE MODE
    CHARGER->>ANODE: Forces electrons in
    ANODE->>SEP: M absorbs H from H2O
    SEP->>CATHODE: OH minus migrates to cathode
    CATHODE->>CHARGER: NiOOH reformed releases electron
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — HEV Battery Pack Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MOD1[Module 1: 6 Cells in Series]
        C1A[Cell 1.2V] --> C2A[Cell 1.2V]
        C2A --> C3A[Cell 1.2V]
        C3A --> C4A[Cell 1.2V]
        C4A --> C5A[Cell 1.2V]
        C5A --> C6A[Cell 1.2V]
    end
    subgraph MOD2[Module 2: 6 Cells in Series]
        C1B[Cell 1.2V] --> C2B[Cell 1.2V]
        C2B --> C3B[Cell 1.2V]
        C3B --> C4B[Cell 1.2V]
        C4B --> C5B[Cell 1.2V]
        C5B --> C6B[Cell 1.2V]
    end
    MOD1 --> MOD2
    MOD2 --> BMS[Battery Management System]
    BMS --> INVERTER[Traction Inverter]
    INVERTER --> MG1[Motor Generator 1]
    INVERTER --> MG2[Motor Generator 2]
    BMS --> COOLING[Liquid Cooling Loop]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a Nickel Metal Hydride battery. State any two advantages it offers over a Nickel Cadmium battery.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Nickel Metal Hydride (NiMH) battery is a **rechargeable alkaline secondary cell** in which the positive electrode is **nickel oxyhydroxide (NiOOH)**, the negative electrode is a **hydrogen-absorbing metal alloy (MH)**, and the electrolyte is an **aqueous potassium hydroxide (KOH) solution** of about 30% concentration. The cell operates by the **reversible absorption and desorption of hydrogen** by the metal alloy at the anode.

**Two Advantages over NiCd (any two, 1.5 marks each):**

1. **Higher specific energy:** NiMH delivers **60–120 Wh/kg** versus NiCd's **40–60 Wh/kg**, providing greater driving range per unit mass — critical for HEV applications.
2. **Environmental compliance:** NiMH uses no toxic **Cadmium**, making it RoHS-compliant and easier to recycle, whereas NiCd requires special hazardous-waste disposal.
3. *(Optional)* Lower memory effect allowing flexible partial-state charging.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Write the half-cell and overall cell reactions during the discharge of a NiMH battery.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

During discharge of a NiMH cell, the following reactions occur:

**Anode (oxidation, 1 mark):**
$$\text{MH} + \text{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^{-}$$

**Cathode (reduction, 1 mark):**
$$\text{NiOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^{-}$$

**Overall cell reaction (1 mark):**
$$\text{MH} + \text{NiOOH} \longrightarrow \text{M} + \text{Ni(OH)}_2$$

Here, "M" denotes the hydrogen-desorbed metal alloy and "MH" denotes its hydride form.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working principle** of a Nickel Metal Hydride battery. Discuss the role of the metal hydride electrode and the KOH electrolyte. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A NiMH battery pack used in a hybrid electric vehicle consists of **28 modules connected in series**, each module containing **6 cells in series with a parallel pair**. Each cell has a nominal voltage of **1.2 V** and a rated capacity of **6.5 Ah**. Calculate: **(i)** the **total pack voltage**, **(ii)** the **total pack capacity in Ah**, (iii) the **total energy stored in kWh**, and (iv) the **specific energy** if the pack mass is **35 kg**. Compare your result with a typical Li-ion pack of the same mass. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### (a) Construction & Working Principle [7 marks]

A NiMH cell is built as a **rolled or stacked electrode sandwich** sealed inside a cylindrical or prismatic nickel-plated steel can.

**Construction (sketch description — 3 marks):**

```
┌────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Safety Vent  ●                    │
│ ┌──────────────────────────────┐   │
│ │  Cathode Cap (+)  NiOOH      │   │
│ ├──────────────────────────────┤   │
│ │  Separator (porous, KOH-soaked)│  │
│ ├──────────────────────────────┤   │
│ │  Anode (−)  AB₅ Alloy Powder │   │
│ │   impregnated with H          │   │
│ └──────────────────────────────┘   │
│  Can (−)  Nickel-plated steel      │
└────────────────────────────────────┘
```

- **Cathode (+):** Nickel hydroxide Ni(OH)₂ pasted into a nickel foam current collector; charged state is NiOOH.
- **Separator:** Non-woven polyolefin soaked in ~ 30% KOH electrolyte.
- **Anode (−):** AB₅-type alloy (e.g., LaNi₅) or AB₂-type (e.g., TiNi₂), capable of reversibly absorbing hydrogen up to H/M ≈ 1.0.
- **Electrolyte:** KOH + small LiOH additive; provides OH⁻ ionic conductivity (~ 0.5 S/cm).
- **Safety vent:** Releases H₂ if internal pressure exceeds ~ 2 MPa.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

During **discharge**, the metal hydride MH releases its hydrogen atom, which combines with OH⁻ from the electrolyte to form H₂O and an electron. The electron travels through the external load to the cathode, where NiOOH is reduced back to Ni(OH)₂. The OH⁻ ions regenerated at the cathode close the ionic loop through the separator.

During **charge**, the reactions reverse: water at the anode is split, hydrogen is absorbed into the alloy (forming MH again), and Ni(OH)₂ is re-oxidized to NiOOH.

The **role of the metal hydride electrode** is to act as a **hydrogen reservoir**, storing up to one hydrogen atom per metal atom (e.g., LaNi₅H₆). The **KOH electrolyte** provides OH⁻ as the charge carrier — since the reaction involves only H⁺/OH⁻ shuttling (no metallic ion), the electrolyte concentration remains essentially constant throughout the cycle, giving NiMH its excellent **reversibility and long cycle life**.

---

#### (b) Numerical Calculation [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- Modules in series: **N_s = 28**
- Cells per module (series): **6**
- Parallel pairs per module: **2** (i.e., 2 cells in parallel)
- Cell nominal voltage: **V_cell = 1.2 V**
- Cell capacity: **C_cell = 6.5 Ah**
- Pack mass: **m = 35 kg**

**(i) Total Pack Voltage (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{pack} &= N_{modules} \times N_{cells\_in\_series\_per\_module} \times V_{cell} \\
V_{pack} &= 28 \times 6 \times 1.2 \\
V_{pack} &= 168 \times 1.2 \\
V_{pack} &= \mathbf{201.6\ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Total Pack Capacity in Ah (2 marks):**

Capacity in series is unchanged (no multiplication); parallel cells add up:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{module} &= 2\ \text{cells in parallel} \times 6.5\ \text{Ah} \\
C_{module} &= 13\ \text{Ah} \\
C_{pack} &= 13\ \text{Ah} \quad \text{(series connection preserves Ah)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,C_{pack} = 13\ \text{Ah}\,}$$

**(iii) Total Pack Energy in kWh (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{pack} &= V_{pack} \times C_{pack} \\
E_{pack} &= 201.6 \times 13 \\
E_{pack} &= 2620.8\ \text{Wh} \\
E_{pack} &= \mathbf{2.62\ kWh}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Specific Energy (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{sp} &= \dfrac{E_{pack}}{m_{pack}} \\
E_{sp} &= \dfrac{2620.8}{35} \\
E_{sp} &= \mathbf{74.9\ Wh/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comparison with Li-ion (1 mark):**

A typical Li-ion NMC pack of the same **35 kg** mass would deliver approximately:
$$E_{Li-ion} = 35\ \text{kg} \times 150\ \text{Wh/kg} = 5250\ \text{Wh} = \mathbf{5.25\ kWh}$$

Therefore, **the Li-ion pack stores ~ 2× more energy** than the equivalent NiMH pack at the same mass, which is why modern HEVs (post-2015) have largely migrated to Li-ion technology.

---

### **Question B (Alternative 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **charging methods** used for NiMH batteries in hybrid electric vehicles. Discuss **constant-current charging, delta-V detection, and temperature-cutoff** strategies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Nernst equation**, derive the open-circuit voltage of a NiMH cell as a function of state of charge (SoC). Evaluate the cell voltage when the cell is at **20% and 80% SoC** at a temperature of **298 K**, given $E^{\circ}_{cell} = 1.32\ \text{V}$ and $n = 1$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### (a) NiMH Charging Methods [7 marks]

HEV NiMH battery packs require **precise charging control** to maximize life and prevent thermal runaway. Three primary methods are used:

**1. Constant-Current Charging (CC) — 2 marks:**

A fixed current (typically 0.5C to 1C, i.e., 6.5 A for a 6.5 Ah cell) is applied until a pre-defined termination criterion is reached. This method is simple but risks **overcharge** if not terminated properly, leading to gas generation and venting.

**2. Delta-V (ΔV) Cut-off Detection — 3 marks:**

NiMH cells exhibit a **small but characteristic voltage drop** (ΔV ≈ −5 to −10 mV per cell) at the moment of full charge due to oxygen recombination onset at the cathode. The charger continuously monitors $dV/dt$ and terminates when a negative inflection is detected. The Toyota Prius BMS uses this method for **accurate 100% SoC detection** without relying on timers.

**3. Temperature Cut-off (TCO) — 2 marks:**

As a **safety backup**, the BMS monitors cell temperature and terminates charging if $T > 45\ °C$ or if the rate of temperature rise $dT/dt$ exceeds **1 °C/min**. This prevents thermal runaway from internal pressure buildup.

**Additional techniques** used in modern HEVs include **pulse charging** and **regenerative-braking integration** where charging current is dynamically modulated by the motor controller.

---

#### (b) Nernst Equation Derivation & SoC Voltage Evaluation [7 marks]

**Derivation (4 marks):**

For the overall cell reaction $\text{MH} + \text{NiOOH} \longrightarrow \text{M} + \text{Ni(OH)}_2$, the Nernst equation gives:

$$E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

Since activities of pure solids are unity and the reaction quotient depends on the fractional discharge $\xi$:

$$Q = \dfrac{1-\xi}{\xi}$$

where $\xi$ is the extent of reaction (= fractional discharge). Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{cell} &= E^{\circ}_{cell} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\dfrac{1-\xi}{\xi}\right) \\
E_{cell} &= E^{\circ}_{cell} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\dfrac{\xi}{1-\xi}\right) \\
\dfrac{RT}{F} &= \dfrac{8.314 \times 298}{96485} = 0.02569\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{cell}(\xi) = 1.32 + 0.02569 \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{\xi}{1-\xi}\right)\ \text{V}\,}$$

**Evaluation (3 marks):**

**At 20% SoC (SoC = 20% means remaining charge = 20%, hence $\xi$ discharged = 0.80, so $1-\xi = 0.20$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{cell} &= 1.32 + 0.02569 \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{0.80}{0.20}\right) \\
E_{cell} &= 1.32 + 0.02569 \cdot \ln(4) \\
E_{cell} &= 1.32 + 0.02569 \cdot 1.3863 \\
E_{cell} &= 1.32 + 0.0356 \\
E_{cell} &= \mathbf{1.356\ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**At 80% SoC (SoC = 80%, discharged = 20%, so $\xi = 0.20$, $1-\xi = 0.80$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{cell} &= 1.32 + 0.02569 \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{0.20}{0.80}\right) \\
E_{cell} &= 1.32 + 0.02569 \cdot \ln(0.25) \\
E_{cell} &= 1.32 + 0.02569 \cdot (-1.3863) \\
E_{cell} &= 1.32 - 0.0356 \\
E_{cell} &= \mathbf{1.284\ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Observation:** At higher SoC the voltage is *lower* (closer to nominal 1.2 V) and at lower SoC the voltage is *higher*. This counterintuitive behavior reflects the alkaline cell's negative voltage coefficient with state of charge — a hallmark KTU conceptual question.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up charge and discharge half-reactions** — Many students incorrectly write oxidation at the cathode. Remember: **ANODE is always where OXIDATION occurs** (regardless of charge/discharge mode).
> 2. **Confusing SoC and DoD** — SoC + DoD = 100%. At 20% SoC, the cell is 80% discharged.
> 3. **Sign error in Nernst equation** — Note $\ln(\xi/(1-\xi))$ has a **positive** sign for the natural log, not negative. Many students write it with the wrong sign and get reverse voltage curves.
> 4. **Forgetting units in energy calculation** — Always convert Wh → kWh by dividing by 1000.
> 5. **Series vs Parallel confusion** — Voltage adds in **series**; capacity adds in **parallel**. State this clearly in your answer.
> 6. **Memory effect:** Do NOT claim NiMH has zero memory effect — it has *negligible* but non-zero memory effect.
> 7. **Nominal vs theoretical specific energy** — Do not write 240 Wh/kg as the practical value; it is the theoretical upper bound.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — NiMH Battery**

- [x] **Cell Voltage:** Nominal **1.2 V**; Standard EMF $E^{\circ}_{cell} = \mathbf{1.32\ V}$.
- [x] **Chemistry:** Anode = MH (hydrogen-storage alloy), Cathode = NiOOH/Ni(OH)₂, Electrolyte = **KOH (~30%)**.
- [x] **Net Reaction:** $\text{MH} + \text{NiOOH} \rightleftharpoons \text{M} + \text{Ni(OH)}_2$
- [x] **Anode half-reaction (discharge):** $\text{MH} + \text{OH}^- \to \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^-$
- [x] **Cathode half-reaction (discharge):** $\text{NiOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \to \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^-$
- [x] **Specific Energy:** Practical **60–120 Wh/kg**; Theoretical ceiling ~ **200 Wh/kg**.
- [x] **Cycle Life:** **500–2000 cycles** at 80% DoD.
- [x] **Self-Discharge:** ~ **20–30% per month** (significant drawback).
- [x] **Operating Temperature:** **−20 °C to +45 °C**.
- [x] **Anode Alloys:** **AB₅** (LaNi₅) most common; **AB₂** (TiNi₂) for higher capacity.
- [x] **Advantages over NiCd:** Higher specific energy, no toxic Cd, RoHS-compliant, low memory effect.
- [x] **Disadvantages vs Li-ion:** Lower specific energy, higher self-discharge, thermal runaway risk during fast charging.
- [x] **HEV Application:** Toyota Prius (Gen I–III), Honda Insight, Ford Escape Hybrid.
- [x] **Nernst Voltage:** $E = E^{\circ} + 0.02569 \cdot \ln[\xi / (1-\xi)]$ at 298 K.
- [x] **Charging Methods:** Constant Current (CC), ΔV Cut-off, Temperature Cut-off (TCO).
- [x] **Safety Vent:** Mandatory for H₂ gas release at overpressure.

> **End of Module 1 — NiMH Battery Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education framework*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lithium-Ion Battery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT501 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lithium-Ion Battery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Lithium-Ion Battery (Li-ion / LIB)** is a rechargeable electrochemical energy storage device that utilizes the reversible intercalation (insertion) and de-intercalation (extraction) of lithium ions between two intercalation host electrodes — a **negative electrode (anode)** and a **positive electrode (cathode)** — separated by a lithium-ion conducting electrolyte and a microporous polymeric separator. The energy conversion is governed by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**, and the nominal cell voltage typically ranges between **3.2 V to 4.2 V** depending on the cathode chemistry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Definition (Board-Standard Wording):**
> A Lithium-Ion battery is a *secondary (rechargeable) battery* in which lithium ions move from the *negative electrode* to the *positive electrode* during discharge, and back during charging. The electrolytes allow ionic movement, while electrons flow through the external circuit to deliver electrical energy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of a Lithium-Ion battery as a **two-city bus shuttle system** running along a dedicated highway:

| Component in Battery | Real-World Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Anode (Graphite)** | City A (Lithium reserve city) | Stores lithium ions when battery is charged |
| **Cathode (LiCoO₂ / NMC / LFP)** | City B (Lithium receiving city) | Releases lithium ions when battery is charged |
| **Electrolyte** | The highway itself | Allows only Li⁺ ions to commute (not electrons) |
| **Separator** | Highway median / divider | Prevents the two cities from colliding (short circuit) |
| **External Circuit** | The road tunnel for cars (electrons) | Forces electrons to take a longer detour to do work |

When discharging: the bus (Li⁺) travels from City A → Highway → City B, while cars (e⁻) take the tunnel and power your EV motor.
When charging: the highway direction reverses — buses return to City A, ready for the next trip.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in LIBs:**
> - Faraday's Constant: **F = 96,485 C/mol**
> - Electron charge: **e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
> - Standard Li/Li⁺ potential: **−3.04 V vs. SHE**
> - Typical Energy Density Range: **100 – 265 Wh/kg** (cell level)
> - Operating Temperature Window: **−20 °C to +60 °C**

## 1.3 Why Lithium-Ion Dominates Automotive Applications

The selection of Li-ion chemistry in modern Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) and Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs) is governed by the **trade-off between specific energy, specific power, cycle life, safety, and cost**. Lithium, being the **lightest metal (atomic mass 6.94 g/mol)** with the **highest electrochemical potential**, enables the highest theoretical specific energy among all commercial battery chemistries.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exam:** Always remember — the *anode* is where **oxidation** occurs during **discharge** (and reduction during charging), while the *cathode* is where **reduction** occurs during **discharge** (and oxidation during charging). Mnemonics: **An Ox** (Anode = Oxidation), **Red Cat** (Reduction = Cathode).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Architecture of a Li-ion Cell

A single Li-ion cell has a **jelly-roll (spiral wound)** or **stacked (prismatic/pouch)** architecture with the following critical layers:

1. **Positive Electrode (Cathode):** A metal foil current collector (typically **Aluminum**, 15–20 μm) coated with the active cathode material. Common chemistries:
   - **LCO** (LiCoO₂) — used in consumer electronics
   - **NMC** (LiNiₓMnᵧCozO₂) — most common in EVs (Tesla, Hyundai, Kia)
   - **LFP** (LiFePO₄) — used in BYD, Tata, MG, entry EVs
   - **NCA** (LiNi₀.₈Co₀.₁₅Al₀.₀₅O₂) — Tesla (in partnership with Panasonic)
2. **Negative Electrode (Anode):** Copper foil current collector (~10 μm) coated with **graphite** (or Si-graphite composite in newer generations).
3. **Separator:** Microporous **polyethylene (PE)** or **polypropylene (PP)** membrane (e.g., Celgard 2325) — thickness ~ 20–25 μm; allows Li⁺ transport but is electronically insulating.
4. **Electrolyte:** Typically **1 M LiPF₆** dissolved in a mixture of organic carbonates — **ethylene carbonate (EC)**, **diethyl carbonate (DEC)**, and **dimethyl carbonate (DMC)**. Conductivity ≈ 10⁻² S/cm.
5. **Current Collectors:** Al for cathode, Cu for anode (Cu cannot be used at cathode due to oxidation at high voltage).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Copper on Anode Side Only?**
> At the high potentials of the cathode (≈ 4.2 V vs. Li/Li⁺), **Aluminum forms a passivating oxide layer** that protects it, while **Copper would corrode/dissolve** at those potentials. Conversely, at the low potential of the anode (≈ 0.05 V vs. Li/Li⁺), **Cu is stable**, but **Al would alloy with Li**.

## 2.2 Electrochemistry of Charge and Discharge

### A. During Discharge (Spontaneous — Galvanic Mode)

$$\text{Cathode (Reduction):} \quad \text{Li}_{1-x}\text{MO}_2 + x\,\text{Li}^+ + x\,e^- \rightarrow \text{LiMO}_2$$

$$\text{Anode (Oxidation):} \quad \text{Li}_x\text{C}_6 \rightarrow \text{C}_6 + x\,\text{Li}^+ + x\,e^-$$

$$\text{Net Reaction:} \quad \text{LiMO}_2 + \text{C}_6 \rightarrow \text{Li}_{1-x}\text{MO}_2 + \text{Li}_x\text{C}_6$$

### B. During Charge (Non-spontaneous — Electrolytic Mode)

The reactions reverse, driven by an external DC source. Electrons are forced from the cathode to the anode through the external circuit, while **Li⁺ ions migrate through the electrolyte from cathode → anode**.

## 2.3 Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV) and Cell Voltage Derivation

The OCV of a cell is given by the **Nernst Equation**:

$$E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln(Q)$$

Where:
- $E^{\circ}_{cell}$ = standard cell potential (V)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $Q$ = reaction quotient

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the **LiCoO₂ / Graphite** couple, $E^{\circ}_{cell} \approx 3.7 \text{ V}$ and $n = 1$ (per Li⁺ transferred). The full charged voltage is **4.2 V**, and the discharge cutoff is typically **2.7 V – 3.0 V**.

## 2.4 The "Why" and "How" — Mechanism Step-by-Step

- **Why Li⁺ and not Na⁺ or K⁺?** Lithium is the lightest alkali metal (M = 6.94 g/mol) and has the smallest ionic radius (76 pm), giving it the highest charge-to-mass ratio → maximum energy density.
- **How does intercalation work?** The layered structure of graphite (anode) and transition metal oxide (cathode) acts like a *sponge* — Li⁺ ions slide in between the layers (van der Waals gaps) without disrupting the host crystal structure.
- **Why graphite and not lithium metal?** Metallic Li dendrites form during cycling, causing internal short circuits and thermal runaway. Graphite accommodates Li⁺ at a low potential (≈ 0.1 V vs. Li/Li⁺) without dendrite formation.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation / Symbol | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Theoretical Specific Energy** | $E_{th} = \dfrac{n \cdot F \cdot V_{oc}}{M_{cell}}$ | Wh/kg | Energy density of cell |
| 2 | **Actual Energy Stored** | $E = V_{nom} \times Q$ (Ah) | Wh | Pack energy calculation |
| 3 | **C-Rate** | $C = \dfrac{I}{Q_{rated}}$ | h⁻¹ | Charge/discharge current |
| 4 | **Coulombic Efficiency** | $\eta_c = \dfrac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100\%$ | % | Cycle quality |
| 5 | **Energy Efficiency** | $\eta_E = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100\%$ | % | Round-trip efficiency |
| 6 | **State of Charge (SOC)** | $SOC = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | % | BMS logic |
| 7 | **State of Health (SOH)** | $SOH = \dfrac{Q_{max, current}}{Q_{max, new}} \times 100\%$ | % | Battery aging |
| 8 | **Nernst Equation** | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | V | Cell voltage prediction |
| 9 | **Depth of Discharge (DOD)** | $DOD = 1 - SOC$ | % | Cycle life link |
| 10 | **Internal Resistance Loss** | $P_{loss} = I^2 R_{int}$ | W | Heat generation |
| 11 | **Arrhenius Aging** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | — | Calendar life |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When writing the Nernst equation, students often confuse the sign convention. Remember: when $Q > 1$ (product-favored), $E < E^{\circ}$, and when $Q < 1$, $E > E^{\circ}$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In automotive systems, Li-ion batteries are used in:
- **Traction Battery Packs** of BEVs (e.g., 60–100 kWh packs, 400 V or 800 V architecture)
- **12 V Auxiliary Batteries** (replacing lead-acid in modern cars)
- **48 V Mild-Hybrid Systems** (MHEV — Belt-Integrated Starter Generator)
- **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEV)** for the high-voltage battery
- **Start-Stop Micro-Hybrid Systems** for high cyclic durability

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Specific Energy of a LiCoO₂ / Graphite Cell

This is a **high-yield KTU 2024 derivation** frequently asked in ESE.

**Step 1 — Identify the half-cell reactions and standard potentials:**

$$\text{Cathode: } \text{LiCoO}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{Li}_{1-x}\text{CoO}_2 + x\,\text{Li}^+ + x\,e^- \quad E^{\circ} = +0.80 \text{ V vs. Li/Li}^+$$

Wait — using **Li/Li⁺ as reference**, we can rewrite:

$$\text{Cathode (Li intercalation):} \quad \text{CoO}_2 + \text{Li}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{LiCoO}_2 \quad E^{\circ}_1 = +0.80 \text{ V}$$

$$\text{Anode (Li intercalation):} \quad \text{Li}^+ + e^- + 6\text{C} \rightarrow \text{LiC}_6 \quad E^{\circ}_2 = 0 \text{ V (reference)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the standard cell EMF:**

$$E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode} = 0.80 - 0 = 0.80 \text{ V vs. Li/Li}^+$$

Hmm — but the actual average operating voltage is **≈ 3.7 V**. This is because we are using the **Li/Li⁺ reference (0 V)**, and the **Co⁴⁺/Co³⁺ redox couple in LiCoO₂ lies at +3.7 V vs. Li/Li⁺** (the cobalt-based potential is +3.7 V when the reference is set to graphite as 0 V).

Let us redo this in the **graphite-reference frame** that is conventional in battery literature:

$$E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{LiCoO_2/Li} - E^{\circ}_{graphite/Li} = 3.7 \text{ V} - 0.1 \text{ V} \approx 3.6 \text{ V to } 3.7 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the molar mass of reactants per mole of electron transferred:**

For the cell reaction: $\text{LiCoO}_2 + \text{C}_6 \rightarrow \text{C}_6 + \text{LiCoO}_2$ (per 1 mol e⁻)

- Mass of LiCoO₂: $M_{LiCoO_2} = 6.94 + 58.93 + (2 \times 16) = 97.87$ g/mol
- Mass of C₆: $M_{C_6} = 6 \times 12.01 = 72.06$ g/mol
- Total mass: $M_{cell} = 97.87 + 72.06 = 169.93$ g/mol

**Step 4 — Apply the theoretical specific energy formula:**

$$E_{th} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot V_{oc}}{M_{cell}}$$

$$E_{th} = \frac{1 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol} \times 3.7 \text{ V}}{169.93 \text{ g/mol}}$$

$$E_{th} = \frac{356{,}994.5 \text{ J/mol}}{169.93 \text{ g/mol}}$$

$$E_{th} = 2100.83 \text{ J/g} = 2100.83 \text{ Wh/kg} \quad \text{(since 1 J/g = 0.2778 Wh/kg)}$$

$$\boxed{E_{th} \approx 583.5 \text{ Wh/kg}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **practical specific energy** of commercial LiCoO₂ cells is only **150–200 Wh/kg** because the full theoretical capacity is never extracted, and the entire cell mass (current collectors, electrolyte, separator, casing) must be accounted for, not just active material.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: SOC Estimation Using Coulomb Counting

**Step 1 — Define the initial state:**

Let $SOC_0$ = initial state of charge (%), $Q_{rated}$ = rated capacity (Ah), $I(t)$ = instantaneous current (A), positive for discharge.

**Step 2 — Set up the differential equation:**

The change in charge with time:

$$\frac{dQ(t)}{dt} = -I(t) \cdot \eta_c$$

where $\eta_c$ is the Coulombic efficiency (≈ 0.999 for Li-ion).

**Step 3 — Integrate over time:**

$$Q(t) = Q_0 - \int_0^t I(\tau) \cdot \eta_c \, d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Express SOC:**

$$SOC(t) = \frac{Q(t)}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\% = \left(SOC_0 - \frac{\eta_c}{Q_{rated}}\int_0^t I(\tau) \, d\tau\right) \times 100\%$$

**Step 5 — Practical discrete form (used in BMS firmware):**

$$SOC_{k+1} = SOC_k - \frac{\eta_c \cdot I_k \cdot \Delta t}{Q_{rated} \times 3600} \times 100\%$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pack Energy and Range Estimation for an EV

**Given:** Battery Pack Voltage $V_{pack} = 400$ V, Capacity $Q_{pack} = 75$ Ah, EV efficiency $\eta_{ev} = 0.18$ kWh/km.

**Step 1 — Pack energy:**

$$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times Q_{pack} = 400 \text{ V} \times 75 \text{ Ah} = 30{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 30 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Usable energy (80% DOD limit):**

$$E_{usable} = 0.8 \times 30 \text{ kWh} = 24 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Range:**

$$R = \frac{E_{usable}}{\eta_{ev}} = \frac{24 \text{ kWh}}{0.18 \text{ kWh/km}} \approx 133.3 \text{ km}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Common Cathode Chemistries

| Parameter | **LCO (LiCoO₂)** | **NMC (LiNiMnCoO₂)** | **LFP (LiFePO₄)** | **NCA (LiNiCoAlO₂)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal Voltage (V) | 3.7 | 3.7 | 3.3 | 3.6 |
| Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | 150–200 | 180–220 | 90–160 | 200–260 |
| Cycle Life (cycles) | 500–1000 | 1000–2000 | 2000–5000 | 500–1000 |
| Safety | Moderate | Moderate | **Excellent** | Moderate |
| Cost | High (Co) | Medium | **Low (no Co)** | High (Co) |
| Thermal Runaway (°C) | ~150 | ~210 | **~270** | ~150 |
| EV Application | Consumer elec. | Hyundai, Kia, VW | BYD, Tata, MG | Tesla (with Panasonic) |
| Environmental | Toxic (Co) | Moderate | **Clean (Fe, P)** | Toxic (Co) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Li-ion Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CATHODE: LiMO2 Active Material] --> A2[Aluminum Foil Current Collector]
    A2 --> A3[Cathode Terminal Positive]
    B1[ANODE: Graphite Active Material] --> B2[Copper Foil Current Collector]
    B2 --> B3[Anode Terminal Negative]
    S1[Microporous Separator PE/PP] --- S2[Blocks Electron Flow]
    S3[Electrolyte LiPF6 in EC/DEC/DMC] --- S4[Conducts Li plus Ions Only]
    R1[External Load Motor or Charger] --- R2[Electron Flow Path]
    A3 --> R1
    R1 --> B3
    S1 -.-> S3
```

## 4.2 Charge-Discharge Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DISCHARGE_MODE
        D1[Anode LiC6 Oxidizes] --> D2[Releases Li plus and e minus]
        D2 --> D3[e minus flow through External Load]
        D2 --> D4[Li plus migrates through Electrolyte]
        D4 --> D5[Li plus intercalates into Cathode LiMO2]
        D5 --> D6[Cathode Li1-xMO2 reduces to LiMO2]
        D3 --> D7[Electrical Work Delivered]
    end
    subgraph CHARGE_MODE
        C1[External DC Source Applied] --> C2[Electrons forced from Cathode to Anode]
        C2 --> C3[Li plus deintercalates from Cathode]
        C3 --> C4[Li plus migrates through Electrolyte]
        C4 --> C5[Li plus intercalates into Anode Graphite]
        C5 --> C6[Energy Stored as Chemical Potential]
    end
```

## 4.3 Battery Management System (BMS) Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Battery Pack 400V 75kWh] --> M1[Cell Voltage Monitoring ADC]
    P1 --> M2[Current Sensor Hall Effect]
    P1 --> M3[Temperature Sensors NTC per module]
    M1 --> C1[Central BMS MCU]
    M2 --> C1
    M3 --> C1
    C1 --> S1[SOC Estimator Coulomb Counting plus Kalman Filter]
    C1 --> S2[SOH Estimator Internal Resistance plus Capacity Fade]
    C1 --> S3[SOP Power Limit Calculator]
    C1 --> S4[Cell Balancing Active or Passive]
    C1 --> S5[Thermal Management Control]
    C1 --> S6[CAN Communication to VCU]
    C1 --> P2[Protective Cutoff Contactors]
```

## 4.4 Voltage Profile Curve (Typical Discharge Curve of LFP vs NMC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[100 percent SOC 4.2V or 3.65V] --> B[Steady Plateau Region 80 percent of Capacity]
    B --> C[Slope Region 20 to 10 percent SOC]
    C --> D[Cutoff Voltage 2.7V or 2.5V]
    E[LFP Curve 3.65V to 2.5V Flat Plateau] --- A
    F[NMC Curve 4.2V to 2.7V Sloped Plateau] --- A
    G[Energy Delivered Integration of V times dQ] --- B
```

## 4.5 Key Operational Notes

- **Electrolyte window stability:** The electrolyte (≈ 1.0 – 4.5 V vs. Li/Li⁺) defines the upper voltage limit. Exceeding 4.3 V causes electrolyte oxidation and **SEI (Solid Electrolyte Interphase)** breakdown.
- **SEI Layer:** A passivating layer (~ 5–50 nm thick) forms on the graphite anode during the first charge. It is composed of Li₂CO₃, LiF, and organic carbonates. It consumes ~ 5–10% of the initial lithium (irreversible capacity loss) but is **critical for safety and cycle life**.
- **Dendrite Risk:** Below 0 °C, Li plating can occur on graphite, forming dendrites that may puncture the separator → **internal short circuit → thermal runaway**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**Define a Lithium-Ion battery. List any two advantages of Li-ion batteries over lead-acid batteries for automotive use.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Lithium-Ion battery** is a rechargeable electrochemical storage device in which lithium ions shuttle between a graphite negative electrode and a transition-metal-oxide positive electrode (e.g., LiCoO₂, NMC, LFP) through a non-aqueous electrolyte, with electrons flowing through the external circuit.

**Two Advantages over Lead-Acid:**
1. **Higher Energy Density:** Li-ion delivers **150–250 Wh/kg** vs. only **30–50 Wh/kg** for lead-acid → 4–5× lighter for the same stored energy.
2. **Longer Cycle Life:** Li-ion offers **1000–3000 cycles** vs. **300–500 cycles** for lead-acid, drastically reducing replacement frequency.
3. *(Bonus)* Lower self-discharge (~ 2% per month vs. 5–15% for lead-acid).
4. *(Bonus)* Maintenance-free (no acid topping-up required).

> [!TIP]
> For 3-mark questions, **definition (1 mark) + 2 advantages (1 mark each)** is the standard KTU valuation pattern. Always quantify with numerical values.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**
**What is the role of the separator in a Lithium-Ion cell? Why is copper used as the anode current collector and aluminum as the cathode current collector?**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of the Separator:**
The **microporous polymeric separator** (PE/PP, ~ 20–25 μm thick) physically separates the anode and cathode to prevent direct contact and internal short circuit, while its nano-scale pores allow **Li⁺ ions to pass through** to complete the internal ionic circuit. It is electronically insulating but ionically conductive (when soaked in electrolyte).

**Why Cu on Anode and Al on Cathode?**
1. At the **low potential of the anode (≈ 0.05 V vs. Li/Li⁺)**, **Al would alloy with lithium** (forming LiAl), making it mechanically unstable. **Copper is chemically inert** at this potential, so Cu is used on the anode side.
2. At the **high potential of the cathode (≈ 3.7–4.2 V vs. Li/Li⁺)**, **Cu would oxidize and dissolve** into the electrolyte. **Aluminum forms a self-passivating Al₂O₃ layer** that prevents further corrosion, making Al the safe choice for the cathode.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation**, Part B questions of 14 marks offer **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both alternatives are given below with full sub-parts and model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working principle of a Lithium-Ion battery with a neat sketch. Describe the charge and discharge reactions.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction** (3 marks): A Li-ion cell consists of:
- **Cathode:** Li-intercalated transition metal oxide (LiCoO₂ / NMC / LFP) coated on an **Aluminum foil** current collector.
- **Anode:** Graphite (or Si-graphite) coated on a **Copper foil** current collector.
- **Separator:** Microporous **polyethylene/polypropylene** membrane, ~ 20–25 μm thick, soaked in electrolyte.
- **Electrolyte:** **1 M LiPF₆** in a mixture of organic carbonates (EC, DEC, DMC).
- **Cell Geometry:** Jelly-roll (cylindrical) or stacked (prismatic/pouch).

**Working Principle** (2 marks): During **discharge**, Li⁺ ions de-intercalate from the graphite anode, travel through the electrolyte, and intercalate into the cathode. Electrons flow through the external circuit, producing electrical work. During **charge**, an external DC source reverses the process.

**Charge Reaction** (1 mark):
$$\text{LiCoO}_2 + \text{C}_6 \xleftarrow{\text{Charge}} \text{Li}_{1-x}\text{CoO}_2 + \text{Li}_x\text{C}_6$$

**Discharge Reaction** (1 mark): The forward direction of the equation above.

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Compare LFP, NMC, and LCO cathode chemistries in terms of specific energy, safety, cycle life, and cost. Justify why LFP is preferred for entry-level EVs in India.

**Model Solution:**

| Property | **LCO (LiCoO₂)** | **NMC (LiNiMnCoO₂)** | **LFP (LiFePO₄)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Energy | 150–200 Wh/kg | 180–220 Wh/kg | **90–160 Wh/kg** |
| Cycle Life | 500–1000 | 1000–2000 | **2000–5000** |
| Safety | Moderate | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| Cost | High (Co) | Medium | **Lowest (no Co)** |
| Thermal Runaway | ~ 150 °C | ~ 210 °C | **~ 270 °C** |
| Voltage | 3.7 V | 3.7 V | 3.3 V |

**Justification for LFP in Indian Entry-Level EVs (3 marks):**
1. **Cost-Effectiveness:** LFP uses **iron and phosphate** — abundant, non-toxic, and cobalt-free. Cobalt is expensive and mined primarily in politically sensitive regions (DRC). LFP reduces cell cost by ~ 20–30%, critical for sub-₹15 lakh EVs (Tata Tiago.ev, MG Comet, BYD Atto 3 base).
2. **Thermal & Safety Superiority:** India's tropical climate pushes cabin and under-hood temperatures above 50 °C. LFP's thermal runaway onset at **270 °C** vs. ~ 150 °C for LCO/NMC provides a critical safety margin.
3. **Longer Cycle Life:** Indian usage patterns involve **frequent slow charging (home AC chargers) and deep DOD**. LFP's 2000–5000 cycle life ensures 8–10 years of usable life, ideal for fleet and personal use.
4. **Lower Maintenance:** LFP is more **tolerant of high SOC storage** (100% SOC is acceptable), unlike NMC which prefers 20–80% SOC for longevity. Simpler BMS requirements.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the function of a Battery Management System (BMS) in an electric vehicle. List the key parameters monitored by the BMS.

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (Block Diagram of BMS)** (2 marks): Refer to **Section 4.3 (BMS Functional Architecture)** above.

**Function of BMS** (3 marks): A BMS is an **electronic control unit (ECU)** that monitors, manages, and protects a rechargeable battery pack. It performs:
1. **Cell Monitoring:** Voltage, current, and temperature measurement at the cell/module level.
2. **SOC & SOH Estimation:** Using Coulomb counting, OCV-SOC lookup, and Kalman filters.
3. **Cell Balancing:** Equalizes cell voltages to prevent overcharge/over-discharge of weaker cells.
4. **Thermal Management:** Controls liquid cooling/heating loops to maintain cells at 15–35 °C.
5. **Safety Protection:** Disconnects contactors during over-voltage, under-voltage, over-current, over-temperature, or short circuit.
6. **Communication:** Reports pack data to the VCU (Vehicle Control Unit) via **CAN bus**.

**Key Parameters Monitored** (2 marks):
- Cell voltage (per cell, typically 2.5–4.2 V)
- Pack voltage (400 V or 800 V)
- Pack current (charge/discharge, up to ±500 A)
- Module temperature (NTC thermistors, −20 to +60 °C)
- Isolation resistance (> 100 Ω/V for HV safety)
- SOC, SOH, SOP (State of Power)

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Derive the theoretical specific energy of a LiCoO₂/Graphite cell. Given: M(LiCoO₂) = 97.87 g/mol, M(C₆) = 72.06 g/mol, F = 96,485 C/mol, n = 1, V_oc = 3.7 V. State why the actual specific energy is much lower than the theoretical value.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Step 1 — Identify the cell reaction per mole of electron:
$$\text{LiCoO}_2 + \text{C}_6 \rightarrow \text{C}_6 + \text{LiCoO}_2 \quad (\text{per 1 mol Li}^+ \text{ transferred})$$

Step 2 — Sum of molar masses of active reactants:
$$M_{cell} = M_{LiCoO_2} + M_{C_6} = 97.87 + 72.06 = 169.93 \text{ g/mol}$$

Step 3 — Apply the specific energy formula:
$$E_{th} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot V_{oc}}{M_{cell}}$$

Step 4 — Substitute:
$$E_{th} = \frac{1 \times 96{,}485 \times 3.7}{169.93} = \frac{356{,}994.5}{169.93} = 2100.84 \text{ J/g}$$

Step 5 — Convert to Wh/kg (× 1/3.6):
$$\boxed{E_{th} = 583.5 \text{ Wh/kg}}$$

**Why Actual << Theoretical** (2 marks):
1. Only **50–70% of theoretical capacity** is practically extractable due to voltage limits, SEI losses, and structural limits of electrodes.
2. **Inactive mass burden:** Current collectors, separator, electrolyte, casing, and BMS add 60–70% extra mass not counted in theoretical calculation.
3. **Discharge curve is sloped:** Average operating voltage is **3.6 V**, not the peak 4.2 V; usable energy is ∫V dQ, not V × Q_max.
4. **C-rate losses:** At high discharge currents, polarization losses (IR drop) reduce delivered energy.
5. **Practical SOC window:** Battery is rarely discharged below 10% or charged above 95%, restricting usable energy to ~ 85% of cell capacity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign confusion in Nernst Equation:** Students often write $E = E^{\circ} + (RT/nF)\ln Q$ instead of the correct minus sign. The minus sign is non-negotiable.
> 2. **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert J/g to Wh/kg (divide by 3.6) costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Anode/Cathode Mislabeling:** During *discharge*, the negative electrode is the **anode (oxidation)** and the positive is the **cathode (reduction)**. During *charge*, these roles reverse (the externally connected anode becomes the cathode of the electrolytic cell). Examiners are strict here.
> 4. **Forgetting to include both electrode masses** in specific energy derivation. Both LiCoO₂ AND C₆ contribute to cell mass.
> 5. **Vague answers on LFP vs NMC:** Always support your claim with **numbers** (cycle life, Wh/kg, °C) — qualitative answers alone lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔋 **Li-ion is a *secondary* (rechargeable) battery** using **intercalation** of Li⁺ into host electrodes.
- 🔋 **Anode (discharge):** Graphite on **Cu** foil, potential ≈ 0.1 V vs. Li/Li⁺.
- 🔋 **Cathode (discharge):** LiMO₂ (LCO/NMC/NCA) or LiFePO₄ on **Al** foil, potential ≈ 3.7 V vs. Li/Li⁺.
- 🔋 **Electrolyte:** 1 M LiPF₆ in EC/DEC/DMC — **non-aqueous** (water reacts violently with Li).
- 🔋 **Separator:** Microporous PE/PP — electronically insulating, ionically conductive.
- 🔋 **Working Voltage Range:** 2.7 V (cutoff) – 4.2 V (full charge) for LCO/NMC; 2.5 V – 3.65 V for LFP.
- 🔋 **Specific Energy Range:** 90–260 Wh/kg (cell-level), 150–200 Wh/kg (pack-level including BMS).
- 🔋 **Coulombic Efficiency:** > 99.9% per cycle.
- 🔋 **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^{\circ} - (RT/nF)\ln Q$ — derivable for any cell.
- 🔋 **Theoretical Energy Formula:** $E_{th} = nFV_{oc}/M_{cell}$ — must include both electrode masses.
- 🔋 **BMS Functions:** Monitor, balance, protect, communicate — without BMS, Li-ion is unsafe.
- 🔋 **LFP Advantages:** Safety + cycle life + cost; Disadvantage: lower energy density.
- 🔋 **NMC Advantages:** Energy density + balanced performance; Used in Hyundai/Kia/BYD premium EVs.
- 🔋 **Key Safety Risk:** **Thermal runaway** triggered by overcharge, internal short circuit, mechanical abuse, or external fire.
- 🔋 **State of Charge (SOC)** estimation methods: Coulomb counting, OCV lookup, Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), Machine Learning.
- 🔋 **Why Li not Na?** Li has the lowest atomic mass and smallest ionic radius → highest gravimetric and volumetric energy density.
- 🔋 **Why graphite not Li-metal?** Li-metal forms **dendrites** → internal short circuit → fire.
- 🔋 **Faraday's Constant (F = 96,485 C/mol)** is indispensable for any electrochemical calculation — memorize it.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy:**
> 1. Memorize the **discharge half-reactions** and **Nernst equation** verbatim.
> 2. Practice at least **one numerical** on theoretical specific energy derivation.
> 3. Be ready to **draw a labeled sketch** of a Li-ion cell cross-section.
> 4. Always carry a **comparison table** mindset for "compare and contrast" type questions.
> 5. Use **units consistently** (V, Ah, Wh, Wh/kg, °C) — unit errors cost easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT501 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Cell (KTU 2024 Definition):** A fuel cell is an **electrochemical energy conversion device** that continuously converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically hydrogen) and an oxidant (typically oxygen from air) directly into **electrical energy**, with **water and heat** as the primary by-products. Unlike a battery, it requires a continuous external supply of fuel and oxidant to sustain the electrochemical reaction, making it a true **open-system energy transducer**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Automotive Electrical and Electronics** syllabus (Module 1), fuel cells are classified as a *future-mobility power source*, sitting alongside batteries and supercapacitors in the modern EV/HEV drivetrain architecture. The reference standard reaction for the proton-exchange membrane fuel cell (PEMFC) used in automotive contexts is:

$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

The standard thermodynamic potential for this cell reaction is **E° = 1.229 V** at standard conditions (**T = 298 K, P = 1 atm**). The higher heating value (HHV) of hydrogen is **286 kJ/mol** and the lower heating value (LHV) is **241 kJ/mol**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 The "Restaurant Kitchen vs. Tiffin Box" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a **battery** is a sealed **tiffin box** 🍱 — it carries a fixed amount of food (chemical energy). Once eaten, you must recharge it or throw it away. Now think of a **fuel cell** as a **live restaurant kitchen** 🍳 — fuel (hydrogen) and air (oxygen) are *continuously delivered* and *cooked* (reacted electrochemically) to produce electricity on demand. As long as the kitchen has gas supply and ventilation, it never stops cooking. The "chef" in the fuel cell is the **electrolyte membrane** and the "plates" are the **anode and cathode electrodes** where the half-reactions occur.

## 1.3 Why Fuel Cells Matter in Automotive Engineering

- **Zero Tailpipe Emissions:** Only water vapor exits the tailpipe.
- **High Theoretical Efficiency (~83% HHV basis)** vs. ICE (~30–40%).
- **Silent Operation:** No combustion → no piston noise.
- **Refueling Time:** Comparable to gasoline (~3–5 min) vs. hours for battery EV charging.
- **Scalable Power Output:** Stack multiple cells to increase voltage.

## 1.4 Core Physical & Chemical Constants (Syllabus-Highlighted)

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday's Constant | $F$ | **96,485** | C/mol |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Standard Cell Potential (H₂/O₂) | $E^{\circ}$ | **1.229** | V |
| Hydrogen HHV | $\Delta H_{HHV}$ | **286** | kJ/mol |
| Hydrogen LHV | $\Delta H_{LHV}$ | **241** | kJ/mol |
| Theoretical Efficiency (LHV) | $\eta_{th}$ | **83 %** | dimensionless |
| Typical Operating Temp (PEMFC) | $T_{op}$ | **60–100** | °C |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> A fuel cell consists of **three primary functional regions**:
> 1. **Anode (Fuel Electrode)** — Hydrogen gas is fed and oxidized.
> 2. **Electrolyte** — Selectively conducts ions (H⁺ in PEMFC, O²⁻ in SOFC).
> 3. **Cathode (Air Electrode)** — Oxygen is reduced.

### 🔋 Working Steps (PEMFC — Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell)

1. **Hydrogen Supply & Adsorption:** Gaseous H₂ is supplied to the **anode flow channel** through a bipolar plate. The gas diffuses through a porous **gas diffusion layer (GDL)** and adsorbs onto a **platinum catalyst** surface.
2. **Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction (HOR) at Anode:** Each H₂ molecule splits into 2 protons and 2 electrons.
   $$\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$$
3. **Ion Conduction Through Electrolyte:** The H⁺ ions (protons) migrate through the **hydrated polymer membrane** (e.g., Nafion™) toward the cathode. Electrons are **forced through the external circuit** because the membrane is electrically insulating.
4. **Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR) at Cathode:** At the cathode catalyst, O₂ from air combines with arriving protons and electrons.
   $$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
5. **Net Cell Reaction:** Adding the two half-reactions yields the overall reaction with water as the by-product.
   $$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + W_{elec} + Q_{heat}$$
6. **Water & Heat Management:** Product water is expelled (humidified air is also used to keep the membrane hydrated). Heat is removed by a **coolant loop**.

## 2.2 The Gibbs Free Energy & Reversible Voltage Foundation

The reversible (no-load) open-circuit voltage of an H₂/O₂ cell is derived from **Gibbs free energy change**:

$$E_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}$$

Where:
- $\Delta G$ = change in Gibbs free energy (J/mol) = **−237.2 kJ/mol** (at 25°C, liquid water product)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred = **2** for H₂
- $F$ = **96,485 C/mol**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Substituting** $\Delta G = -237{,}200$ J/mol and $n = 2$:
>
> $$E_{rev} = -\frac{(-237{,}200)}{(2)(96{,}485)} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$
>
> This matches the standard electrode potential difference between the **hydrogen electrode (0 V)** and the **oxygen electrode (+1.229 V)** in the electrochemical series.

## 2.3 Voltage Losses (Polarizations) — Why Real Voltage < 1.229 V

Real fuel cells suffer three major irreversibilities:

| Polarization Type | Cause | Approx. Loss (mV) | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Activation Loss ($\eta_{act}$)** | Sluggish ORR kinetics at cathode | 100–400 | Better Pt catalyst, higher T |
| **Ohmic Loss ($\eta_{ohm}$)** | Ionic & electronic resistance | 20–100 | Thinner membrane, better plates |
| **Concentration Loss ($\eta_{conc}$)** | Mass-transport limitations at high current | 50–300 | Optimized GDL, higher pressure |

The actual cell terminal voltage is:
$$V_{cell} = E_{rev} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$$

## 2.4 Stack Voltage & Power Output

A single cell delivers ~**0.6–0.7 V** under load. For automotive power (~200–400 V bus), cells are stacked in series:

$$V_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell}$$

Where $N$ = number of cells. A typical automotive PEMFC stack contains **300–400 cells** to deliver **250–350 V**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorize These for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{rev} = -\dfrac{\Delta G}{nF}$ | Reversible cell voltage from Gibbs energy |
| 2 | $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V | Standard H₂/O₂ cell EMF |
| 3 | $E_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{nF}$ | Thermoneutral voltage (HHV = 1.481 V, LHV = 1.253 V) |
| 4 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \dfrac{1.229}{1.481} = 83\%$ | Theoretical efficiency (HHV basis) |
| 5 | $\eta_{th,LHV} = \dfrac{1.229}{1.253} = 98\%$ | Theoretical efficiency (LHV basis) |
| 6 | $V_{cell} = E_{rev} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$ | Operating cell voltage |
| 7 | $V_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell}$ | Stack voltage (series) |
| 8 | $P_{stack} = V_{stack} \cdot I$ | Stack electrical power |
| 9 | $\eta_{system} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{th}} \times 100\%$ | Voltage efficiency |
| 10 | $W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G$ | Maximum reversible work per mole fuel |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Toyota Mirai (2014):** First mass-produced hydrogen FCEV sedan, 114 kW PEMFC stack, ~500 km range.
- **Hyundai NEXO:** Second-gen FCEV SUV with 135 kW stack and 5-min refueling.
- **Mercedes-Benz GLC F-CELL:** Plug-in FCEV with both battery and fuel cell.
- **Heavy-Duty Trucks:** Cummins/Hydrogenics fuel cell systems for class-8 long-haul trucks.
- **Backup Power:** Bloom Energy SOFC servers for data centers and telecom towers.
- **Space:** NASA used **Alkaline Fuel Cells (AFC)** in Apollo missions (by-product water was even drinkable!).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reversible Voltage from Gibbs Free Energy

**Problem Statement:** Derive the reversible open-circuit voltage of an H₂/O₂ fuel cell operating at standard conditions (T = 298 K, P = 1 atm), producing liquid water as the product.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Write the overall cell reaction.**

$$\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

**Step 2: Identify the change in Gibbs free energy for the reaction.**

From standard thermodynamic tables:
$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol} = -237{,}200 \text{ J/mol}$$

The negative sign indicates the reaction is **spontaneous** and releases useful work.

**Step 3: Relate Gibbs free energy to electrical work.**

The maximum non-expansion work obtainable from a constant-T, constant-P electrochemical reaction equals $-\Delta G$. This work equals the electrical energy delivered by the cell:

$$W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G = n \cdot F \cdot E_{rev}$$

The factor $n \cdot F$ is the total charge transferred per mole of fuel, and $E_{rev}$ is the reversible voltage (electromotive force).

**Step 4: Solve for $E_{rev}$.**

Rearranging:
$$E_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the numerical values.**

For the H₂/O₂ cell: $n = 2$ electrons, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.

$$E_{rev} = -\frac{(-237{,}200)}{(2)(96{,}485)}$$

$$E_{rev} = \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{E_{rev} = 1.229 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 6: Verify with electrode potentials.**

The standard reduction potential of O₂ to H₂O is **+1.229 V**, and the standard potential for H⁺/H₂ is **0 V** (reference). Therefore:
$$E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode} = 1.229 - 0 = 1.229 \text{ V} \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical (Thermoneutral) Voltage from Enthalpy

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the thermoneutral voltage of the H₂/O₂ cell using the higher heating value (HHV) of hydrogen.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the HHV of hydrogen.**

$$\Delta H_{HHV} = -286 \text{ kJ/mol} = -286{,}000 \text{ J/mol}$$

(The negative sign denotes energy released.)

**Step 2: Use the relation between enthalpy and thermoneutral voltage.**

The thermoneutral voltage $E_{th}$ is the voltage that corresponds to converting **all** the chemical energy (not just the Gibbs free energy) into electrical work — i.e., it assumes the heat of reaction is also recovered as work:

$$E_{th} = -\frac{\Delta H}{nF}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values.**

$$E_{th} = -\frac{(-286{,}000)}{(2)(96{,}485)}$$

$$E_{th} = \frac{286{,}000}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{E_{th,HHV} = 1.481 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 4: Repeat for LHV (water as vapor).**

Using $\Delta H_{LHV} = -241$ kJ/mol:
$$E_{th,LHV} = -\frac{(-241{,}000)}{2 \cdot 96{,}485} = \frac{241{,}000}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{E_{th,LHV} = 1.253 \text{ V}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Theoretical Efficiency

**Problem Statement:** Compute the maximum theoretical (reversible) efficiency of an H₂/O₂ fuel cell on HHV and LHV bases.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define efficiency.**

The theoretical efficiency is the ratio of useful electrical work to total chemical energy input:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{elec,max}}{\Delta H} = \frac{-\Delta G}{-\Delta H} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values (HHV basis).**

$$\eta_{th,HHV} = \frac{237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}}{286 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{th,HHV} = 0.829 = 82.9\%}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values (LHV basis).**

$$\eta_{th,LHV} = \frac{237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}}{241 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{th,LHV} = 0.984 = 98.4\%}$$

**Step 4: Physical interpretation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **16.9% gap** between HHV and LHV efficiency is the **latent heat of vaporization** of water. When the product water leaves as vapor (typical in PEMFC at 80°C), that energy is *not* recoverable as work. Hence automotive PEMFC stacks are usually rated on the LHV basis.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Number of Cells for an Automotive Stack

**Problem Statement:** An automotive PEMFC stack must deliver a nominal voltage of **300 V** at a current of **300 A**. If each cell operates at **0.65 V** under load, how many cells are required? What is the stack power?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute the number of cells in series.**

$$N = \frac{V_{stack}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{300}{0.65}$$

$$\boxed{N = 461.5 \Rightarrow 462 \text{ cells}}$$

**Step 2: Compute the actual stack voltage.**

$$V_{stack} = 462 \times 0.65 = 300.3 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Compute the stack power output.**

$$P_{stack} = V_{stack} \cdot I = 300.3 \times 300$$

$$\boxed{P_{stack} = 90{,}090 \text{ W} \approx 90 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 4: Compute the system efficiency.**

$$\eta_{sys} = \frac{V_{cell}}{E_{th,HHV}} = \frac{0.65}{1.481} = 0.439 = 43.9\%$$

This is a typical real-world automotive PEMFC efficiency at rated load (well below the 83% theoretical maximum, mainly due to polarization losses).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Types of Fuel Cells (KTU High-Yield)

| Fuel Cell Type | Electrolyte | Charge Carrier | Operating Temp (°C) | Efficiency (%) | Automotive Suitability | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PEMFC** (Proton Exchange Membrane) | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | H⁺ | **60–100** | 50–60 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Best) | Cars, buses, trucks |
| **AFC** (Alkaline) | KOH solution | OH⁻ | **60–90** | 60–70 | ⭐⭐ (Poor — CO₂ poisoning) | Space (Apollo) |
| **PAFC** (Phosphoric Acid) | Liquid H₃PO₄ | H⁺ | **150–220** | 40–45 | ⭐ (Slow startup) | Stationary power |
| **MCFC** (Molten Carbonate) | Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ | CO₃²⁻ | **600–700** | 50–55 | ⭐ (Too hot) | Large stationary |
| **SOFC** (Solid Oxide) | YSZ (Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia) | O²⁻ | **700–1000** | 55–65 | ⭐ (Too hot, slow start) | Stationary, APU |
| **DMFC** (Direct Methanol) | Polymer membrane | H⁺ | **60–120** | 30–40 | ⭐⭐⭐ (Portable) | Laptops, military |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** The automotive syllabus stresses **PEMFC** as the primary traction power source. AFC and SOFC appear only as *comparative knowledge* for question banks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Fuel Cell Operating Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2TANK[Hydrogen Storage Tank] --> ANODEFLOW[Anode Flow Field]
    AIRSUP[Air Blower and Humidifier] --> CATHODEFLOW[Cathode Flow Field]
    ANODEFLOW --> ANODE[Anode Catalyst Layer HOR]
    CATHODEFLOW --> CATHODE[Cathode Catalyst Layer ORR]
    ANODE --> MEMBRANE[Proton Exchange Membrane Electrolyte]
    MEMBRANE --> CATHODE
    ANODE --> EXTCIRCUIT[External Circuit Electrical Load]
    EXTCIRCUIT --> CATHODE
    CATHODE --> WATEROUT[Product Water and Heat]
    WATEROUT --> COOLLOOP[Coolant Thermal Management Loop]
    ANODEFLOW --> RECIRC[Unreacted Hydrogen Recirculation]
    RECIRC --> H2TANK
    COOLLOOP --> RAD[Radiator Heat Rejection]
    classDef power fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef chem fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1,color:#000
    classDef therm fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    class H2TANK,AIRSUP chem
    class ANODE,CATHODE,MEMBRANE,ANODEFLOW,CATHODEFLOW chem
    class EXTCIRCUIT power
    class WATEROUT,COOLLOOP,RECIRC,RAD therm
```

**Block-by-Block Explanation:**

- **Hydrogen Storage Tank:** High-pressure Type IV composite tank (350 bar or 700 bar) holds compressed H₂.
- **Air Blower / Humidifier:** Delivers filtered, pressurized, and humidified air to the cathode.
- **Anode Flow Field & Cathode Flow Field:** Serpentine or interdigitated channels in bipolar plates distribute gases evenly.
- **Anode Catalyst Layer (HOR):** Pt/C catalyst where H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻.
- **Cathode Catalyst Layer (ORR):** Pt/C catalyst where ½O₂ + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂O.
- **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM):** Hydrated Nafion-like polymer; conducts H⁺ only.
- **External Circuit:** Path for electron flow → delivers DC power to traction inverter → motor.
- **Product Water & Heat:** Removed by cathode exhaust.
- **Coolant Loop:** Maintains stack at ~80°C.
- **Hydrogen Recirculation:** Improves utilization and humidification.

---

## 4.2 Half-Cell Reaction Flow Diagram (PEMFC)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ANO[ANODE SIDE]
        H2GAS[H2 Gas Molecule] --> ADSORB[Adsorbs on Pt Catalyst]
        ADSORB --> SPLIT[Splits into 2 H plus and 2 e minus]
    end
    subgraph MEM[POLYMER MEMBRANE]
        SPLIT --> PROTONS[2 H plus ions migrate through hydrated membrane]
        SPLIT --> ELECTRONS[2 e minus blocked by membrane]
        ELECTRONS --> EXT[Forced through external circuit and load]
    end
    subgraph CAT[CATHODE SIDE]
        EXT --> COMB[Electrons arrive at cathode]
        O2IN[0.5 O2 from air] --> COMB
        PROTONS --> COMB
        COMB --> H2OPROD[Forms 1 H2O molecule plus heat]
        H2OPROD --> EXPEL[Water expelled via cathode exhaust]
    end
    classDef anode fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    classDef cathode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    classDef mem fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#000
    class H2GAS,ADSORB,SPLIT anode
    class PROTONS,ELECTRONS,EXT,MEM mem
    class O2IN,COMB,H2OPROD,EXPEL cathode
```

---

## 4.3 Polarization Curve (Voltage vs Current Density)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual V–I Curve Block Diagram:** Although Mermaid cannot directly plot XY curves, the following block diagram illustrates the **three loss regions** that compose the polarization curve of a fuel cell.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OCV[Open Circuit Voltage 1.229 V] --> ACTREG[Activation Loss Region Steep initial drop]
    ACTREG --> OHMIC[Ohmic Loss Region Linear gradual drop]
    OHMIC --> CONC[Concentration Loss Region Steep drop at high current]
    CONC --> CUTOFF[Limiting Current Density Cell cannot deliver more]
    classDef curve fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#075985,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    class OCV,CUTOFF curve
    class ACTREG,OHMIC,CONC loss
```

**Key Operating Point:** Automotive PEMFC stacks are designed to operate in the **ohmic region** where the curve is roughly linear, balancing power output and efficiency.

---

## 4.4 System-Level FCEV Powertrain Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H2TANKFCEV[High Pressure H2 Tank 700 bar] --> REG[Pressure Regulator and Ejector]
    REG --> STACKFCEV[PEMFC Stack 300 to 400 V DC]
    AIRFCEV[Air Intake and Compressor] --> HUMID[Humidifier] --> STACKFCEV
    STACKFCEV --> BOOST[DC DC Boost Converter]
    BOOST --> INVERTER[Three Phase Traction Inverter]
    INVERTER --> MOTOR[Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor]
    MOTOR --> GEAR[Single Speed Reduction Gearbox]
    GEAR --> WHEELS[Drive Wheels]
    STACKFCEV --> BATT[Li ion Traction Battery Buffer]
    BATT --> INVERTER
    STACKFCEV --> COOLSYS[Coolant Pump and Radiator]
    COOLSYS --> STACKFCEV
    classDef fuel fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
    class H2TANKFCEV,REG,STACKFCEV,AIRFCEV,HUMID fuel
    class BOOST,INVERTER,BATT,COOLSYS elec
    class MOTOR,GEAR,WHEELS mech
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The **DC-DC boost converter** is *essential* because the stack voltage varies widely with load (from ~0.6 V/cell to 1.0 V/cell OCV). The boost converter provides a **regulated 350–400 V bus** to the inverter. The **Li-ion battery** handles transient peak loads (acceleration) and recovers regenerative braking energy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q]

**Q: Define a fuel cell. With a neat sketch, explain the working of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> A **fuel cell** is an electrochemical device that converts the chemical energy of a fuel (H₂) and oxidant (O₂) directly into electrical energy, producing water and heat as by-products. **(1 mark)**
>
> In a **PEMFC**, hydrogen is supplied to the **anode** where it splits into protons and electrons via the **Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction (HOR)**: H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻. The protons migrate through the **polymer electrolyte membrane** (e.g., Nafion) to the cathode, while electrons are forced through the external circuit producing useful DC power. **(1 mark)**
>
> At the **cathode**, oxygen from air combines with protons and electrons via the **Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR)**: ½O₂ + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂O, releasing water and heat. The overall reaction is H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O + Electrical Energy + Heat. **(1 mark)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q]

**Q: List any three advantages of fuel cells over internal combustion engines (ICE) for automotive applications. State the standard EMF of an H₂/O₂ fuel cell.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Three advantages of fuel cells over ICE:** **(2 marks)**
> 1. **Higher efficiency** (40–60% practical) vs. ICE (25–35%) — fuel cell is not Carnot-limited.
> 2. **Zero tailpipe emissions** — only water vapor exits.
> 3. **Silent operation** — no combustion, no moving pistons; ideal for urban vehicles.
>
> **Standard EMF of H₂/O₂ cell:** $E^{\circ} = $ **1.229 V at 25°C and 1 atm.** **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either Option A or Option B in full**.

---

### ❓ OPTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)**. State the half-cell and overall reactions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PEMFC stack consists of **400 cells** connected in series, each operating at **0.70 V** under load and delivering a current of **250 A**. Calculate:
   (i) Stack terminal voltage
   (ii) Stack power output
   (iii) System voltage efficiency (use $E_{th,HHV} = 1.481$ V)
   (iv) Heat generated by the stack (use HHV = 286 kJ/mol) **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION

### Part (a) — Construction and Working of PEMFC **(7 marks)**

**Construction (Block Diagram Description — 3 marks):**

A single PEMFC cell consists of:
- **Anode and cathode current collectors / bipolar plates:** Made of graphite or metal, with machined flow channels for gas distribution.
- **Gas Diffusion Layers (GDL):** Porous carbon paper/cloth on either side of the membrane.
- **Catalyst Layers:** Pt nanoparticles (≈ 0.1–0.5 mg/cm² loading) deposited on carbon support, coated on the membrane (CCM approach).
- **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM):** Typically Nafion™ (perfluorosulfonic acid), 50–200 µm thick, conducts H⁺ when hydrated.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
- **Anode side:** H₂ gas enters the flow channels, diffuses through GDL to the catalyst, and undergoes HOR: H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻.
- **Membrane transport:** H⁺ ions move through the hydrated membrane; electrons are blocked and routed through the external load.
- **Cathode side:** Air (O₂) enters, diffuses through the cathode GDL, and combines with H⁺ and e⁻ at the catalyst: ½O₂ + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂O.
- **Overall reaction:** H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O + W_electrical + Q_heat.
- **By-products:** Water (liquid/vapor) is expelled; heat is removed by a coolant loop maintaining ~80°C.

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem **(7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Number of cells: $N = 400$
