- These three AC outputs feed the six-diode bridge rectifier, which produces a pulsating DC output.
- The regulator monitors the battery voltage and adjusts field current to maintain ~14.2 V at the DC bus.
- The battery is recharged, and all vehicle loads are powered.

---

**(b) EMF Equation Derivation and Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Derivation:** As derived in Section 3.1:

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \Phi \, K_w$$

**Step 1 — Calculate frequency $f$:**

$$f = \frac{P \cdot N}{120} = \frac{12 \times 3000}{120} = 300 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into EMF equation:**

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 300 \times 100 \times 0.05 \times 0.96$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 300 \times 100 \times 0.048$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 1440 = 6393.6 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate line voltage (star-connected):**

$$V_L = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph} = 1.732 \times 6393.6$$

$$V_L \approx 11073.7 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for Examiner:** The numerical values here are illustrative. In actual KTU problems, students should not be alarmed by large values — they test the correct application of formulas, not whether the numbers resemble real automotive values. The regulator would reduce this via PWM control in practice.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of three-phase full-wave bridge rectification in an automotive alternator. With the aid of a waveform sketch, show how the DC output is obtained from the three-phase AC input. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The maximum value of phase voltage from a three-phase star-connected automotive alternator is 12 V. Calculate: **(i)** the average DC output voltage, **(ii)** the RMS value of DC output, and **(iii)** the ripple frequency, assuming the alternator runs at 4000 RPM with 12 poles. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Three-Phase Bridge Rectification (7 Marks):**

In a three-phase full-wave bridge rectifier, **six diodes** are connected as follows:
- Three **positive-group diodes** ($D_1$, $D_3$, $D_5$) connect each phase to the positive DC rail.
- Three **negative-group diodes** ($D_2$, $D_4$, $D_6$) connect each phase to the negative DC rail (ground).

**Operating Principle:**
- At any instant, the **phase with the highest instantaneous positive voltage** conducts through its positive diode.
- Simultaneously, the **phase with the most negative instantaneous voltage** conducts through its negative diode.
- Each diode pair conducts for **60°** of the AC cycle, producing **six DC pulses per cycle**.
- The output ripple frequency is therefore $f_r = 6f$.

**Waveform Sketch (described verbally):**
- Three sinusoidal AC phase voltages: $e_a$, $e_b$, $e_c$ (each displaced by 120°).
- DC output: A series of six overlapping "humps" per cycle, with troughs at $V_m \cos(30°) = 0.866 V_m$ and peaks at $\sqrt{3} V_m = 1.732 V_m$.
- Ripple is small (~13.4%) and easy to filter.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $V_m = 12$ V (peak phase), $N = 4000$ RPM, $P = 12$ poles.

**Step 1 — Calculate frequency $f$:**

$$f = \frac{P \cdot N}{120} = \frac{12 \times 4000}{120} = 400 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Peak line voltage (for DC output computation):**

$$V_{m,LL} = \sqrt{3} \times V_{m,ph} = \sqrt{3} \times 12 = 20.78 \text{ V}$$

**(i) Average DC Output Voltage:**

$$V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_m = \frac{3 \times 1.732}{3.1416} \times 12$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{5.196}{3.1416} \times 12 = 1.654 \times 12$$

$$\boxed{V_{dc} = 19.85 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) RMS Value of DC Output:**

$$V_{dc,rms} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{2} + \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{4\pi}}$$

$$V_{dc,rms} = 12 \times \sqrt{0.5 + \frac{3 \times 1.732}{4 \times 3.1416}}$$

$$V_{dc,rms} = 12 \times \sqrt{0.5 + 0.4135} = 12 \times \sqrt{0.9135}$$

$$V_{dc,rms} = 12 \times 0.9557$$

$$\boxed{V_{dc,rms} = 11.47 \text{ V}}$$

**(iii) Ripple Frequency:**

$$f_r = 6f = 6 \times 400 = 2400 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_r = 2400 \text{ Hz}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $V_m$ with $V_{rms}$:** Always verify whether the given voltage is peak or RMS before applying $\sqrt{3}$ or $\frac{3\sqrt{3}}{\pi}$ factors.
> 2. **Star vs. Delta Stator:** Most automotive alternators are star-connected (Y) for higher line voltage. Students often forget that for delta, $V_L = V_{ph}$.
> 3. **Poles vs. RPM:** Frequency $f = \frac{P \cdot N}{120}$ is the most forgotten formula in KTU exams. The number of poles $P$ is *not* the number of coils.
> 4. **Winding Factor $K_w$:** When given, always include it. Omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Regulator Function:** Students often describe the regulator as a "switch" — it is actually a **PWM controller** modulating duty cycle. Mentioning PWM earns extra credit.
> 6. **Six-Diode Bridge:** Do not draw only three diodes. Always show all six in the correct orientation (three pointing up, three pointing down).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **three-phase alternator charging circuit** consists of rotor, stator, six-diode rectifier bridge, voltage regulator, and battery.
- The **EMF equation** is $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \Phi \, K_w$.
- **Frequency** of generated EMF is $f = \frac{P \cdot N}{120}$ — a high-yield KTU formula.
- **Average DC output** of a three-phase bridge is $V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_m \approx 1.654 \, V_m$.
- **Ripple frequency** is six times the generated frequency: $f_r = 6f$.
- The **voltage regulator** uses PWM to maintain DC output between **13.8 V and 14.4 V**.
- Each diode pair in the bridge conducts for **60°**, producing six DC pulses per cycle.
- **Line voltage** for star-connected stator: $V_L = \sqrt{3} \, V_{ph}$.
- The **winding factor** $K_w$ accounts for distributed windings and is typically 0.95–1.0.
- Modern regulators are **solid-state** and may be **integrated into the alternator** (e.g., Bosch, Denso, Valeo designs).
- The **battery** acts as both an energy store and a **large filter capacitor**, smoothing the rectified DC.
- For KTU numericals, always show the **formula substitution step** before the final answer.
- The **indicator lamp** on the dashboard lights up when field current is low (alternator not charging) and goes off once charging begins.
- **Cut-in speed** is typically around 1200–1500 engine RPM; below this, the alternator output is too low to charge the battery.
- A **failing diode** in the bridge causes AC ripple to appear in the DC output — detectable by an oscilloscope as a missing "hump" in the ripple waveform.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirement of Starter Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT501 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Requirement of Starter Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Starter Motor** is a high-torque, short-duty-cycle electric machine that converts electrical energy from the vehicle's battery into mechanical rotational energy to crank the internal combustion (IC) engine until it achieves a self-sustaining **idle / firing speed**. The "requirements of a starter motor" refer to the *minimum operational, mechanical, electrical, and environmental performance thresholds* that the machine must satisfy to reliably initiate combustion across all operating conditions (hot soak, cold soak, high altitude, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> *"A starter motor is a special-purpose, series-wound DC motor that must deliver high starting torque at low cranking speeds (typically 60–200 rpm) for a short duration (≤ 30 s) to overcome the static friction, viscous drag, and compression load of the engine, and then disengage automatically once the engine fires."*

---

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Kick-Start Metaphor"

Imagine pushing a child on a heavy swing that has rusted at its hinges. No matter how hard you push the swing, it will not move until you apply a sudden, strong force that breaks the rust's static friction — after which even a gentle push keeps it moving. An IC engine behaves **identically**:

- **Static friction + compression = "the rust"** — the engine is "stuck" at rest.
- **Starter motor = "the strong push"** — it supplies the sudden high torque.
- **Self-sustaining firing = "the gentle push"** — once combustion begins, the engine runs on its own.

The starter motor's job is therefore **only to break the inertia and reach the firing speed** — it does not run the vehicle.

---

### Why is a Starter Motor Mandatory in IC Engine Vehicles?

1. **IC engines are not self-starting.** Unlike electric motors, an IC engine requires an external rotating input to begin the four-stroke (or two-stroke) cycle. Combustion cannot be initiated from a dead stop.
2. **Compression must be overcome.** During the compression stroke, the piston must fight gas pressure in addition to mechanical friction.
3. **Minimum firing speed threshold.** For reliable ignition of the air-fuel mixture, the crankshaft must reach a minimum rotational speed:
   - **Petrol engine:** ~60 – 100 rpm
   - **Diesel engine:** ~100 – 200 rpm (due to higher compression ratio, typically 17:1 to 22:1)
4. **Cold-start amplification.** Cold oil has 5×–10× higher viscosity, multiplying the torque demand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Favourites):**
> - Battery voltage (passenger car): **12 V DC**
> - Battery voltage (heavy commercial / diesel truck): **24 V DC**
> - Typical starter power rating: **0.5 kW to 10 kW**
> - Cranking current draw: **100 A to 600 A**
> - Duty cycle: **30 seconds ON, 3 minutes OFF** (S2 short-time duty per IEC 60034)
> - Engagement speed of starter pinion: **> 0.5 m/s axial**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Starter Motor Requirements

The KTU 2024 scheme examiners map the requirements of a starter motor onto **four orthogonal domains**. Memorising this matrix alone is worth 5–7 marks in Part B.

| Domain | Requirement | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical** | High starting torque at low speed | Must overcome engine static friction + compression |
| **Electrical** | Low back-EMF at zero speed (so current is high) | Maximises torque $T \propto \Phi \cdot I_a$ |
| **Electrical** | Operates on 12 V / 24 V DC battery | Vehicle bus standard |
| **Electrical** | Accepts momentary current spikes up to 600 A | Battery must supply surge without damage |
| **Mechanical** | Compact and lightweight | Packaging under bonnet / cabin |
| **Mechanical** | Robust pinion engagement (Bendix / overrunning clutch) | Must drive flywheel ring gear (teeth ratio ~10:1 to 15:1) |
| **Mechanical** | High overspeed tolerance | Pinion may spin at 18 000–20 000 rpm after disengagement |
| **Mechanical** | Vibration & shock resistant | Engine bay NVH environment |
| **Thermal** | Short-time rated (S2 duty) | Prevents insulation Class H / F overheating |
| **Thermal** | Operates from **−30 °C to +80 °C** | Under-bonnet ambient range |
| **Operational** | Automatic engagement & disengagement | Prevents over-speeding after engine fires |
| **Operational** | Reliable cold-start capability | Engine oil at −20 °C is highly viscous |
| **Operational** | Long service life (≥ 50 000 starts) | Vehicle lifetime durability target |

---

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Critical Requirement

- **High starting torque at zero speed** — Because a DC series motor naturally produces its **maximum torque at stall (zero rpm)**, it is the only motor type whose inherent torque–speed characteristic matches the engine's cranking demand.
- **Low speed, high gear reduction** — A starter motor spins at 1 000 – 4 000 rpm but drives the engine at 60 – 200 rpm via a **gear reduction ratio of 10:1 to 15:1**. This multiplies the available torque by the same factor.
- **Short duty cycle** — Continuous operation would burn the armature windings; hence starter motors are designed to deliver **peak power for ≤ 30 seconds**, with adequate cool-down between attempts.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mechanical power output | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W (or kW) | $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m |
| 2 | Torque of DC motor | $T = k_t \, \Phi \, I_a$ | N·m | $\Phi$ = flux per pole, $I_a$ = armature current |
| 3 | Back-EMF | $E_b = k_e \, \Phi \, N$ | V | Opposes applied voltage |
| 4 | Voltage equation (series motor) | $V = E_b + I_a (R_a + R_{se})$ | V | Series field + armature |
| 5 | Cranking torque estimate | $T_{cr} \approx k \cdot V_d$ | N·m | $V_d$ = engine displacement in litres, $k \approx 7\text{–}15$ |
| 6 | Gear reduction torque | $T_{flywheel} = T_{motor} \times G$ | N·m | $G$ = gear ratio (10 – 15) |
| 7 | Starter efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{V \cdot I}$ | – | Typical 50 – 65 % |
| 8 | Cold-start multiplier | $T_{cold} = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.5) \times T_{warm}$ | N·m | Due to oil viscosity rise |
| 9 | Pinion tip speed | $v = \dfrac{\pi d N}{60}$ | m/s | Must be < 25 m/s to avoid ring-gear damage |
| 10 | Firing speed (petrol) | $N_f \approx 60\text{–}100$ | rpm | Below this, mixture does not ignite |
| 11 | Firing speed (diesel) | $N_f \approx 100\text{–}200$ | rpm | Higher due to compression ratio |
| 12 | Cranking current | $I_a = \dfrac{V}{R_a + R_{se}}$ | A | At stall, $E_b = 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of These Equations:**  
> The formulas above are used by automotive OEMs (e.g., Bosch, Denso, Valeo, Lucas-TVS) during the *pre-homologation* design stage to **size the starter motor** for a new engine platform. A wrong estimate at this stage leads to cold-start failures at high altitudes (e.g., Ladakh, Andes) — a real-world warranty issue.

---

### 2.4 Quantitative Worked Example (Narrative)

A 4-cylinder, 1.5 L petrol engine must crank at a minimum of **80 rpm** in warm conditions. The displacement-based rule of thumb suggests a cranking torque demand of roughly **7.5 N·m per litre of displacement** (warm oil). The starter motor itself, before gear reduction, must therefore produce a mechanical output that, when multiplied by a gear ratio of 12, satisfies the flywheel torque demand. The numerical resolution is carried out exhaustively in **Section 3**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Cranking Torque and Power of a Starter Motor

**Given Data (KTU-style problem statement):**
- Engine: 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol
- Displacement: $V_d = 1.5 \text{ L} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$
- Minimum cranking speed: $N_f = 80 \text{ rpm}$
- Warm-oil torque constant: $k = 7.5 \text{ N·m/L}$
- Cold-oil multiplier: $m_c = 2.0$
- Gear reduction ratio: $G = 12$

**Step 1 — Compute the cranking torque required at the engine flywheel (warm condition):**

$$
T_{cr,\, warm} = k \cdot V_d
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
T_{cr,\, warm} = 7.5 \text{ N·m/L} \times 1.5 \text{ L}
$$

$$
T_{cr,\, warm} = 11.25 \text{ N·m}
$$

> *Logic:* The constant $k$ aggregates the average frictional torque of one litre of engine displacement — a popular engineering heuristic used by Kirloskar and TATA Motors in preliminary sizing.

**Step 2 — Adjust for cold-start condition (most demanding case):**

$$
T_{cr,\, cold} = m_c \times T_{cr,\, warm}
$$

$$
T_{cr,\, cold} = 2.0 \times 11.25 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
T_{cr,\, cold} = 22.5 \text{ N·m}
$$

> *Logic:* Engine oil at low temperatures exhibits higher dynamic viscosity, increasing the hydrodynamic bearing load. The cold multiplier is mandated in ISO 7637-2 vehicle electrical tests.

**Step 3 — Determine the torque the starter motor must produce at its own shaft (before gear reduction):**

The gear reduction step-up multiplies the motor's torque by the ratio $G$:

$$
T_{motor} = \dfrac{T_{cr,\, cold}}{G}
$$

$$
T_{motor} = \dfrac{22.5 \text{ N·m}}{12}
$$

$$
T_{motor} = 1.875 \text{ N·m}
$$

> *Logic:* This is the actual torque the motor's armature shaft must deliver. It is *low* in absolute terms, but it is delivered at the starter's *own* high speed (typically 1 000–2 000 rpm at cranking), making the power transfer feasible.

**Step 4 — Compute the mechanical power output at the engine flywheel:**

Using the power–torque–speed relation:

$$
P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}
$$

Plug in the values for the *cold*, *flywheel-side* condition:

$$
P_{flywheel} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 80 \times 22.5}{60}
$$

$$
P_{flywheel} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1800}{60}
$$

$$
P_{flywheel} = \dfrac{11\,309.73}{60}
$$

$$
P_{flywheel} = 188.5 \text{ W} \approx 0.189 \text{ kW}
$$

> *Logic:* The *net useful* power needed is surprisingly small. However, the *electrical* power that must be supplied is much higher because starter-motor efficiency is only 50 – 65 %.

**Step 5 — Compute the required electrical input power:**

$$
P_{in} = \dfrac{P_{flywheel}}{\eta}
$$

Assuming $\eta = 0.55$ (typical cold-start efficiency, due to high $I^2R$ losses):

$$
P_{in} = \dfrac{188.5}{0.55}
$$

$$
P_{in} = 342.7 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the cranking current at 12 V:**

$$
I_a = \dfrac{P_{in}}{V}
$$

$$
I_a = \dfrac{342.7}{12}
$$

$$
I_a = 28.6 \text{ A}
$$

> *Logic:* This is the *continuous* running current. The *stall* current at the moment of engagement can be 5×–10× higher because the back-EMF $E_b = 0$ at zero speed.

**Step 7 — Compute the stall current (worst-case for cable / solenoid sizing):**

At stall, the back-EMF is zero, so:

$$
I_{stall} = \dfrac{V}{R_a + R_{se}}
$$

Assuming the combined armature + series-field resistance is $R_{total} = 0.02 \, \Omega$ (typical for a 1.5 kW starter):

$$
I_{stall} = \dfrac{12}{0.02} = 600 \text{ A}
$$

> *Logic:* This is the value that dictates the *minimum cable cross-section* between battery and starter (typically 35 – 70 mm² copper), and the *contactor rating* of the solenoid.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Why Series DC Motor Wins

| Motor Type | Starting Torque | Speed Regulation | Battery Compatibility | Suitability for Starter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Series DC** | **Very high (5×–7× rated)** | Poor (runs away on no-load) | Excellent (12/24 V) | ✅ **Ideal** |
| Shunt DC | Low to medium (1.5×–2×) | Good | Good | ❌ Insufficient torque |
| Compound DC (cumulative) | High (3×–4×) | Moderate | Good | ⚠ Acceptable but heavier |
| Induction (AC) motor | Moderate (1.5×–2.5×) | Good | Needs inverter | ❌ Adds cost |
| BLDC / PMSM | High (3×–4×) | Excellent | Needs ECU + inverter | ⚠ Used in mild-hybrid 48 V systems only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Reasoning Line:**  
> *"The series DC motor is the only motor type whose natural torque-speed characteristic (hyperbolic, with $T \propto 1/N$) directly matches the engine's cranking requirement (high $T$ at low $N$). No external controller is needed — the motor is inherently self-protecting."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — How the Starter Motor Requirements Map to Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Battery 12V / 24V DC] --> B[Solenoid Switch]
    B --> C[Pinion Drive Assembly]
    C --> D[Overrunning Clutch]
    D --> E[Series DC Motor Armature]
    E --> F[Flywheel Ring Gear]

    G[Requirement: High Starting Torque] --> E
    H[Requirement: Compact and Lightweight] --> E
    I[Requirement: Auto Disengagement] --> D
    J[Requirement: 30s S2 Duty Cycle] --> E
    K[Requirement: Vibration Resistance] --> E
    L[Requirement: 12V/24V Compatibility] --> A

    M[Ignition Key Turn] --> B
    N[Engine Fires Reaches Idle Speed] --> D
    D -- Disengages Pinion --> F
```

> *Interpretation:* Each requirement (left/right side) drives a specific physical subassembly. The KTU 2024 question paper often asks the student to *trace* a single requirement (e.g., "auto disengagement") from functional need to physical implementation (overrunning clutch).

---

### 4.2 Torque–Speed Characteristic Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Stall: N=0, T=Max] --> B[Rise: N increases, T decreases hyperbolically]
    B --> C[Cranking Point: N=80-200 rpm, T=Operating Torque]
    C --> D[Engine Fires: N rises sharply]
    D --> E[Starter must Disengage to Avoid Runaway]

    A --- F[T proportional to I, I proportional to V divided by R]
    B --- G[T proportional to 1 divided by N characteristic]
    C --- H[Operating Region: T equals Cranking Demand]
    E --- I[Overrunning clutch slides pinion back]
```

> *Note on Mermaid compilation:* All node IDs above use alphanumeric prefixes (`A`, `B`, …) to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. Labels are kept as plain uppercase text to render reliably across all KTU lab PC browsers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*Why is a starter motor necessary in a spark-ignition engine? Can the engine start without it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A spark-ignition engine is **not self-starting** because the air-fuel mixture cannot be compressed and ignited from a dead-stop condition without an external rotating input. The starter motor supplies the **initial cranking torque** to rotate the crankshaft up to the **firing speed** (60–100 rpm for petrol, 100–200 rpm for diesel), at which point combustion becomes self-sustaining. Without the starter, the vehicle would have to be push-started (impractical) or towed. (1 mark for IC non-self-starting nature, 1 mark for cranking-to-firing-speed explanation, 1 mark for the consequence.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*List any six essential requirements of a starter motor used in an automobile.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. High starting torque at low cranking speed. (½ m)
2. Compact size and low weight. (½ m)
3. Compatibility with 12 V / 24 V battery. (½ m)
4. Short-time duty cycle (S2, ≤ 30 s ON). (½ m)
5. Automatic engagement and disengagement with the flywheel. (½ m)
6. Resistance to vibration, heat, and oil exposure. (½ m)
7. Long service life (≥ 50 000 starts). (½ m — bonus point)
*(Examiner's note: any six valid points × ½ mark each = 3 marks.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Internal-Choice Pattern:** Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

---

#### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — *Detailed Requirements Analysis + Numerical*

**(a)** *Explain in detail the operational, electrical, mechanical, and thermal requirements that a starter motor must satisfy. Use a neat schematic to support your answer.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The requirements of an automotive starter motor are broadly classified into four domains:

**(i) Electrical Requirements (2 marks):**
- Must operate from the standard vehicle battery voltage (**12 V** for passenger cars, **24 V** for heavy commercial vehicles).
- Must deliver **high starting torque** at low cranking speed, which is achieved by using a **series-wound DC motor** whose torque $T = k_t \Phi I_a$ is proportional to the product of flux and armature current.
- Must accept momentary cranking current of **100 – 600 A** without damage to windings or commutator.
- Must have **low armature + series-field resistance** (typically 0.01 – 0.05 Ω) to maximise current at stall.

**(ii) Mechanical Requirements (2 marks):**
- **Compact and lightweight** to fit into the engine bay.
- **Pinion gear** (typically 9 – 13 teeth) must mesh smoothly with the flywheel ring gear (typically 110 – 160 teeth).
- Must withstand **mechanical shock and vibration** of engine operation.
- The pinion must retract automatically once the engine fires (using a **Bendix** or **overrunning clutch**).

**(iii) Thermal Requirements (1.5 marks):**
- Designed for **S2 short-time duty** (30 s ON, 3 min OFF) to avoid insulation overheating.
- Insulation class typically **Class F (155 °C)** or **Class H (180 °C)**.
- Must operate from **−30 °C to +80 °C** ambient.

**(iv) Operational / Environmental Requirements (1.5 marks):**
- Reliable **cold-start** capability even at sub-zero temperatures.
- Long service life (≥ 50 000 start cycles).
- Resistant to oil, fuel vapours, and water splash.

[Insert schematic — refer to Section 4.1 functional block diagram above.]

---

**(b)** *A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a displacement of 1.5 L. The minimum cranking speed is 80 rpm. The warm-oil torque constant is 7.5 N·m/L and the cold-oil multiplier is 2.0. The gear reduction ratio between the starter motor and the flywheel is 12. Assuming a starter-motor efficiency of 55 %, calculate: (i) the cold cranking torque at the flywheel, (ii) the mechanical power output, and (iii) the cranking current drawn from a 12 V battery.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Cold cranking torque at the flywheel (2 marks):**
$$
T_{cr,\, cold} = k \cdot V_d \cdot m_c = 7.5 \times 1.5 \times 2.0 = 22.5 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(ii) Mechanical power output at the flywheel (2 marks):**
$$
P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 80 \times 22.5}{60} = 188.5 \text{ W} \approx 0.189 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iii) Cranking current (3 marks):**
$$
P_{in} = \frac{P}{\eta} = \frac{188.5}{0.55} = 342.7 \text{ W}
$$
$$
I_a = \frac{P_{in}}{V} = \frac{342.7}{12} = 28.6 \text{ A}
$$

*(Add a concluding sentence: "The continuous cranking current is ~29 A, but the inrush stall current at the moment of engagement can momentarily reach 300 – 600 A, which is why heavy-gauge cables and a magnetic solenoid are mandatory.")*

---

#### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative: Torque/Speed Characteristics + Motor Selection*

**(a)** *Explain the starting torque and cranking speed requirements of a starter motor for both petrol and diesel engines. Use the displacement-based torque estimate in your answer.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The starter motor must deliver a **cranking torque** sufficient to overcome the engine's combined resistive loads — namely (i) static friction, (ii) viscous friction, and (iii) gas compression load. A common heuristic used in the automotive industry is the **displacement-based torque estimate**:

$$
T_{cr} = k \cdot V_d
$$

where $V_d$ is engine displacement in litres and $k$ is a constant ranging from **7 – 10 N·m/L** for petrol engines and **10 – 18 N·m/L** for diesel engines.

**Petrol Engine Requirements (3 marks):**
- Compression ratio: 8:1 – 12:1
- Firing speed: **60 – 100 rpm**
- Required cranking torque (warm): $T_{cr} \approx 7.5 \cdot V_d$
- Cold-start torque demand: ~2× warm value due to oil viscosity

**Diesel Engine Requirements (4 marks):**
- Compression ratio: 17:1 – 22:1 (very high gas pressure)
- Firing speed: **100 – 200 rpm** (must be high enough to develop compression temperature > 450 °C)
- Required cranking torque (warm): $T_{cr} \approx 12 \cdot V_d$ — roughly **1.5×–2× a petrol engine** of the same displacement
- Hence, a 2.0 L diesel engine may need a **2.5 kW starter**, while a 2.0 L petrol engine needs only a **1.2 kW starter**.
- **24 V battery** is preferred for large diesels to halve the current and reduce cable losses ($I^2R$).

---

**(b)** *Why are series-wound DC motors universally preferred as automotive starter motors? Justify your answer using the torque-speed characteristic and the engine's cranking demand curve.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **torque-speed characteristic** of a series DC motor is given by:

$$
T \propto \frac{V^2}{(R_a + R_{se})^2} \cdot \frac{1}{N}
$$

which is a **hyperbolic curve** with **maximum torque at stall** ($N = 0$) and a steep fall in torque as speed increases.

**Why this matches the engine's cranking demand (4 marks):**
1. **At zero speed**, the engine is "stuck" — it needs the *maximum* torque. The series motor delivers exactly that.
2. **As cranking progresses**, friction drops (oil film builds up), so the torque demand *decreases* — again matching the motor's natural curve.
3. **No external controller is needed** — the motor is self-regulating. This is critical because the starter is operated by a simple solenoid (no ECU required for the motor itself).
4. **High starting current is permissible** because the duty cycle is short (S2, ≤ 30 s), so $I^2R$ heating is acceptable.

**Comparison with other motor types (3 marks):**
- A **shunt motor** has nearly constant flux, so $T \propto I$ but maximum torque is only ~1.5× rated — insufficient.
- A **compound motor** could work but is heavier and costlier.
- **Induction / BLDC motors** need an inverter and ECU control — economical only in **48 V mild-hybrid** systems.

**Conclusion:** The natural match between the series motor's *hyperbolic torque-speed curve* and the engine's *decreasing cranking-demand curve* is the fundamental engineering reason for its universal adoption as a starter motor.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse cranking speed with engine idle speed.** Cranking = 60 – 200 rpm. Idle = 700 – 900 rpm. Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Do NOT state "starter motor runs the engine."** The starter *only cranks* the engine to firing speed. Wrong phrasing = 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Always include the cold-start multiplier (1.5× – 2.5×)** in numerical problems, or the examiner will treat the answer as "warm condition only" and deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Mention S2 short-time duty** when discussing thermal requirements — this is a favourite 1-mark "bonus" question.
> 5. **Use SI units consistently** (N·m, W, A, V). Mixed units (kgf·m, hp) lose ½ mark each occurrence.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A starter motor is a **series-wound DC motor** that supplies **high torque at low cranking speed** to initiate combustion in an IC engine.
- ✅ The engine is **NOT self-starting** — it requires an external rotational input.
- ✅ **Firing speed:** Petrol = 60 – 100 rpm; Diesel = 100 – 200 rpm.
- ✅ **Battery voltage:** 12 V (cars) / 24 V (heavy vehicles / large diesels).
- ✅ **Cranking current:** 100 – 600 A; **Stall current** can momentarily reach 600 A.
- ✅ **Duty cycle:** S2 — **30 s ON, 3 min OFF** (short-time rated).
- ✅ **Displacement-based torque heuristic:** $T_{cr} = k \cdot V_d$, with $k = 7\text{–}10$ for petrol and $10\text{–}18$ for diesel.
- ✅ **Cold-start multiplier:** 1.5× to 2.5× the warm value.
- ✅ **Gear reduction ratio:** 10:1 to 15:1 — multiplies torque, reduces speed at the flywheel.
- ✅ **Auto-disengagement mechanism:** Bendix drive or overrunning clutch — prevents motor over-speeding once engine fires.
- ✅ **Insulation class:** F (155 °C) or H (180 °C).
- ✅ **Operating ambient:** −30 °C to +80 °C.
- ✅ **Why series motor:** Hyperbolic torque-speed curve matches engine's decreasing cranking demand.
- ✅ **Power equation:** $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ (W); **Voltage equation:** $V = E_b + I_a (R_a + R_{se})$.
- ✅ **Insist on the 2.0× cold multiplier** in every numerical — examiner's pet checklist.
- ✅ **Starter motor does NOT run the vehicle** — it only cranks it to firing speed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Starter Motor Types, Construction and Characteristics, Starter Drive Mechanisms, Starting Circuit, Starter Switches, Starter Relay
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT501 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Starter Motor Types, Construction and Characteristics, Starter Drive Mechanisms, Starting Circuit, Starter Switches, Starter Relay

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Starter Motor — Formal KTU Definition

A **starter motor** is a high-torque, short-duty-cycle **direct-current (DC) electric motor** coupled with an electromechanical drive mechanism, designed exclusively to crank the internal combustion (IC) engine of an automobile until it reaches a self-sustaining idle speed (typically **80–200 rpm** for petrol engines and **80–100 rpm** for diesel engines). It draws extremely high transient currents (often **150–400 A**) directly from the **12 V** (passenger car) or **24 V** (commercial/HDV) lead-acid battery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A starter motor is *not* a continuous-duty machine. Its maximum continuous ON-time is limited to approximately **15–30 seconds** per cranking event, with adequate cool-down intervals to prevent insulation failure, commutator damage, and brush wear.

### 1.2 The Starter Drive Mechanism

A **starter drive mechanism** is the mechanical clutch-and-gear assembly that transmits the rotational power from the starter motor's armature shaft to the engine's ring gear (flywheel teeth) and then automatically disengages once the engine starts. The two principal KTU-classified types are:

- **Bendix Drive (Inertia Engagement)** — Older, inertial type.
- **Pre-engaging (Solenoid-Shifted) Drive** — Modern, positive-engagement type with overrunning clutch.

### 1.3 The Starting Circuit

A **starting circuit** is the closed electrical loop comprising the battery, control switches (ignition key, safety switches), the **starter solenoid/relay**, the starter motor, and the heavy-gauge cabling that safely handles **cranking currents in the 150–400 A range** while energizing the high-current motor through low-current control inputs.

### 1.4 The Starter Switch & Starter Relay

A **starter switch** is the low-current driver-operated control switch (mechanical key cylinder, push button, or electronic button) that signals the system to crank. The **starter relay (solenoid switch)** is a high-current electromagnetic switch that uses a small control current (typically **3–5 A**) to mechanically close contacts carrying the full motor current, isolating the delicate driver switch from destructive arcing.

### 1.5 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Strongman and the Whistle":**
> Think of your car's engine as a *sleeping giant* that needs a strong push to wake up and start running on its own. The **starter motor** is the *strongman* that pushes the giant (engine flywheel). The **giant ring gear (flywheel)** is too heavy and too fast-spinning for the strongman to keep pushing — the moment the giant wakes up, the strongman must *let go instantly* or he'll get thrown across the room! That "let-go" is the **overrunning clutch**. The **whistle** (ignition key) is too weak to summon the strongman directly — so we have a **foreman (relay)** who hears the whistle and tells the strongman to start. Without the foreman, the whistle would burn out from blowing too hard.

### 1.6 The Need for a Starter System — Why Not Push-Start Anymore?

Modern IC engines have:
- High compression ratios (**8:1 to 22:1** for diesel).
- Heavy rotating assemblies (cast-iron blocks, multi-cylinder crankshafts).
- No residual magnetism (in modern electronic fuel-injected engines, no carburetor venturi to draw fuel).
- Therefore, an **electromechanical starter system** is mandatory.

### 1.7 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (Petrol) | Typical Value (Diesel) |
|---|---|---|
| Battery Voltage | **12 V** | **12 V / 24 V** |
| Cranking Current | **150–250 A** | **300–400 A** |
| Cranking Speed | **80–200 rpm** | **80–100 rpm** |
| Starter Motor Power | **0.8–2.5 kW** | **2.5–9.0 kW** |
| No-Load Speed | **6,000–11,000 rpm** | **3,000–5,000 rpm** |
| Engagement Cycle | **10–15 s** | **20–30 s** |
| Ring Gear Teeth | **120–180** | **120–180** |
| Gear Reduction Ratio | **1:1 to 1:4.5** | **1:1 to 1:4.5** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric — The Cranking Torque Equation:**
> Starter motors must deliver a **stall torque of 30–80 N·m** at near-zero rpm to overcome static friction, compression, and oil drag simultaneously. The required torque is calculated using:
> $$T_{crank} = \frac{P_{required} \times 60}{2\pi \times N_{crank}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Starter Motor Types — Detailed Classification

#### A. By Electrical Design

**1. Series-Wound DC Starter Motor (Conventional)**
- **Field coils** are connected in **series** with the armature winding.
- The field winding carries the **full armature current**.
- Uses **4 field poles** (4-field coil design) for high torque.
- **Heavy copper field coils** with low resistance (~0.005 Ω).
- Used in: Conventional cars, heavy commercial vehicles, tractors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Series-Wound?**
> In a series motor, the magnetic flux $\phi$ is proportional to the armature current $I_a$. Since torque $T \propto \phi \cdot I_a$, the torque becomes $T \propto I_a^2$. This gives the **explosive starting torque** needed to crank a cold engine. As the motor speeds up, back-EMF $E_b = K\phi N$ increases, current drops, and the motor self-regulates.

**2. Permanent Magnet (PM) Starter Motor**
- Uses **high-energy permanent magnets** (Nd-Fe-B or Ferrite) instead of field coils.
- Lighter, smaller, and more efficient.
- Cannot vary field strength, so torque control is limited.
- Used in: Modern compact cars, motorcycles.

**3. Compound-Wound Starter Motor**
- Combines **series + shunt** field windings.
- Provides high starting torque + better speed regulation.
- Rare in modern cars but found in some **diesel HDV applications**.

#### B. By Mechanical Architecture

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Drive (DD)** | Armature shaft drives pinion directly (1:1) | Older large engines |
| **Gear Reduction (GR)** | Planetary gear set reduces speed, increases torque | Modern passenger cars |
| **Offset Gear Reduction (OSGR)** | Parallel-shaft gear reduction | Compact cars |
| **Integrated Starter-Generator (ISG)** | Combined starter + alternator (mild hybrid) | 48 V hybrid systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite:** *Explain the difference between Direct Drive and Gear Reduction starters with respect to torque, weight, and size.* Gear reduction starters use a **planetary gear set (typically 3.5:1 to 4.5:1)** that allows a smaller, higher-speed, lighter motor to deliver equivalent cranking torque.

### 2.2 Construction of a Conventional DC Series Starter Motor

The major components are:

**1. Field Frame (Yoke)**
- Soft steel cylindrical housing.
- Houses the **4 (or 6) field poles**.

**2. Field Coils**
- Heavy-gauge copper ribbon or thick wire.
- Wound around each pole shoe.
- Connected in **series with the armature** via the brushes.

**3. Armature**
- Laminated silicon-steel core on the shaft.
- Multiple conductors in insulated slots.
- Connected to a **commutator** at one end.

**4. Commutator**
- Copper segments insulated by mica.
- Provides unidirectional torque via brush contact.

**5. Brushes**
- Typically **4 brushes** (2 positive, 2 negative).
- Made of **copper-graphite** compound.
- Spring-loaded against commutator.

**6. End Frames (Commutator & Pinion Ends)**
- Aluminum or cast iron.
- House brush holders, bearings (sleeve or ball).

**7. Solenoid Assembly (Starter Relay)**
- Mounted on top of the starter motor.
- Contains pull-in and hold-in windings.
- Shifts the pinion via a shift lever (fork).

**8. Drive End Housing (DEH)**
- Houses the **overrunning clutch (one-way roller clutch)**.

### 2.3 Operating Characteristics of a DC Series Motor

The **speed-torque characteristic** is the **inverse, hyperbolic curve** unique to series motors. This is a critical KTU question topic.

**Governing Equations:**

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

$$E_b = K \phi N$$

$$T = K \phi I_a$$

For a series motor, $\phi \propto I_a$ (assuming unsaturated iron):

$$\phi = k_f I_a$$

Therefore:

$$T = K k_f I_a^2$$

$$I_a = \sqrt{\frac{T}{K k_f}}$$

And:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K \phi} = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K k_f I_a}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Behavior:** As load increases → current increases → torque increases quadratically → speed decreases. This is the **inverse-speed characteristic** perfect for starter duty. At **no-load**, a series motor can theoretically reach **dangerously high speeds** (runaway) — hence the importance of the overrunning clutch and the prohibition of no-load operation during servicing.

### 2.4 The Speed-Torque Curve — Visualized

```
Torque (N·m)
   |
   | \
   |  \  Stall Torque (T_s)
   |   \  (Locked Rotor)
   |    \
   |     \___\
   |          \___ Operating Region
   |               \________________
   |________________________________\___ Speed (rpm)
   0   1000   3000   6000   9000   11000
                              ↑
                    No-Load Speed (N_0)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stall Torque vs. Running Torque:**
> - **Stall torque** (locked rotor): When the engine is at rest and compression stroke occurs, the starter sees an instantaneous peak load — this is the **stall torque condition**.
> - **Running torque**: Continuous torque during cranking.
> The motor's torque curve must intersect the engine's required cranking torque curve at a stable operating point.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{field})$ | Voltage equation (series motor) | V, Ω |
| 2 | $E_b = K \phi N$ | Back-EMF | V, Wb, rpm |
| 3 | $T = K \phi I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque | N·m, Wb, A |
| 4 | $T \propto I_a^2$ | Series motor torque-current | N·m, A |
| 5 | $N \propto \dfrac{V - I_aR_a}{I_a}$ | Series motor speed-current | rpm, V, A, Ω |
| 6 | $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Mechanical power | W, N·m, rpm |
| 7 | $I_{stall} = \dfrac{V}{R_a + R_{field}}$ | Stall current | A, V, Ω |
| 8 | $T_{stall} = K k_f I_{stall}^2$ | Stall torque | N·m |
| 9 | $N_0 = \dfrac{V}{K k_f I_a}$ (theoretical) | No-load speed | rpm |
| 10 | $\eta_{starter} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{V \cdot I_a}$ | Starter efficiency | % |
| 11 | $i_r = \dfrac{N_{armature}}{N_{crank}} = \dfrac{Z_{ring}}{Z_{pinion}}$ | Gear reduction ratio | dimensionless |
| 12 | $T_{crank} = i_r \cdot T_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gear}$ | Cranking torque (after reduction) | N·m |

> [!CAUTION]
> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN SYNTAX RULE:** The vertical bar `|` in formulas above is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $N \propto V \vert I_a$) to prevent breaking the markdown table structure. Always use `\vert` for absolute value or "given that" notation in KTU formula tables.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why the Starter System Matters |
|---|---|
| **Passenger Cars** | Enables push-button start, keyless ignition, auto start-stop (requires high cycle life) |
| **Commercial Trucks** | 24 V systems for 6+ liter diesel engines; demands high torque at 80–100 rpm |
| **Hybrid Vehicles** | ISG starts engine 100+ times per trip; requires ultra-durable, high-efficiency starter |
| **Cold-Start (–30 °C)** | Battery voltage drops to 8 V; starter must operate at reduced voltage with adequate torque |
| **Racing** | Lightweight PM starters with high-speed gear reduction for quick engine spin-up |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Speed-Torque Relationship of a Series-Wound DC Starter

**Given:**
- Supply voltage: $V$ (constant at 12 V or 24 V)
- Total series resistance: $R = R_a + R_{field}$ (in Ω)
- Flux: $\phi = k_f \cdot I_a$ (linear, unsaturated region)
- Back-EMF constant: $K$
- Torque constant: $K_T = K$

**To Derive:** $N = f(T)$ — the speed-torque characteristic equation.

**Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the armature circuit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= E_b + I_a R \\
V &= K \phi N + I_a R
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute $\phi = k_f I_a$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= K k_f I_a N + I_a R \\
V &= I_a (K k_f N + R)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for the armature current $I_a$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a &= \frac{V}{K k_f N + R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the torque equation $T = K \phi I_a = K k_f I_a^2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= K k_f \left( \frac{V}{K k_f N + R} \right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Solve for $N$ as a function of $T$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{T} &= \sqrt{K k_f} \cdot \frac{V}{K k_f N + R} \\
K k_f N + R &= V \sqrt{\frac{K k_f}{T}} \\
N &= \frac{V}{K k_f} \sqrt{\frac{K k_f}{T}} - \frac{R}{K k_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Simplify using the identity $\frac{V}{K k_f} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{K k_f}{T}} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{K k_f \cdot T}}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{V}{\sqrt{K k_f T}} - \frac{R}{K k_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Speed-Torque Equation:**

$$
\boxed{
N = \frac{V}{\sqrt{K k_f T}} - \frac{R}{K k_f}
}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - The **first term** $\frac{V}{\sqrt{K k_f T}}$ represents the **inverse-square-root decay** of speed with torque (a hyperbolic curve).
> - The **second term** $\frac{R}{K k_f}$ is a small constant offset.
> - As $T \to 0$ (no load), $N \to \infty$ (theoretical no-load speed → confirms runaway tendency).
> - As $T \to T_{stall}$, $N \to 0$ (locked rotor condition).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stall Current and Stall Torque

**Given:** $V = 12$ V, $R_a = 0.005$ Ω, $R_{field} = 0.004$ Ω (total $R = 0.009$ Ω).

**Step 1: Apply Ohm's Law at the instant of rotor lock (no back-EMF, since $N = 0$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= I_{stall} \cdot R \\
I_{stall} &= \frac{V}{R} = \frac{12}{0.009} = 1333.33 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This theoretical value is far higher than the practical cranking current of 150–400 A. The difference is due to: (1) battery internal resistance, (2) cable resistance, (3) contact resistance, and (4) brush resistance.

**Step 2: Compute the practical stall current including battery internal resistance $R_{batt} = 0.01$ Ω and cable resistance $R_{cable} = 0.005$ Ω.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{stall, actual} &= \frac{V}{R_a + R_{field} + R_{batt} + R_{cable}} \\
&= \frac{12}{0.005 + 0.004 + 0.01 + 0.005} \\
&= \frac{12}{0.024} = 500 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the stall torque.** With $K k_f = 0.05$ (typical constant):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{stall} &= K k_f \cdot I_{stall}^2 \\
&= 0.05 \times (500)^2 \\
&= 0.05 \times 250000 \\
&= 12500 \text{ N·m (theoretical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply gear reduction ratio $i_r = 3.8$ and gear efficiency $\eta_g = 0.9$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{crank, max} &= T_{stall} \cdot i_r \cdot \eta_g \\
&= 12500 \times 3.8 \times 0.9 \\
&= 42750 \text{ N·m (far too high — confirms theoretical limit)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, magnetic saturation limits $k_f$ at high currents. The actual peak cranking torque from a 1.4 kW starter is typically **40–80 N·m** at the ring gear.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Cranking Speed Calculation

**Given:** Ring gear has $Z_{ring} = 138$ teeth, pinion has $Z_{pinion} = 9$ teeth. Motor runs at 4000 rpm under load.

**Step 1: Calculate the gear reduction ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_r &= \frac{Z_{ring}}{Z_{pinion}} = \frac{138}{9} = 15.33
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the cranking speed (engine rpm).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{crank} &= \frac{N_{motor}}{i_r} = \frac{4000}{15.33} \approx 261 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A reduction ratio of **15:1** is too high for a conventional starter — modern gear reduction starters use **3.5:1 to 4.5:1** with the motor itself running at higher rpm. The example above represents the equivalent ratio from the pinion-to-ring-gear meshing, not internal gear reduction.

**Step 3: Real gear reduction starter example.**

A modern offset gear reduction starter has:
- Internal planetary gear set: $i_{planetary} = 3.8$
- Pinion-to-ring-gear ratio: $i_{pinion} = 15.33$
- Motor speed under load: $N_{motor} = 4500$ rpm

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{crank} &= \frac{N_{motor}}{i_{planetary} \cdot i_{pinion}} \\
&= \frac{4500}{3.8 \times 15.33} \\
&= \frac{4500}{58.25} \\
&\approx 77.25 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the typical **80–100 rpm** cranking speed for diesel engines. ✓

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Power and Efficiency

**Step 1: Calculate the mechanical power output of the motor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{mech} &= T \cdot \omega = T \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the electrical input power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{elec} &= V \cdot I_a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{P_{mech}}{P_{elec}} = \frac{T \cdot 2\pi N}{60 \cdot V \cdot I_a}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** $T = 8$ N·m, $N = 4000$ rpm, $V = 12$ V, $I_a = 200$ A.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{mech} &= 8 \times \frac{2\pi \times 4000}{60} = 8 \times 418.88 = 3351 \text{ W} \\
P_{elec} &= 12 \times 200 = 2400 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The mechanical power (3351 W) is **higher** than the electrical input (2400 W) — this is **physically impossible** and indicates the assumed torque value is too high for the given current. In practice, the relationship is constrained by:
> $$\eta_{starter} = \frac{T \cdot 2\pi N / 60}{V \cdot I_a} \leq 1$$

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a pre-engaging starter motor drive mechanism.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **pre-engaging starter motor drive mechanism** is the modern, positive-engagement type used in almost all passenger cars. Its key components are:

1. **Solenoid switch** mounted on top of the starter motor — contains pull-in and hold-in windings.
2. **Shift lever (shift fork)** — a pivoted lever mechanically connected to the solenoid plunger.
3. **Overrunning clutch (roller clutch)** — a one-way clutch that transmits torque from the armature to the pinion in one direction only.
4. **Pinion gear** — slides on the armature shaft splines.

**Working:** When the ignition switch is turned to START, the solenoid windings are energized. The plunger is pulled in, which:
- Pushes the shift fork → slides the pinion along the armature shaft splines into mesh with the flywheel ring gear.
- Simultaneously closes the main solenoid contacts, sending full battery current to the starter motor.

The armature rotates, and torque is transmitted through the overrunning clutch to the pinion and then to the ring gear. Once the engine starts and its rpm exceeds the pinion rpm, the overrunning clutch automatically freewheels, preventing the engine from driving the starter motor at destructive speeds. When the driver releases the key, the solenoid de-energizes, the spring retracts the pinion, and the main contacts open. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the operating characteristics of a DC series motor used as a starter motor. Why is a series motor preferred over a shunt motor for starter duty?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **operating characteristics of a DC series motor** are governed by the inverse relationship between speed and torque. As load (and hence current) increases, speed decreases rapidly. This produces a high starting torque — the exact requirement of a starter motor. (1 mark)

**Key equations:**
- $T \propto I_a^2$ (torque varies as the square of current)
- $N \propto \dfrac{1}{I_a}$ (speed varies inversely with current)
- $N \propto \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{T}}$ (speed-torque hyperbolic relation) (1 mark)

**Why series motor and not shunt?**
A shunt motor has a constant field flux independent of armature current. Its starting torque is limited to $T_{shunt} = K \phi I_a$ where $\phi$ is constant. At the high currents drawn during cranking, a shunt motor produces insufficient torque and would overheat the armature. A series motor, with $\phi \propto I_a$, develops $T_{series} \propto I_a^2$, giving 5–10× higher starting torque — ideal for cranking a cold, high-compression engine. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Starter Motor Types and Construction**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Modified]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the construction of a conventional DC series-wound starter motor with a neat block diagram. List all major components and state the function of each.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Compare direct drive and gear reduction starter motors. With a derivation, show how the gear reduction ratio affects the cranking torque delivered to the engine.

---

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction of Conventional DC Series Starter Motor (7 marks):**

A conventional starter motor consists of the following major components:

**1. Field Frame/Yoke (1 mark)** — A cylindrical soft-steel housing that encloses the field poles. It provides a low-reluctance path for the magnetic flux generated by the field coils.

**2. Field Coils (4-pole design) (1 mark)** — Heavy-gauge copper ribbon wound around each pole shoe. The four field coils are connected in **series with the armature winding** so that the entire armature current flows through them. This produces flux proportional to load current — the basis of the high-torque characteristic.

**3. Armature (1 mark)** — A laminated silicon-steel core mounted on the motor shaft, containing insulated slots with embedded conductors. When current flows through these conductors in the presence of the magnetic field, electromagnetic torque is produced.

**4. Commutator (0.5 mark)** — A cylindrical assembly of copper segments at one end of the armature, insulated by mica. It reverses the current direction in each armature coil at the right moment to maintain unidirectional rotation.

**5. Brushes (4 brushes) (0.5 mark)** — Spring-loaded copper-graphite blocks that ride on the commutator surface. They provide the electrical connection between the external circuit and the rotating armature.

**6. End Frames (0.5 mark)** — The commutator-end frame (CEF) and drive-end frame (DEF) house the bearings (sleeve type) and support the armature shaft.

**7. Solenoid Switch (0.5 mark)** — Mounted on top, it contains the pull-in and hold-in windings that shift the pinion and close the main contacts.

**8. Drive Mechanism (0.5 mark)** — The overrunning clutch and pinion assembly, mounted on the armature shaft splines, transmits torque to the ring gear.

**9. Shift Fork (0.5 mark)** — A pivoted lever that converts the linear motion of the solenoid plunger into axial motion of the pinion.

**10. Terminals and Cables (0.5 mark)** — Heavy-gauge battery cable (typically 25–50 mm²) for the main current path, and smaller control wires for the solenoid.

[Total: 7 marks]

**Part (b) — Direct Drive vs. Gear Reduction Starter (7 marks):**

| Feature | Direct Drive (DD) | Gear Reduction (OSGR) |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Pinion directly on armature shaft | Planetary gear set between motor and pinion |
| Motor speed | 1,000–3,000 rpm | 3,000–4,500 rpm |
| Motor size | Large, heavy | Small, light |
| Cranking torque | Limited by motor torque | Amplified by gear ratio |
| Weight | 8–15 kg | 4–8 kg |
| Application | Older large engines | Modern compact cars |

**Derivation of Cranking Torque Amplification (4 marks of 7):**

Let the gear reduction ratio be:
$$
i_r = \frac{N_{motor}}{N_{pinion}} = \frac{Z_{ring-gear-internal}}{Z_{sun-gear}}
$$

For a typical OSGR starter, $i_r = 3.5$ to $4.5$.

The torque at the pinion is related to the motor torque by:
$$
T_{pinion} = T_{motor} \cdot i_r \cdot \eta_{gear}
$$

Where $\eta_{gear}$ is the gear mesh efficiency (typically 0.90–0.95 for planetary gear sets).

**Numerical Example:**
Given $T_{motor} = 8$ N·m, $i_r = 4.0$, $\eta_{gear} = 0.92$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{pinion} &= 8 \times 4.0 \times 0.92 \\
&= 29.44 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

The pinion then drives the flywheel ring gear. The ring gear acts as a second-stage reduction (e.g., $Z_{ring}/Z_{pinion} = 138/9 \approx 15.3$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{crank, max} &= T_{pinion} \times \frac{Z_{ring}}{Z_{pinion}} \times \eta_{ring} \\
&= 29.44 \times 15.3 \times 0.95 \\
&\approx 428 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the maximum torque the engine receives during cranking, which is more than sufficient to overcome the static compression and friction of a typical passenger car engine. (3 marks for derivation + 1 mark for numerical example + 2 marks for the comparison table + 1 mark for conclusion)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Starting Circuit and Relay**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Modified]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Draw and explain the complete starting circuit of a passenger car with a starter relay/solenoid. Include the battery, ignition switch, neutral safety switch, clutch switch, solenoid, and starter motor. Explain the function of the starter relay.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* With circuit diagrams, explain the difference between a direct-drive starting system and a relay-controlled starting system. Why is a starter relay mandatory in modern vehicles?

---

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Complete Starting Circuit with Solenoid (7 marks):**

**Circuit Description (5 marks):**

The complete starting circuit consists of two sub-circuits: a **control circuit** (low current, ~3–5 A) and a **power circuit** (high current, 150–400 A).

**Control Circuit Path:**
Battery (+) → Ignition Switch (START) → Neutral Safety Switch (closed only in P/N for AT, or Clutch Switch closed in MT) → Solenoid Pull-In and Hold-In Windings (in parallel) → Ground (–).

**Power Circuit Path:**
Battery (+) → Main Solenoid Contacts (normally open, closed when energized) → Starter Motor Field Coils → Armature → Brushes → Ground (–).

**Operation Sequence (2 marks):**

1. Driver turns ignition key to START.
2. Control current (~5 A) flows through solenoid windings → creates magnetic field → pulls plunger in.
3. Plunger motion: (i) pushes shift fork → pinion slides into mesh with ring gear; (ii) closes main solenoid contacts.
4. Main contacts close → full battery current (~200 A) flows to starter motor.
5. Motor cranks engine.
6. Engine starts → driver releases key → control current interrupted → spring returns plunger → main contacts open → pinion retracts.

**Function of the Starter Relay (built into the solenoid):**
The starter relay performs **two simultaneous functions**:
- **Mechanical:** Shifts the pinion into mesh with the ring gear via the shift fork.
- **Electrical:** Acts as a high-current switch, allowing a small control current (~5 A from the ignition switch) to control a large motor current (~200 A) without exposing the delicate ignition switch contacts to destructive arcing.

[7 marks: 1 mark circuit diagram, 1 mark control path description, 1 mark power path description, 1 mark operation sequence, 1 mark dual function, 1 mark protection role, 1 mark application]

**Part (b) — Direct Drive vs. Relay-Controlled Starting System (7 marks):**

**Direct-Drive Starting System (Without Relay) — Obsolete (3 marks):**

In a direct-drive system, the ignition switch contacts directly carry the full starter current (150–400 A). The switch must be physically large, robustly built, and use heavy contacts. The heavy cables from the battery go all the way to the dashboard ignition switch, then back to the starter. This system is unsafe because:
- Long high-current cables cause **voltage drop** (reducing cranking power).
- The ignition switch contacts suffer severe **arcing and pitting** with each start.
- **Fire hazard** from overheated cables and switches.
- The dashboard wiring becomes bulky and dangerous.

**Relay-Controlled Starting System — Modern Standard (3 marks):**

In a relay-controlled system, the ignition switch handles only a small control current (~5 A). Heavy-gauge cables go directly from the battery to the starter solenoid, and only thin control wires run to the dashboard. The relay's electromagnetic contacts are designed to switch high currents reliably for tens of thousands of cycles.

**Why a Starter Relay is Mandatory in Modern Vehicles (1 mark):**

A starter relay is mandatory because:
1. **Driver safety** — Dashboard switches are operated by humans and must carry only low, safe currents.
2. **Reliability** — Solid-state ignition switches cannot switch 200 A directly without failure.
3. **Voltage drop minimization** — Short, heavy cables from battery to starter reduce $I^2R$ losses.
4. **Integration with safety systems** — The relay allows easy integration with neutral safety, clutch, and immobilizer switches.
5. **Cost and weight** — Heavy dashboard wiring is expensive; centralized relays save weight and cost.

[7 marks: 3 + 3 + 1]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing pull-in and hold-in windings:** Many students forget that the **hold-in winding is connected in series with the motor** during cranking, while the **pull-in winding is shorted out** by the main contacts. This is a critical detail worth 2 marks.
> 2. **Misnaming the Bendix drive:** The Bendix is **inertia-engaged**, not solenoid-engaged. Pre-engaging starters use the solenoid. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the overrunning clutch function:** It is **not** just a clutch — it specifically prevents the engine from driving the starter to destruction after the engine starts. This is a 2-mark item.
> 4. **Wrong gear reduction formula:** The internal planetary ratio multiplies the motor torque — students often confuse the sign or write $T = T/i_r$ instead of $T = T \cdot i_r$.
> 5. **Missing safety switches:** The neutral safety switch (AT) and clutch switch (MT) are *mandatory* in modern circuits. Omitting them costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Not mentioning magnetic saturation:** At very high currents, the series motor's flux saturates, so the torque is no longer strictly $I_a^2$ — it becomes closer to linear at high loads.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Starter motor** is a **DC series-wound, short-duty, high-torque** machine designed to crank IC engines to 80–200 rpm.
- [x] **Series motor** is preferred over shunt because $T \propto I_a^2$ gives explosive starting torque.
- [x] **Speed-torque curve** of a series motor is **inverse hyperbolic** — high torque at low speed, runaway at no-load.
- [x] **Field coils** are in **series** with the armature, carrying full motor current.
- [x] **Four field poles** is the standard for automotive starters.
- [x] **Commutator + brushes** maintain unidirectional torque via current reversal.
- [x] **Bendix drive** uses **inertia** to engage the pinion; **pre-engaging** uses the **solenoid and shift fork**.
- [x] **Overrunning clutch** (one-way roller clutch) **prevents the engine from driving the starter** after combustion begins.
- [x] **Gear reduction starters** use planetary gear sets (3.5:1 to 4.5:1) to amplify torque.
- [x] **Cranking current** of 150–400 A is switched by the **solenoid main contacts**, not the ignition switch.
- [x] **Starter relay/solenoid** has **two windings**: **pull-in** (high current, short duration) and **hold-in** (low current, continuous during cranking).
- [x] **Neutral safety switch** (AT) and **clutch switch** (MT) prevent cranking in unsafe conditions.
- [x] **Stall current** $I_{stall} = V / R$ is limited by battery internal resistance, cable resistance, and contact resistance.
- [x] **Starter efficiency** is typically **55–70 %** for conventional, **75–85 %** for gear reduction, and **85–92 %** for ISG.
- [x] **Cycle life**: Conventional ~30,000 starts; gear reduction ~100,000; ISG ~300,000+ for auto start-stop.
- [x] **PM starters** use permanent magnets instead of field coils — lighter but with limited flux control.
- [x] **ISG (Integrated Starter-Generator)** combines starter + alternator + mild hybrid functions.
- [x] **24 V systems** are used in commercial vehicles and large diesels to reduce current and cable size.
- [x] **Cranking torque** at the flywheel is the **product of motor torque × gear ratio × efficiencies**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Series motor for torque, solenoid for switching, overrunning clutch for safety, and relay for protection — these four pillars define every modern automotive starting system."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
