Define a knock sensor. Why is it essential in a modern petrol engine? (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** A knock sensor is a **piezoelectric vibration transducer** mounted on the engine block / cylinder head that detects abnormal combustion (knock / detonation) and converts it into a voltage signal sent to the ECU. It is essential because: 1. It allows the ECU to **retard ignition timing** during knock, protecting pistons, rings, and bearings. 2. It enables the engine to operate near the **MBT (Minimum spark advance for Best Torque)**, giving **better fuel economy** and **lower emissions** without the safety risk of running conservative timing. **(3 marks — definition 1, importance 2)** --- ### **Q2. List any two types of knock sensors and state the principle on which it works. (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Principle:** **Direct Piezoelectric Effect** — when mechanical stress is applied to a piezoelectric material (e.g., PZT), an electric charge is generated proportional to the applied force. **Types:** 1. **Resonant (tuned / narrowband)** — mechanically tuned to a single frequency (~7 kHz), gives high output only at that frequency. 2. **Non-resonant (broadband)** — wideband accelerometer-type, flat response across 1 kHz to 22 kHz, used with DSP/FFT in modern ECUs. **(3 marks — principle 1, types 2)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choice-based, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Module has an ESE Part B question with **internal choice**. Solve **either Option A OR Option B**. Each option has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. --- ### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper]` **Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **knock sensor system** in a petrol engine. Describe how the ECU uses this signal for **closed-loop ignition timing control**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram** (draw flowchart showing: Knock → Sensor → Bandpass Filter → ADC → Threshold Compare → Retard/Advance → Ignition Coil — already shown in Section 4.1). **Working Explanation:** 1. The knock sensor is a **piezoelectric device** rigidly bolted (torque ~20–25 Nm) to the engine block. 2. When knock occurs, the cylinder pressure shock waves create **high-frequency vibration** in the block (5–15 kHz). 3. The seismic mass inside the sensor vibrates, stressing the **PZT ceramic**, generating an analog voltage. 4. The voltage (typically 100 mV to 5 V peak-to-peak) is routed via **shielded twisted pair** to the ECU. 5. The ECU's signal-conditioning circuit applies a **5–15 kHz bandpass filter**, removing low-frequency combustion rumble and high-frequency ring noise. 6. The signal is **rectified, integrated** over each cylinder's combustion window, and **peak-detected** for that cylinder. 7. The peak is compared to a **running background noise average**. 8. If the **signal-to-noise ratio** exceeds the threshold (≈ 6 dB), knock is flagged. 9. The ECU **retards the spark** of that cylinder by 1°–3° ATDC on the next firing. 10. After several clean cycles, timing is **gradually re-advanced** to MBT. **Result:** Closed-loop operation at the knock limit, ensuring maximum efficiency with safety. **(7 marks — diagram 2, working steps 4, ECU logic 1)** --- **Q. (b)** A knock sensor has a piezoelectric coefficient $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N, an electrode area $A = 50$ mm², and an internal capacitance $C_p = 1$ nF. The seismic mass is $m = 5$ g, and the engine block experiences a knock-induced acceleration of $a = 50$ g at a frequency of 7 kHz. Calculate the **peak output voltage** of the sensor. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N - $A = 50 \text{ mm}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m² - $C_p = 1 \text{ nF} = 1 \times 10^{-9}$ F - $m = 5 \text{ g} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ kg - $a = 50 \text{ g} = 50 \times 9.81 = 490.5$ m/s² - $f = 7$ kHz (not needed for peak voltage, only for waveform) **Step 1: Compute the inertial force on the piezo element.** $$ F = m \cdot a = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 490.5 $$ $$ F = 2.4525 \text{ N} $$ **Step 2: Compute the charge generated by the piezo element.** $$ Q = d_{33} \cdot F = 400 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.4525 $$ $$ Q = 981 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C} = 981 \text{ pC} $$ **Step 3: Compute the peak output voltage across the piezo capacitance.** $$ V_{peak} = \dfrac{Q}{C_p} = \dfrac{981 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} $$ $$ V_{peak} = 0.981 \text{ V} \approx 980 \text{ mV} $$ **Result:** The peak output voltage is approximately **0.98 V**, which is comfortably above the ECU's detection threshold. **(7 marks — F calculation 2, Q calculation 2, V calculation 2, conclusion 1)** --- ### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **Q. (a)** Explain the construction and **types** of knock sensors. Compare resonant and non-resonant types in detail. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Construction** (refer to Section 2.4 of these notes): - PZT piezoelectric ceramic disc, tungsten seismic mass, steel housing, 2-pin shielded connector, internal damping material. - Sealed against oil/water with rubber grommet. - Mounting thread: M8 or M10, torque 20–25 Nm. **Types:** | Feature | Resonant (Tuned) | Non-Resonant (Broadband) | |---|---|---| | Frequency response | Narrow peak at $f_n \approx 7$ kHz | Flat from ~1 kHz to 22 kHz | | Mechanism | Spring–mass tuned to knock frequency | Accelerometer with PZT | | Output amplitude | High (Q-amplified) | Lower, but uniform | | Knock detection | Only at tuned frequency | All knock modes | | ECU processing | Simple threshold | DSP/FFT required | | Era of use | Older vehicles (pre-2000) | All modern vehicles | | Cost | Lower | Slightly higher | **Resonant sensor:** The seismic mass on the PZT forms a spring–mass system. Natural frequency is tuned to ~7 kHz. When the engine block vibrates at exactly this frequency, the sensor "rings" with maximum amplitude, producing a high-output pulse that is easy to detect. Limitation: misses knock at other frequencies. **Non-resonant sensor:** The PZT acts as a stiff accelerometer with flat response over a wide band. ECU performs Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to identify *which* frequencies contain knock. Highly accurate, can identify knock in each cylinder individually. Used in modern Bosch, Denso, Continental ECUs. **(7 marks — construction 2, type-1 details 2, type-2 details 2, comparison 1)** --- **Q. (b)** A vehicle is fitted with a knock sensor. Discuss the **causes and effects of engine knock**, and explain how the **ECU takes corrective action** when knock is detected. Also mention the relevant **OBD-II diagnostic trouble codes** related to the knock sensor. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Causes of Engine Knock:** 1. Low-octane fuel (or incorrect fuel grade for the engine). 2. **Excessively advanced ignition timing** (ECU map error, sensor failure). 3. **Carbon deposits** in combustion chamber (hot spots, increased compression). 4. **High intake air temperature** (lower volumetric efficiency, more end-gas auto-ignition tendency). 5. **Lean air-fuel mixture** (higher combustion temperatures). 6. **High ambient temperature** and altitude effects. 7. **Faulty cooling system** (overheated cylinder head). **Effects of Engine Knock:** 1. **Pistons, rings, bearings** — mechanical damage from shock loading. 2. **Exhaust valves & spark plugs** — overheating, pre-ignition. 3. **Loss of power** and **fuel economy**. 4. **Audible pinging** — customer complaint. 5. **Catalyst damage** due to high exhaust gas temperature. 6. In extreme cases — **catastrophic engine failure**. **ECU Corrective Action:** 1. The ECU monitors the knock sensor signal continuously in the 5–15 kHz band. 2. When knock is detected, the ECU **retards the ignition timing** of the affected cylinder by 1°–3° per firing event. 3. This continues until the knock signal disappears. 4. The ECU then **slowly advances the timing back** over many cycles to recover MBT performance. 5. If persistent, the ECU may illuminate the **Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL)** and store a fault code. **Relevant OBD-II Codes:** - **P0325** — Knock Sensor 1 Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1) - **P0326** — Knock Sensor 1 Circuit Range/Performance - **P0327** — Knock Sensor 1 Circuit Low Input - **P0328** — Knock Sensor 1 Circuit High Input - **P0330** — Knock Sensor 2 Circuit Malfunction (Bank 2) - **P0332** — Knock Sensor 2 Circuit Low Input **(7 marks — causes 2, effects 2, ECU action 2, OBD codes 1)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes — Avoid These!** > > 1. **Do not say "knock sensor sends a digital signal"** — it is **analog, passive, two-wire**, with no supply voltage. (−1 mark typical) > 2. **Do not say "knock sensor detects engine RPM"** — that is the **crank/cam position sensor's** job. The knock sensor detects **vibration** only. (−1 mark) > 3. **Do not say "knock sensor directly controls the spark plug"** — it provides **input to the ECU**; the **ignition coil** is the actuator. (−1 mark) > 4. **Do not write "ECU increases timing when knock is heard"** — the action is to **retard** (decrease) timing, not increase. This is the most common conceptual error. (−1 mark) > 5. **Do not forget the torque value (20–25 Nm)** if asked about mounting — students often omit this. (−0.5 to −1 mark) > 6. **Do not say "the sensor is powered by 5 V"** — it is **passive**, no power supply. (−1 mark) > 7. **Always mention the bandpass filter (5–15 kHz)** in ECU signal processing — this is a high-value keyword for full marks. > 8. **For the resonant sensor derivation**, do not forget $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$ — this is the most-clipped formula on KTU papers. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Knock-Sensor Quick-Recall List (Pin This on Your Wall!):** - **Full form:** Knock Sensor (KS); also called **Detonation Sensor**. - **Principle:** **Direct piezoelectric effect** in PZT ceramic. - **Sensor type:** **Passive**, **2-wire**, **analog**, **high-impedance**, accelerometer-style. - **No power supply** is required by the sensor itself. - **Knock frequency band:** **5 kHz to 15 kHz** (most common: 6–8 kHz). - **Mounting location:** Engine block (between cylinders) or cylinder head. - **Mounting torque:** **20 – 25 Nm** (clean, dry, oil-free threads). - **Sensor output:** Typically **100 mV – 5 V peak-to-peak** analog voltage. - **Output is proportional to acceleration:** $V_{out} \propto \dfrac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}}$. - **Two types:** **Resonant (tuned, narrowband)** vs **Non-resonant (broadband, accelerometer)**. - **Modern vehicles use non-resonant** + ECU DSP/FFT. - **Knock causes:** low-octane fuel, over-advanced timing, carbon deposits, hot spots, lean mixture. - **Knock effects:** piston damage, overheating, power loss, ping noise, catalyst damage. - **ECU action on knock detection:** **Retard timing 1°–3° per event**, then **slow re-advance** to MBT. - **MBT** = Minimum spark advance for Best Torque. - **Piezo equation:** $V = \dfrac{d \cdot m \cdot a}{C_p}$. - **Resonant natural frequency:** $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$. - **Knock frequency estimate:** $f \approx \dfrac{c \cdot k}{2L} \Rightarrow$ typical mode-1 ≈ 6 kHz. - **Knock detection criterion:** signal **≥ ~6 dB** above background noise. - **OBD-II codes:** **P0325, P0326, P0327, P0328, P0330, P0332**. - **Connector type:** 2-pin **shielded** (signal + ground). - **Sensor must NOT be painted** at the mounting surface. - **Re-use of mounting bolt** — many are one-time-use (torque-to-yield). - **ECU does FFT** to identify per-cylinder knock, then performs **selective per-cylinder retard**. - **Cold-start strategy:** ECU uses knock sensor as a *confirmation* that timing advance is safe. - **Real-world impact:** Knock sensor enables **5–10 % fuel economy gain** vs. a detuned engine. - **Symptom of faulty KS:** MIL on, fixed/limp-mode timing, audible pinging, poor fuel economy. - **Symptom of loose KS bolt:** intermittent MIL, false retard, hesitation. - **Test method:** tap engine block with a metal rod near KS while engine runs; ECU should briefly retard timing (scan tool verifies). > **END OF MODULE 4 — KNOCK SENSOR NOTES** > **© KTU 2024 Scheme | PCMUT501 | Module 4**

topics.title: Optical Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT501 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optical Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Optical Sensor** is a non-contact electronic transducer that converts incident light photons (electromagnetic radiation in the ultraviolet, visible, or infrared spectrum) into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, or change in resistance). In automotive engineering, optical sensors are critical sub-systems of the modern vehicle's Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), powertrain management, and comfort/lighting control modules. They operate on the quantum mechanical principle of the **photoelectric effect** (discovered by Heinrich Hertz in 1887 and mathematically formalized by Einstein in 1905), where incident photons with energy $E = h\nu$ (where $h$ is Planck's constant and $\nu$ is the frequency of light) interact with the semiconductor lattice to liberate charge carriers (electron-hole pairs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Optical sensors in automobiles are devices that detect light intensity, position, distance, or the presence/absence of objects by converting light energy into electrical signals, forming the backbone of vision-based driver assistance and engine control systems.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing in a dark room holding a digital camera. When you open the curtains and sunlight floods in, the camera's image sensor (a CMOS or CCD array) instantly reacts — millions of pixels convert incoming light into electrical voltages that the processor reads to form a picture. **An optical sensor does exactly this, but for a single purpose**: instead of capturing a full image, it measures *how much* light is hitting it, *where* a light beam is positioned, or *how far away* an object is based on reflected light.

**Automotive Example Analogy:**
- **Your eyes = Optical sensors**: When you drive into a tunnel, your pupils dilate (sensor sensitivity increases) so you can see better. Similarly, an automotive **Light Sensor (ALS – Ambient Light Sensor)** tells the ECU to automatically turn on the headlights.
- **Bats in a cave = LiDAR**: Bats emit sound and listen for echoes to "see" in the dark. A **LiDAR sensor** emits laser pulses and measures the reflected time to build a 3D map of the road.

## 1.3 Classification of Optical Sensors Used in Vehicles

| **Type** | **Operating Principle** | **Primary Automotive Application** |
|----------|------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Photoresistor (LDR)** | Resistance changes with incident light intensity | Twilight detectors, automatic headlamps |
| **Photodiode** | Photocurrent generated in reverse-biased PN junction | Rain sensors, light detection, optical encoders |
| **Phototransistor** | Light amplifies base current in BJT structure | Optical encoders, object detection, safety curtains |
| **Photovoltaic Cell** | Generates EMF when illuminated | Sunroof control, solar-powered vehicles, battery charging |
| **Laser-Based LiDAR** | Time-of-flight measurement of laser pulses | Adaptive cruise control, emergency braking, autonomous driving |
| **Infrared (IR) Sensor** | Detects thermal radiation (heat) | Night vision assist, occupant detection, blind-spot monitoring |
| **Camera-based (CMOS/CCD)** | 2D image array of photodiodes | Lane departure warning, traffic sign recognition, parking assist |
| **Fiber Optic Sensor** | Light transmitted through fiber, modulated by external parameter | In-cylinder combustion analysis, EMI-immune measurement |

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants and metrics are **mandatory** for KTU numerical problems and derivations. Memorize these values.

- **Planck's Constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J}\cdot\text{s}$
- **Speed of Light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Photon Energy:** $E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$
- **Standard Visible Spectrum:** Wavelength range from **380 nm (violet)** to **750 nm (red)**
- **Near-Infrared (NIR) for Automotive LiDAR:** Typically **905 nm** or **1550 nm** wavelengths
- **Photosensitivity Unit:** $S = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}}$ measured in **A/W** (Amperes per Watt)
- **Quantum Efficiency (QE):** $\eta = \frac{\text{Number of electrons generated}}{\text{Number of incident photons}} \times 100\%$
- **Responsivity:** $R_\lambda = \frac{\eta \lambda q}{hc}$ where $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$ is the electron charge
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles of Key Optical Sensor Types

### 2.1.1 Photoresistor (Light Dependent Resistor — LDR)

A photoresistor is a passive two-terminal component fabricated from a high-resistivity semiconductor such as **Cadmium Sulfide (CdS)**. In the absence of light, very few charge carriers are available in the conduction band, resulting in high dark resistance (typically $\geq 1 \text{ M}\Omega$). When photons with energy greater than the bandgap strike the material, valence electrons gain sufficient energy to jump to the conduction band, dramatically reducing resistance.

**Why it works (The Physics):**
- The CdS bandgap is $E_g \approx 2.42 \text{ eV}$, which corresponds to photons in the visible spectrum ($\lambda \leq 512 \text{ nm}$)
- More light → more photons → more electron-hole pairs → lower resistance
- This is a **photoconductive** effect (resistance change, not current generation)

**LDR Resistance Equation:**
The relationship between incident illumination $L$ (in lux) and resistance $R$ is given by the empirical formula:
$$R = A \cdot L^{-\alpha}$$
where $A$ is a constant depending on the material, and $\alpha$ is the **spectral sensitivity exponent** (typically 0.7 to 0.9 for CdS).

### 2.1.2 Photodiode

A photodiode is a PN junction diode operated in **reverse bias**. Photons striking the depletion region generate electron-hole pairs, which are separated by the built-in electric field, producing a photocurrent proportional to the incident light intensity.

**Key Equations:**
$$I_{total} = I_{dark} + I_{photo} = I_{saturation} \left(e^{\frac{qV}{kT}} - 1\right) - I_{ph}$$
where $I_{ph} = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt}$ is the photocurrent, $I_{dark}$ is the reverse leakage current in the absence of light, $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$ is the Boltzmann constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin.

**Photodiode Operating Modes:**
1. **Photoconductive mode:** Reverse-biased — faster response, linear output
2. **Photovoltaic mode:** Zero bias — used in solar cells, lower noise

### 2.1.3 Phototransistor

A phototransistor is essentially a bipolar junction transistor (BJT) where the base current is supplied by a photodiode (light-sensitive base-collector junction). The transistor's current gain ($\beta$, typically 100–1000) amplifies the tiny photocurrent, making phototransistors far more sensitive than photodiodes.

$$I_{CE} = \beta \cdot I_{ph} = \beta \cdot R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt}$$

**Trade-off:** Higher sensitivity but slower response (due to base-collector capacitance) compared to photodiodes.

### 2.1.4 Optical Encoder (Incremental Type)

An optical encoder consists of:
- A **light source** (LED, usually infrared)
- A **code wheel/disk** with alternating transparent and opaque slots
- A **photodetector array** (photodiodes or phototransistors)
- Signal conditioning electronics

As the code wheel rotates (coupled to a shaft), the light beam is alternately transmitted and blocked, producing a **pulse train** at the photodetector output. The frequency of pulses is proportional to shaft speed.

$$f_{pulse} = \frac{N \cdot \omega}{2\pi}$$
where $N$ is the number of slots on the code wheel and $\omega$ is the angular velocity in rad/s.

**Automotive Use:** Crankshaft position sensor, wheel speed sensor (ABS), steering angle sensor.

### 2.1.5 LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)

LiDAR emits laser pulses (typically at 905 nm or 1550 nm wavelength) and measures the time-of-flight ($t$) of the reflected pulse. The distance to the target is calculated as:
$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$
The factor of 2 accounts for the round-trip (pulse travels to object and back).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** LiDAR operating at **1550 nm** is preferred for automotive use over 905 nm because 1550 nm eye-safe power limits are ~40× higher, allowing more powerful pulses and longer range detection without eye-safety concerns.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains all critical formulas you must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE problems on Optical Sensors. Pay close attention to units!

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Variables & Units** |
|-------------|----------------|----------------------|
| $E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $\lambda$ in m |
| $R = A \cdot L^{-\alpha}$ | LDR resistance vs illumination | $L$ in lux, $\alpha \approx 0.7$–$0.9$ |
| $I_{ph} = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt}$ | Photocurrent from incident optical power | $R_\lambda$ in A/W, $P_{opt}$ in W |
| $R_\lambda = \frac{\eta \lambda q}{hc}$ | Spectral responsivity | $\eta$ dimensionless (0–1), $\lambda$ in m |
| $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | LiDAR distance (time-of-flight) | $d$ in m, $t$ in seconds |
| $f_{pulse} = \frac{N \cdot \omega}{2\pi}$ | Optical encoder pulse frequency | $N$ = slots/rev, $\omega$ in rad/s |
| $I_{CE} = \beta \cdot I_{ph}$ | Phototransistor collector current | $\beta$ = current gain, $I_{ph}$ in A |
| $V_{OC} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_s} + 1\right)$ | Photovoltaic cell open-circuit voltage | $I_s$ = saturation current, $V_{OC}$ in V |
| $P_{max} = V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF$ | Maximum solar cell power | $FF$ = fill factor (typically 0.7–0.85) |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Optical sensors are pivotal in **six major automotive domains**:

1. **Safety (ADAS)**: Forward collision warning, automatic emergency braking (AEB), blind-spot detection, lane-keep assist (cameras + LiDAR)
2. **Engine Management**: Crank/cam position sensing (optical encoders), knock detection
3. **Comfort & Convenience**: Automatic headlamps (ALS), rain-sensing wipers, auto-dimming rear-view mirrors
4. **Climate Control**: Solar load sensors for HVAC optimization (sunroof-integrated photodiodes)
5. **Emissions Control**: Oxygen sensing (NOT covered here — uses electrochemical principle)
6. **Autonomous Driving**: Multi-camera perception suites, solid-state LiDAR arrays, 4D radar-vision fusion
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Photon Energy from Wavelength (Foundation)

> **Problem Statement:** Derive the relationship between photon energy and incident wavelength, and compute the energy of a single photon emitted by an automotive LiDAR system operating at $\lambda = 905 \text{ nm}$.

**Step 1: Start with Planck's quantum hypothesis.**
Max Planck proposed that electromagnetic radiation is quantized into discrete packets called photons, each carrying energy proportional to frequency:
$$E = h \cdot \nu$$
where $h$ is Planck's constant and $\nu$ is the frequency of the EM wave.

**Step 2: Relate frequency to wavelength.**
For any wave, frequency and wavelength are related by the wave equation $c = \lambda \cdot \nu$, hence:
$$\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3: Substitute to get the energy-wavelength relationship.**
$$E = h \cdot \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4: Plug in numerical values for $\lambda = 905 \text{ nm}$.**

First, convert the wavelength to SI units (meters):
$$\lambda = 905 \text{ nm} = 905 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 9.05 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$

Now substitute:
$$E = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J}\cdot\text{s}) \times (3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s})}{9.05 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5: Calculate numerator.**
$$h \cdot c = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8 = 19.878 \times 10^{-26} = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J}\cdot\text{m}$$

**Step 6: Divide by $\lambda$.**
$$E = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{9.05 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.2196 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J} = 2.196 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

**Step 7: Convert to electron-volts (eV) for practical engineering use.**
Since $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$:
$$E = \frac{2.196 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.371 \text{ eV}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{E_{photon} = 2.196 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = 1.371 \text{ eV}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This photon energy (1.371 eV) is greater than the bandgap of silicon ($E_g^{Si} = 1.12 \text{ eV}$), confirming that silicon photodetectors can efficiently detect 905 nm LiDAR pulses. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: LiDAR Distance Calculation (Time-of-Flight)

> **Problem Statement:** A forward-facing automotive LiDAR emits a laser pulse that returns to the sensor after a round-trip time of $t = 1.33 \text{ \mu s}$. Calculate the distance to the detected object (vehicle ahead). Assume the pulse travels through air at approximately the speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$.

**Step 1: Recall the time-of-flight distance formula.**
Since the laser pulse must travel **to the object and back**, the total distance traversed by light is $2d$, where $d$ is the one-way distance:
$$2d = c \cdot t$$

**Step 2: Solve for $d$.**
$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 3: Convert time to SI units.**
$$t = 1.33 \text{ \mu s} = 1.33 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Substitute values.**
$$d = \frac{(3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}) \times (1.33 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s})}{2}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the numerator.**
$$c \cdot t = 3 \times 10^8 \times 1.33 \times 10^{-6} = 3.99 \times 10^2 = 399 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Divide by 2.**
$$d = \frac{399}{2} = 199.5 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{d \approx 200 \text{ m}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The detected object is approximately **200 meters** ahead. This is consistent with typical automotive long-range LiDAR specifications (200–250 m range), enabling highway-speed adaptive cruise control.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Optical Encoder Angular Velocity

> **Problem Statement:** An optical encoder mounted on a vehicle's crankshaft has $N = 360$ slots on its code disk. The output pulse train measured by the photodetector has a frequency of $f = 2400 \text{ Hz}$. Calculate the crankshaft's rotational speed in RPM (revolutions per minute).

**Step 1: Recall the encoder frequency equation.**
Each slot transition (light → dark or dark → light) produces one pulse. Thus, one complete revolution of the disk produces $N$ pulses.
$$f = \frac{N \cdot \omega}{2\pi}$$

**Step 2: Solve for angular velocity $\omega$.**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot f}{N}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 2400}{360} = \frac{4800\pi}{360} = \frac{40\pi}{3} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Convert $\omega$ to RPM.**
Recall that $\omega$ in rad/s relates to RPM via:
$$N_{RPM} = \omega \times \frac{60}{2\pi}$$

**Step 5: Substitute $\omega$ into the conversion formula.**
$$N_{RPM} = \frac{40\pi}{3} \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{40 \times 60}{3 \times 2} = \frac{2400}{6} = 400 \text{ RPM}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{40\pi}{3} \text{ rad/s} \approx 41.89 \text{ rad/s}, \quad N_{RPM} = 400 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A crankshaft speed of 400 RPM corresponds to a low engine idle speed. The ECU uses this signal to synchronize fuel injection and ignition timing with precise piston position.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Optical Sensor Types

| **Parameter** | **LDR** | **Photodiode** | **Phototransistor** | **LiDAR** | **Camera (CMOS)** |
|---------------|---------|----------------|---------------------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Response Time** | Slow (50–100 ms) | Fast (ns range) | Medium ($\mu$s) | Very Fast | Medium (ms) |
| **Sensitivity** | Medium | Low (needs amplification) | High ($\times \beta$) | Very High (single photons possible) | High (array) |
| **Spectral Range** | Visible (CdS) | UV–NIR (Si: 200–1100 nm) | Same as photodiode | Single wavelength (905/1550 nm) | Visible + NIR |
| **Output Type** | Resistance change | Current | Current | Time-stamped pulses | Digital image |
| **Cost** | Very Low | Low | Low | Very High | Medium |
| **Automotive Application** | Twilight sensor | Rain sensor, encoder | Wheel speed sensor | ADAS, autonomous | Lane/pedestrian detection |
| **Operating Temperature** | -20°C to +70°C | -40°C to +125°C | -40°C to +125°C | -40°C to +85°C | -40°C to +105°C |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Photodiode Responsivity Calculation

> **Problem Statement:** A silicon photodiode has quantum efficiency $\eta = 0.85$ at wavelength $\lambda = 850 \text{ nm}$. Calculate its responsivity in A/W.

**Step 1: Use the responsivity formula.**
$$R_\lambda = \frac{\eta \cdot \lambda \cdot q}{h \cdot c}$$

**Step 2: Identify all constants.**
- $\eta = 0.85$
- $\lambda = 850 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 8.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J}\cdot\text{s}$
- $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 3: Substitute into the formula.**
$$R_\lambda = \frac{0.85 \times (8.5 \times 10^{-7}) \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3 \times 10^8)}$$

**Step 4: Calculate numerator.**
$$0.85 \times 8.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 11.57 \times 10^{-26} = 1.157 \times 10^{-25}$$

**Step 5: Calculate denominator.**
$$6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8 = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}$$

**Step 6: Divide.**
$$R_\lambda = \frac{1.157 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 0.582 \text{ A/W}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{R_\lambda = 0.582 \text{ A/W}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** For every 1 Watt of 850 nm optical power striking this photodiode, 0.582 Amperes of photocurrent is generated. This high responsivity is ideal for low-light automotive applications like night vision.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics
