topics.title: Relays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT501 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Relays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relay (IEC 60255-1 / ISO 7588-1 Definition):** An electrically operated, **electromagnetic switching device** in which a relatively low-power control current flowing through a coil magnetically attracts a movable armature to open or close one or more sets of electrical contacts, thereby switching a separate, often higher-power, load circuit.

In the context of **Automotive Electrical and Electronics Systems (PCMUT501)**, a relay is a *remote-controlled switch* that allows a low-current driver circuit (e.g., an ECU, a microcontroller output, or a manual switch) to control a high-current load circuit (e.g., headlamps, horn, fuel pump, starter solenoid auxiliary) **without routing the heavy current through the control switch itself**.

### 1.1.1 Automotive-Specific Definition (KTU 2024 Perspective)

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive Relay:** A normally-isolated, electromagnetic or solid-state switching element rated for **12 V DC** (conventional ICE vehicles) or **24 V DC** (heavy commercial / off-road vehicles) and used to multiplex, protect, and remotely actuate high-current actuators from low-current logic-level controllers while providing galvanic isolation, fault tolerance, and ease of serviceability.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a relay as a "lever-and-string" mechanism.**
>
> Imagine you (the small switch/ECU) are standing at the **driver's seat** and want to switch ON a **massive industrial floodlight** (the high-current load) located 5 meters away in the engine bay. You are too weak to push the heavy switch directly. So, you pull a **thin string** (the control coil) which, via a lever, **mechanically toggles the heavy switch** for you.
>
> - The **string you pull** = the **control circuit** (low current, e.g., 0.1 A)
> - The **lever** = the **electromagnetic armature**
> - The **heavy switch it toggles** = the **contact set** (handles 30 A–40 A)
> - The **floodlight** = the **automotive load** (headlamp, horn, fuel pump)
>
> You *never touch the heavy current* — the relay does the heavy lifting for you.

This is the essence of **galvanic isolation**: the control side and the load side are *electrically separate* but *magnetically/mechanically coupled*.

---

## 1.3 Why Relays Are Indispensable in Modern Automobiles

| Reason | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| **Current amplification** | ECU/MCU I/O pins typically source/sink **20 mA – 250 mA**, but loads demand **5 A – 40 A**. |
| **Switch contact protection** | Manual dashboard switches have small contacts; routing 20 A+ through them causes **arcing, pitting, and welding**. |
| **Wire-harness miniaturization** | Long runs of thin control wire replace thick battery cables, saving **mass, cost, and fuel**. |
| **Safety & Serviceability** | A failed relay is a cheap, plug-in **fuse-like service item**, far safer than a failed ECU. |
| **Logic multiplexing** | One ECU pin can control many relays sequentially (e.g., timed wiper stages). |
| **Voltage-drop minimisation** | Power runs directly from battery via short, heavy cables through relay contacts — *minimum* I²R loss. |

---

## 1.4 Standard Automotive Relay Ratings (KTU 2024 Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A typical **Bosch-type ISO mini-relay** used in passenger cars is rated at:
> - **Coil voltage:** 12 V DC (nominal)
> - **Coil resistance:** ~80 Ω (pull-in) and ~**360 Ω** (with economizer / resistor)
> - **Pull-in voltage:** ≤ **7.2 V** (60% of nominal)
> - **Drop-out voltage:** ≥ **1.2 V** (10% of nominal)
> - **Contact rating:** 30 A / 40 A continuous at **12 V DC**
> - **Operating temperature:** **−40 °C to +125 °C**
> - **Mechanical life:** 10⁷ operations
> - **Electrical life:** 10⁵ operations at full rated load

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of an Electromechanical Automotive Relay

A relay, in its most fundamental form, is composed of **four functional sub-assemblies**:

1. **Coil Assembly (Electromagnet)**
   - A bobbin wound with **enamelled copper wire** (typically AWG 32–36).
   - Connected to **two control terminals** (85 and 86 in ISO nomenclature).
   - When energised, generates a magnetomotive force (MMF) given by **F = N · I** where N = turns count and I = coil current.

2. **Magnetic Core & Yoke**
   - Made of **low-carbon steel or silicon-iron laminations** to minimise eddy-current losses.
   - Provides a low-reluctance flux path between the coil and the armature.

3. **Armature (Movable Contact Carrier)**
   - A pivoted ferromagnetic plate attracted by the coil's magnetic field.
   - Spring-loaded to return to the *normal* (de-energised) position.

4. **Contact Set (Load-Side Switch)**
   - **Common (COM)** terminal — usually pin **30**.
   - **Normally Open (NO)** — pin **87**.
   - **Normally Closed (NC)** — pin **87a** (only in changeover/PDT relays).
   - Contact materials: **AgSnO₂ (silver tin oxide)** for high inrush, or **AgNi (silver nickel)** for general purpose.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

> [!NOTE]
> The relay operates on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and **Lorentz Force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field**.

### Step-by-step energisation sequence:

1. **Driver command:** ECU pulls the control pin HIGH (or LOW, depending on topology).
2. **Coil energisation:** Current begins to flow through the coil, given by  
   $I_{coil}(t) = \dfrac{V_{bat}}{R_{coil}} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$  
   where the **electrical time constant** $\tau = L_{coil} / R_{coil}$.
3. **Flux build-up:** The MMF establishes a magnetic flux in the core, $\Phi(t) \propto N \cdot I_{coil}(t)$.
4. **Force on armature:** The magnetic force $F_{mag} = \dfrac{\Phi^2}{2\mu_0 A}$ overcomes the opposing spring force $F_{spring}$.
5. **Armature attraction & contact closure:** The NO contact closes; NC opens. Audible *click* and contact bounce (~1–2 ms).
6. **Load energisation:** Battery voltage is now applied to the load through the closed contacts.
7. **Steady state:** Coil current settles to $I_{coil,ss} = V_{bat}/R_{coil}$.

### De-energisation sequence:

1. **Driver releases:** Control pin goes to high-impedance.
2. **Field collapse:** The inductor opposes the change in current, generating a **back-EMF spike** of $V_L = -L \dfrac{di}{dt}$ (often 80–300 V, hence the **flyback/snubber diode** across the coil).
3. **Armature release:** Spring force returns armature to normal position.
4. **Contact opening:** Load is disconnected. A small **arc** is drawn across the contacts (worse with inductive/DC loads).

---

## 2.3 Types of Automotive Relays (Comprehensive Taxonomy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Coverage:** Students must be able to *classify, sketch, and explain application* of the following relay types.

### 2.3.1 By Switching Mechanism

| Type | Symbol | Operating Principle | Automotive Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SPST (Single Pole, Single Throw) — Form A** | ⊣⊢ | One NO contact, closes on energise | Headlamps, horn, fog lamps |
| **SPDT (Single Pole, Double Throw) — Form C / Changeover** | ⊣⊃ | One COM toggles between NO and NC | Wiper Hi/Lo, heated mirror |
| **DPST / DPDT** | Two poles | Two independent circuits switched together | Auxiliary cooling fans, dual horns |
| **Solid-State Relay (SSR)** | Triac/MOSFET symbol | Opto-isolated semiconductor switching, no moving parts | ECU-driven PWM loads, EV traction pre-charge |
| **Hybrid Relay** | Coil + SSR | Electromagnetic pull-in + semiconductor carry | EV pre-charge contactors, HV battery isolation |

### 2.3.2 By Packaging (ISO Standards)

- **ISO Mini Relay** — 5-pin or 4-pin, ~25 mm cube — most common in modern cars.
- **ISO Micro Relay** — 15 mm cube — for tight packaging (instrument clusters, body controllers).
- **Bosch-style** (rectangular, 5-pin) — legacy, but still in commercial vehicles.
- **PCB-mount relay** — soldered onto ECU/BCM internal PCBs (e.g., Panasonic AGN series).
- **Plug-in PCB relay (PC-pin)** — combines pluggable serviceability with PCB mounting.

### 2.3.3 By Special Function

- **Latching (Bistable) Relay** — retains position after coil de-energisation. Two coils (Set & Reset) or a single polarity-reversing coil. Used in **EV battery disconnect units (BDU)** and **memory circuits** (e.g., sunroof position).
- **Time-Delay Relay (ON-delay / OFF-delay)** — uses RC network or IC timer. Used in **intermittent wipers, headlamp "follow-me-home"** delay circuits.
- **Flasher Relay** — thermal or electronic, used in **turn-signal and hazard** circuits.
- **Current-Sensing Relay** — coil senses load current; trips on over-current. Used in **A/C compressor clutch protection**.
- **Smart / Intelligent Relay** — contains a microcontroller; can do diagnostics, soft-start, PWM dimming, and LIN/CAN communication. Found in **modern BCMs (Body Control Modules)**.

---

## 2.4 Relay Coil Transient Protection (Snubber Design)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiners' Favourite:** When asked to "explain the need for a flyback diode across a relay coil," present the following logic.

When a relay coil (a large inductor) is suddenly de-energised, the collapsing magnetic flux generates a **back-EMF** that can reach several hundred volts — enough to destroy sensitive ECU output drivers (MOSFETs/IGBTs).

**Solutions:**

1. **Flyback Diode (1N4007 / UF4007):** A reverse-biased diode across the coil provides a path for the inductive kick.  
   Clamping voltage ≈ V_coil + 0.7 V.

2. **Zener Diode (across coil, cathode to +):** Faster turn-off, slightly higher dissipation.

3. **RC Snubber (across contacts or coil):** Damps oscillations, suppresses arcing at contacts.

4. **TVS (Transient Voltage Suppressor):** Bidirectional, used in safety-critical ECUs.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table — it covers 90% of relay-related numerical questions.**

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coil steady-state current | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{bat}}{R_{coil}}$ | A | At rated 12 V, $R_{coil} = 80\ \Omega \Rightarrow I_{coil} = 0.15\ \text{A}$ |
| 2 | Coil power dissipation | $P_{coil} = I_{coil}^2 \cdot R_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{bat}^2}{R_{coil}}$ | W | Typically **1.8 W – 2.0 W** at 12 V |
| 3 | Coil energisation time-constant | $\tau = \dfrac{L_{coil}}{R_{coil}}$ | s | Determines pick-up delay |
| 4 | Inductive voltage spike | $V_L = -L_{coil} \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | V | Justifies flyback diode |
| 5 | Magnetic force on armature | $F_{mag} = \dfrac{B^2 \cdot A}{2\mu_0}$ | N | Where B = flux density, A = pole-face area |
| 6 | Pull-in voltage (typical) | $V_{pull\text{-}in} \approx 0.6 \cdot V_{nom}$ | V | ISO 7588-1: **≤ 7.2 V at 12 V nominal** |
| 7 | Drop-out voltage (typical) | $V_{drop\text{-}out} \approx 0.1 \cdot V_{nom}$ | V | ISO 7588-1: **≥ 1.2 V at 12 V nominal** |
| 8 | Contact voltage drop (closed) | $V_{drop} = I_{load} \cdot R_{contact}$ | V | $R_{contact} \le 100\ \text{m}\Omega$ typical |
| 9 | Contact power dissipation | $P_{contact} = I_{load}^2 \cdot R_{contact}$ | W | Heat sink design parameter |
| 10 | Power delivered to load | $P_{load} = V_{load} \cdot I_{load}$ | W | Useful output |
| 11 | Relay efficiency (approx.) | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{load}}{P_{load} + P_{coil} + P_{contact}}$ | — | Often **> 0.99** for power-hungry loads |
| 12 | Back-EMF energy stored in coil | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} L_{coil} I_{coil}^2$ | J | Must be dissipated by snubber |
| 13 | Snubber resistor sizing (rule-of-thumb) | $R_{snub} = \dfrac{V_{clamp}}{I_{coil}}$ | Ω | Limits discharge current |
| 14 | Time to open (drop-out time) | $t_{drop} = \dfrac{L}{R_{coil} \parallel R_{snub}} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{I_{peak}}{I_{hold}}\right)$ | s | Not commonly numerical |
| 15 | Inrush derating factor | $I_{cont,max} = \dfrac{I_{rated}}{k_{derate}}$ | A | $k_{derate} = 0.6 - 0.8$ for purely resistive loads with high inrush (e.g., incandescent) |

> **Engineering utility:** These equations govern the design of **automotive wire harnesses, fuse boxes, BCM firmware timing parameters, and PCB thermal budgets**. A 5 % voltage drop in the coil circuit can push $V_{coil}$ below pull-in voltage during cranking (where battery sags to ~6 V), causing *load-dump-induced relay chatter* — a well-known field-failure mode.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Relay Coil Current & Pull-in Delay

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU 2024 Style, 7 marks):**
> A 12 V automotive relay has a coil resistance of **80 Ω** and inductance of **0.4 H**. Compute:
> (a) The steady-state coil current and coil power dissipation.
> (b) The time required for the coil current to reach **80 %** of its final value.
> (c) The peak inductive kick voltage if the coil is switched off in **1 ms**.

### 3.1.1 Given Data

$$
V_{bat} = 12\ \text{V}, \quad R_{coil} = 80\ \Omega, \quad L_{coil} = 0.4\ \text{H}, \quad \Delta t = 1\ \text{ms}
$$

### 3.1.2 Part (a) — Steady-State Current & Power

**Step 1:** Apply Ohm's law to the DC coil circuit at steady state (inductor behaves as a short-circuit for DC).

$$
I_{ss} = \frac{V_{bat}}{R_{coil}}
$$

$$
I_{ss} = \frac{12}{80}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{ss} = 0.150\ \text{A} = 150\ \text{mA}}
$$

**Step 2:** Power dissipated in the coil (Joule heating).

$$
P_{coil} = V_{bat} \cdot I_{ss} = I_{ss}^2 \cdot R_{coil}
$$

$$
P_{coil} = 12 \times 0.150
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{coil} = 1.80\ \text{W}}
$$

### 3.1.3 Part (b) — Time to Reach 80 % of $I_{ss}$

**Step 1:** The coil follows a first-order RL charging transient.

$$
i(t) = I_{ss}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right), \quad \tau = \frac{L_{coil}}{R_{coil}}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the electrical time constant.

$$
\tau = \frac{0.4}{80}
$$

$$
\tau = 0.005\ \text{s} = 5\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $t$ such that $i(t) = 0.8 \, I_{ss}$.

$$
0.8 = 1 - e^{-t/\tau}
$$

$$
e^{-t/\tau} = 0.2
$$

$$
\frac{t}{\tau} = -\ln(0.2) = \ln(5) = 1.609
$$

$$
t = 1.609 \times 5\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{80\%} = 8.05\ \text{ms}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Part (c) — Inductive Kick Voltage

**Step 1:** The voltage across an inductor during current change is given by Faraday's law.

$$
V_L = L_{coil} \cdot \frac{di}{dt}
$$

**Step 2:** If the current falls from $I_{ss} = 0.150$ A to 0 in $\Delta t = 1$ ms (linear approximation),

$$
\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{0 - 0.150}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = -150\ \text{A/s}
$$

**Step 3:** The magnitude of the induced voltage is

$$
\mid V_L \mid = 0.4 \times 150 = 60\ \text{V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{kick} = -60\ \text{V} \;\;(\text{reversed polarity across coil})}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This 60 V spike is more than enough to destroy an unprotected ECU output MOSFET (rated 20 V $V_{DS}$). A **flyback diode** clamps it to **−0.7 V + 12 V = −11.3 V** relative to battery, safely within ECU limits.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Contact Voltage Drop & Load Power

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A headlamp relay feeds a **55 W halogen bulb** at **13.5 V** (alternator-charged). The closed contact resistance is **50 mΩ**. Compute:
> (a) Load current.
> (b) Voltage drop across the contacts.
> (c) Power lost in contacts and **percentage efficiency** of the relay path.
> (d) Voltage actually delivered to the bulb.

### 3.2.1 Given Data

$$
P_{bulb} = 55\ \text{W}, \quad V_{system} = 13.5\ \text{V}, \quad R_{contact} = 50\ \text{m}\Omega = 0.050\ \Omega
$$

### 3.2.2 Part (a) — Load Current

**Step 1:** Use $P = V \cdot I$ to find load current.

$$
I_{load} = \frac{P_{bulb}}{V_{system}} = \frac{55}{13.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{load} = 4.07\ \text{A}}
$$

### 3.2.3 Part (b) — Contact Voltage Drop

**Step 1:** Apply Ohm's law across the contact.

$$
V_{drop} = I_{load} \cdot R_{contact} = 4.07 \times 0.050
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{drop} = 0.204\ \text{V}}
$$

### 3.2.4 Part (c) — Power Lost and Efficiency

**Step 1:** Contact power dissipation.

$$
P_{contact} = I_{load}^2 \cdot R_{contact} = (4.07)^2 \times 0.050 = 16.6 \times 0.050
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{contact} = 0.83\ \text{W}}
$$

**Step 2:** Efficiency of the relay (ignoring coil losses for the contact path).

$$
\eta_{contact} = \frac{P_{bulb}}{P_{bulb} + P_{contact}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{contact} = \frac{55}{55 + 0.83} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{contact} = 98.5\%}
$$

### 3.2.5 Part (d) — Voltage Delivered to the Bulb

$$
V_{bulb} = V_{system} - V_{drop} = 13.5 - 0.204
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{bulb} = 13.30\ \text{V}}
$$

> **Engineering insight:** Even a tiny 50 mΩ contact drop matters at high currents. At **40 A** (starter relay), the same resistance dissipates $I^2 R = 80$ W and the voltage drop is **2 V** — enough to cause a "no-crank" complaint in cold weather.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Mechanical vs Solid-State Relays

| Parameter | Electromechanical Relay (EMR) | Solid-State Relay (SSR) | Hybrid Relay |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Switching element** | Moving armature + contacts | TRIAC / MOSFET / IGBT | EMR contact + parallel SSR |
| **Galvanic isolation** | Yes (inherent) | Yes (optical or transformer) | Yes |
| **Switching speed** | 5 – 20 ms | 0.05 – 0.5 ms | ~1 ms |
| **Contact bounce** | 1 – 2 ms | None | None on SSR, EMR still bounces |
| **Electrical life** | 10⁵ cycles | 10⁹ cycles | 10⁷ cycles |
| **Mechanical life** | 10⁷ cycles | N/A | 10⁷ cycles |
| **On-state voltage drop** | ~50 mV | ~1.5 V (MOSFET) / ~1.7 V (TRIAC) | ~50 mV (post-pull-in) |
| **Leakage (off-state)** | 0 (mechanical gap) | 1 – 10 mA | 0 |
| **AC mains handling** | Good | Excellent | Limited |
| **DC load breaking** | Good (with snubber) | Poor (needs MOSFET for DC) | Good |
| **Cost** | Low (₹30 – ₹150) | High (₹200 – ₹2000) | Very high |
| **Failure mode** | Welded contacts / open coil | Short circuit (thermal runaway) | Fail-safe open (EMR) |
| **Automotive uses** | Headlamps, horn, fans, pumps | ECU-driven PWM, sensor heaters, EV pre-charge | HV battery isolation (EV) |
| **Audible click** | Yes (diagnostic feature) | No (silent — *not* always desired) | Soft click |

---

## 3.4 Latching Relay — Energy Analysis

> **Why use latching relays?**
> A conventional relay consumes **1.8 W continuously** to hold its contacts. A latching relay consumes **zero holding power** — the position is maintained by *permanent magnet* latching. For an EV battery disconnect unit (BDU) that must remain closed for years without supervision, latching relays save significant energy.

The energy to *switch* a latching relay (one pulse):

$$
E_{pulse} = P_{coil} \times t_{pulse} = 1.8 \times 0.020 = 0.036\ \text{J}
$$

For a conventional relay held for 1 hour:

$$
E_{conv} = 1.8 \times 3600 = 6480\ \text{J}
$$

**Energy saving ratio:** ~180,000 : 1 over a continuous-hold scenario.

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Magnetic Pull-in Force (Conceptual)

The energy stored in the magnetic field of the air gap is

$$
E_{field} = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0} \cdot V_{gap}
$$

where $V_{gap} = A \cdot \delta$ is the air-gap volume.

The force on the armature is the *negative gradient* of this energy with respect to gap length $\delta$:

$$
F_{mag} = -\frac{dE_{field}}{d\delta} = \frac{B^2 \cdot A}{2\mu_0}
$$

> This is why **larger pole-face area A** and **higher flux density B** yield stronger relays. Designers often use **shaped pole faces** and **graded air gaps** to optimise pull-in characteristics.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Automotive Relay Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    batA[Battery 12V] -->|B+ Heavy Cable| fuseA[Fuse Box]
    fuseA -->|Pin 30 Battery| relayA[Relay Coil + Contact Assembly]
    ecuA[ECU / Switch Control Pin] -->|Low Current Signal| driverA[ECU Output Driver MOSFET]
    driverA -->|Pin 85| relayA
    relayA -->|Pin 86| gndA[Chassis Ground]
    relayA -->|Pin 87 Output| loadA[Load Bulb Pump Motor]
    loadA --> gndA
    diodeA[Flyback Diode 1N4007] -.->|Reverse Biased Across Coil| relayA
    snubA[RC Snubber] -.->|Across Contacts| relayA

    classDef power fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef signal fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef protect fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class batA,fuseA,relayA power
    class ecuA,driverA signal
    class loadA load
    class diodeA,snubA protect
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Yellow path** = high-current power (battery → relay → load)
> - **Blue path** = low-current signal (ECU → driver → coil)
> - **Green node** = the load (bulb, pump, motor)
> - **Pink elements** = protection components (flyback diode, RC snubber)
> - **Dashed lines** = protective / parasitic connections, not part of the main power flow.

---

## 4.2 Relay Control State Machine (ECU Perspective)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF
    OFF --> CHARGING: Driver Command ON<br>Pin 85 driven HIGH
    CHARGING --> PICKUP: Coil current reaches Ipickup<br>t approx 8 ms
    PICKUP --> ON: Contacts closed<br>Load energised
    ON --> HOLDING: Continuous coil energisation<br>Pcoil approx 1.8 W
    HOLDING --> DROPOUT: Driver command OFF<br>Pin 85 set to HiZ
    DROPOUT --> OFF: Contacts open<br>Back EMF clamped by diode
    OFF --> [*]
    HOLDING --> FAULT_OPEN: Overcurrent detected<br>ECU deasserts
    HOLDING --> FAULT_WELD: Contacts welded<br>Load remains ON
    FAULT_WELD --> [*]
```

> **Key engineering note:** Many modern BCMs implement a *diagnostic feedback* — they monitor the actual voltage on pin 87 to detect a *welded contact* (always ON despite command OFF) or an *open coil* (never ON despite command ON). This is the basis of **ISO 14229 (UDS) fault codes P0xxx** for relay circuits.

---

## 4.3 ISO Mini-Relay Pinout Schematic (5-pin SPDT)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ISO_MINI[ISO MINI RELAY 5-PIN TOP VIEW]
        pin85[Pin 85<br>Coil +]
        pin86[Pin 86<br>Coil -]
        pin30[Pin 30<br>Battery In COM]
        pin87[Pin 87<br>Normally Open]
        pin87a[Pin 87a<br>Normally Closed]
    end
    pin30 --- pin87
    pin30 --- pin87a
    pin85 --- pin86

    pin30 -->|From Battery via Fuse| batB[B+ 12V]
    pin87 -->|To Load| loadB[Headlamp Bulb]
    pin87a -.->|To Alternate Load NC| loadC[Idle Lamp]
    pin85 -->|From ECU Driver| ecuB[ECU Output]
    pin86 -->|To Ground| gndB[Chassis GND]
```

> **Pin numbering convention (ISO 7588-1):**
> - **30** = Battery feed (input)
> - **85** = Coil terminal A
> - **86** = Coil terminal B
> - **87** = Output to load (NO)
> - **87a** = Output to alternate load (NC) — *present only in SPDT variants*

> **Reading guide:**

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Two compulsory questions of 3 marks each, expected answer length 3–5 sentences plus a diagram/equation where relevant.

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, 3 marks]`
**Define an automotive relay and list any four typical applications in a modern passenger car.**

**Model Answer:**
An automotive relay is an **electromagnetically operated switch** that allows a low-current control circuit to switch a high-current load circuit while providing **galvanic isolation** between the two. The control side (coil) typically draws 100–200 mA at 12 V, while the contact side can switch 20–40 A. Four typical applications are:

1. **Headlamp control** — driver-side switch sends a tiny current to the relay coil; the relay closes the high-current path to the bulbs.
2. **Horn actuation** — the steering-wheel horn pad energises a relay that supplies ~10 A to the horn coil.
3. **Fuel-pump switching** — the ECU energises the FP relay only during cranking and engine running (safety).
4. **Radiator cooling-fan** — the BCM or PCM commands dual relays for low-speed and high-speed fan operation.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, 3 marks]`
**Explain why a flyback diode is connected across a relay coil driven by an ECU. What is the consequence of omitting it?**

**Model Answer:**
A relay coil is a **large inductor** storing magnetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$. When the ECU's output MOSFET switches OFF, the collapsing flux induces a **back-EMF spike** $V_L = -L \, di/dt$, which can reach **60–300 V** in automotive coils. This spike can **avalanche-break the ECU's driver transistor**, corrupt neighbouring circuit nodes, and inject EMI into the CAN bus.

A **flyback diode** (typically 1N4007) connected **reverse-biased across the coil** provides a free-wheeling path for the inductive current, clamping the spike to **$V_{bat} + 0.7\ \text{V}$** and safely dissipating the stored energy as heat in the coil resistance.

*Consequence of omission:* **ECU output-stage failure** within a few hundred switching cycles — a well-documented warranty failure in 1990s vehicles.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern: each Part-B question carries 14 marks, divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks, with internal choice. **Two full alternative questions are given below for student practice.**

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2023]`

> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working principle of an SPST electromagnetic automotive relay**. Label the parts: coil, core, armature, spring, NO contact, common terminal. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A 12 V relay has a coil resistance of **90 Ω** and inductance of **0.3 H**. It is used to switch a **60 W headlamp**. The contact resistance is **40 mΩ**. Compute:
>     (i) Steady-state coil current and coil power.
>     (ii) Load current and voltage drop across the contacts.
>     (iii) Percentage of battery power delivered to the load. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Construction (diagram — see Section 4.3):** A 12 V electromagnetic SPST relay consists of:

1. **Coil** wound on a plastic bobbin, terminated at pins **85** and **86**.
2. **Iron core / yoke** providing a low-reluctance magnetic circuit.
3. **Spring-loaded armature** pivoted at one end, carrying the moving contact.
4. **Stationary contact** at pin **87** (NO).
5. **Common terminal** at pin **30** connected to the battery via a fuse.
6. **Contact housing** of heat-resistant plastic (e.g., PA66 GF30) and arc-quenching chamber.

**Working principle:**
- When 12 V is applied across 85–86, current $\sim$ 130 mA flows through the coil.
- This generates an MMF $F = N I$ that magnetises the core.
- The resulting flux $\Phi$ produces a force $F_{mag} = B^2 A / 2\mu_0$ on the armature.
- When $F_{mag}$ exceeds the spring tension, the armature snaps to the core, closing the NO contact.
- The closed contact (resistance ~40 mΩ) applies battery voltage to the load.
- When the coil is de-energised, the spring returns the armature; a flyback diode absorbs the inductive spike.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Given:** $V_{bat} = 12\ \text{V}$, $R_{coil} = 90\ \Omega$, $L_{coil} = 0.3\ \text{H}$, $P_{load} = 60\ \text{W}$, $R_{contact} = 0.040\ \Omega$.

**(i) Coil current and power:**

$$
I_{coil} = \frac{V_{bat}}{R_{coil}} = \frac{12}{90} = 0.133\ \text{A}
$$

$$
P_{coil} = V_{bat} \cdot I_{coil} = 12 \times 0.133 = 1.60\ \text{W}
$$

**(ii) Load current and contact drop:**  
Assuming $V_{system} = 13.5\ \text{V}$ (engine running) for the load calculation:

$$
I_{load} = \frac{P_{load}}{V_{system}} = \frac{60}{13.5} = 4.44\ \text{A}
$$

$$
V_{drop} = I_{load} \cdot R_{contact} = 4.44 \times 0.040 = 0.178\ \text{V}
$$

**(iii) Power delivered vs. battery power supplied:**

$$
P_{battery} = V_{bat} \cdot (I_{coil} + I_{load}) \;\; \text{(simplified, both paths independent)}
$$

Total battery power to the relay box = $P_{coil} + P_{load\_at\_12V}$. More meaningfully, the **load-path efficiency** is:

$$
\eta_{load} = \frac{P_{load}}{P_{load} + P_{contact}} = \frac{60}{60 + (4.44)^2 \times 0.040} = \frac{60}{60 + 0.789}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{load} = 98.7\%}
$$

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Compare **electromechanical relays (EMR)** and **solid-state relays (SSR)** under the following heads: switching speed, electrical life, on-state voltage drop, galvanic isolation, failure mode, and suitability for DC loads. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** With a circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **latching (bistable) relay** used in an **electric vehicle's battery disconnect unit (BDU)**. Why is a latching relay preferred over a conventional relay in this application? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

*(See full comparison matrix in Section 3.3 for the table-based answer; the exam-friendly form is below.)*

| Parameter | EMR | SSR |
|---|---|---|
| **Switching speed** | 5–20 ms (slow, mechanical inertia) | 0.05–0.5 ms (100× faster) |
| **Electrical life** | 10⁵ cycles (contact erosion) | 10⁹ cycles (no wear) |
| **On-state drop** | 50–100 mV (low loss) | 1.5–2 V (MOSFET/TRIAC) |
| **Galvanic isolation** | Inherent (mechanical) | Optical / transformer |
| **Failure mode** | Fail-open (safe) or contact weld | Short-circuit (unsafe) |
| **DC load breaking** | Good (with snubber) | Poor — needs MOSFET-based SSR |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **EMI** | Low (mechanical) | Higher (dv/dt, leakage) |
| **Audible click** | Yes (diagnostic benefit) | No |

**Conclusion:** EMRs dominate **automotive switching of high DC currents** (starter, headlamps, fans) where low conduction loss and fail-safe behaviour are paramount. SSRs dominate **PWM-controlled or fast-switching applications** (LED drivers, sensor heaters) where mechanical bounce is unacceptable.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Latching Relay in EV BDU — Circuit Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    bduMain[Battery Pack 400V] --> contMain[Main Contactor Latching Relay]
    contMain --> invMain[Inverter DC Link]
    coilSet[Set Coil 12V] --> contMain
    coilRst[Reset Coil 12V] --> contMain
    bmsMain[BMS Controller] -->|100ms Pulse| coilSet
    bmsMain -->|100ms Pulse| coilRst
    permMag1[Permanent Magnet] -.->|Holds Position| contMain
```

**Operation:**
- The BDU houses a **latching relay (or contactor)** with two coils — **Set** and **Reset**.
- To close: BMS sends a brief **Set pulse** (~50–200 ms) that magnetises the armature. The armature is then *held* by a **permanent magnet** — no continuous coil power required.
- To open: BMS sends a **Reset pulse** of opposite polarity, which overcomes the permanent magnet and releases the armature.
- The high-current contacts (typically 200–600 A continuous) now connect or disconnect the 400 V traction pack from the inverter.

**Why latching is preferred:**

1. **Zero holding power** — critical in a parked EV where the HV system is isolated for weeks/months. A conventional relay would drain the 12 V battery in days.
2. **Safety** — in a crash, the BMS can *guarantee* the relay opens by sending a Reset pulse even if the 12 V supply is partially compromised.
3. **Thermal budget** — no continuous coil heating allows the BDU enclosure to be smaller and lighter.
4. **ISO 6469-3 / AIS-156 compliance** — EV safety standards *require* fail-safe isolation; latching relays meet this elegantly.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in relay questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the flyback diode** — Always mention it when describing any ECU-driven relay circuit. Examiners look for this in any 7-mark question. *(−2 marks if missed.)*
>
> 2. **Confusing pin numbers 85/86 and 30/87** — ISO 7588-1 is the global standard; using non-standard pin numbering costs marks in sketch questions.
>
> 3. **Using RMS instead of peak voltage** in back-EMF calculations — Remember that inductive spikes are *instantaneous peak* values, not RMS.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the difference between SPST and SPDT** — A 4-pin relay has no NC (87a) terminal; a 5-pin does. State this clearly in diagrams.
>
> 5. **Not stating the pull-in / drop-out voltage thresholds** — These are *standardised* values (60 % / 10 % of nominal). Merely saying "the relay switches ON" loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 6. **Mixing up EMR and SSR advantages** — Students often claim SSRs are "always better" — they are not, especially for DC high-current automotive loads. Always present a *balanced* comparison.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the pre-charge circuit** in EV BDU answers — A pure latching relay cannot switch the inrush of an empty DC link capacitor. Examiners expect you to mention the **pre-charge resistor + small contactor** in parallel.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and pin it above your study desk.**

- [x] A relay is an **electromagnetic switch** that uses a low-current control signal to switch a high-current load, with **galvanic isolation** between the two sides.
- [x] **Pin numbering (ISO 7588-1):** **30** = Battery, **85 & 86** = Coil, **87** = NO output, **87a** = NC output (5-pin SPDT only).
- [x] **Pull-in voltage** ≈ 60 % of nominal (≤ 7.2 V for 12 V relay); **Drop-out voltage** ≈ 10 % (≥ 1.2 V).
- [x] **Coil current** $I = V/R$; typical 12 V relay draws **100–200 mA**; dissipates **1.5–2 W**.
- [x] **Inductive back-EMF** $V = -L \, di/dt$ — always present at turn-off; clamped by **flyback diode** (clamp ≈ $V_{bat} + 0.7\ \text{V}$).
- [x] **Time constant** $\tau = L/R$; relay typically takes **5–10 ms** to pick up (mechanical + electrical delays).
- [x] **Contact resistance** is in **mΩ** (≤ 100 mΩ); at 40 A this can still drop **> 2 V** — critical for starter circuits.
- [x] **Types to know:** SPST/SPDT/DPDT, latching, time-delay, flasher, current-sensing, solid-state, hybrid.
- [x] **SSR** = no moving parts, fast, silent, long life, but **on-state drop higher** and **DC breaking is poor**.
- [x] **Latching relay** consumes **zero holding power** — essential in **EV BDU** and memory circuits.
- [x] **Smart/Intelligent relays** (in BCMs) provide **diagnostics, PWM dimming, and LIN/CAN communication**.
- [x] **Failure modes:** EMR → welded contacts (fail-ON, dangerous) or open coil (fail-OFF, safe); SSR → short circuit (fail-ON, dangerous).
- [x] **Automotive loads commonly relayed:** headlamps, horn, fuel pump, AC clutch, radiator fan (Lo/Hi), starter solenoid aux, heated rear window, blower motor, fog lamps.
- [x] **Modern trend:** relays being **replaced by power-MOSFETs inside BCMs/PDMs (Power Distribution Modules)** for high-current loads, but **electromechanical relays still dominate** the highest-current (> 30 A) and most cost-sensitive circuits.
- [x] **Standards to cite in answers:** ISO 7588-1, ISO 7588-2, IEC 60255-1, AEC-Q100/Q101 (for automotive-grade components).
- [x] **Exam mantra:** *"A relay protects the switch, not the load."* — if you remember only one line, remember this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT501 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piezoelectric Effect:** The **Piezoelectric Effect** is the ability of certain crystalline materials to generate an internal electric charge (and thus a measurable potential difference across their faces) when subjected to a mechanical stress. Conversely, the same class of materials physically deforms when an external electric field is applied across them. The prefix "Piezo" is derived from the Greek word *"piezein"*, meaning *to press or squeeze*.

In the context of **Automotive Electrical and Electronics Systems (PCMUT501)**, piezoelectric devices act as **bidirectional electromechanical transducers** — converting mechanical energy (vibration, pressure, knock, acceleration) into electrical energy (sensing) and electrical energy into precise mechanical motion (actuation). They belong to the broader family of *smart materials* and form the sensing backbone of modern Electronic Control Units (ECUs).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight:** Piezoelectricity is the underlying principle for the **Knock Sensor**, **Crankshaft Position Sensor**, **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) Sensor**, **Accelerometer**, and **Piezoelectric Fuel Injectors** in passenger cars and heavy commercial vehicles.

---

## 1.2 The Two Reciprocal Effects (Direct & Converse)

| **Effect Name** | **Energy Conversion** | **Direction** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Direct Piezoelectric Effect** | Mechanical → Electrical | Sensor (Generator Mode) |
| **Converse Piezoelectric Effect** | Electrical → Mechanical | Actuator (Motor Mode) |
| **Inverse Piezoelectric Effect** | Electrical → Mechanical | Alternate name for Converse |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Squeezed Lemon and the Electric Toothbrush"

### Real-World Analogy 1: The Squeezed Crystal (Direct Effect)
Imagine a **transparent lemon-shaped balloon filled with positive charges on one side and negative charges on the other**. When the balloon is at rest, the internal charges are perfectly balanced, and no voltage appears at the terminals. Now, **squeeze the balloon firmly** with your fingers. The internal charge distribution shifts asymmetrically, and a tiny voltage appears across the surface electrodes. This is exactly what happens inside a **piezoelectric crystal** — a mechanical nudge disturbs the lattice, displaces charges, and produces a measurable voltage.

### Real-World Analogy 2: The Piezoelectric Lighter (Actuator)
A standard **gas lighter** in a kitchen contains a small piezoelectric crystal. When you press the trigger, a spring-loaded hammer strikes the crystal. The mechanical impact generates a **>10 kV pulse** that arcs across a small air gap and lights the gas. The same principle, reversed and miniaturized, drives **automotive piezoelectric fuel injectors** that can perform multiple injections per combustion cycle with sub-millisecond precision.

### Real-World Analogy 3: The Talking Drum Membrane
A **drum skin** vibrates when struck (mechanical input) and pushes air to produce sound (mechanical output). Similarly, a piezoelectric disc vibrates when excited by an AC voltage at its resonance frequency and produces sound waves — this is the operating principle of **ultrasonic parking sensors** and **reverse horns** in modern cars.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental metrics that any KTU examiner expects you to know:
> - **Piezoelectric Charge Coefficient ($d_{ij}$):** Measured in **Coulomb per Newton (C/N)** or **meter per Volt (m/V)**. Typical value for **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT): $d_{33} \approx 300 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N**.
> - **Relative Permittivity ($\varepsilon_r$):** For PZT-5A, $\varepsilon_r \approx 1700$.
> - **Coupling Factor ($k$):** A dimensionless metric representing the efficiency of energy conversion. For PZT, $k \approx 0.5$ to $0.7$.
> - **Curie Temperature ($T_C$):** The temperature above which the material loses its piezoelectric properties. For PZT, $T_C \approx 350^\circ$C. For Barium Titanate, $T_C \approx 120^\circ$C.
> - **Resonance Frequency ($f_r$):** Typically in the range of **1 kHz to 10 MHz** for automotive sensors.

---

## 1.5 Why the Effect Exists — Crystal Class Physics

The piezoelectric effect is observed only in **non-centrosymmetric crystals** — that is, crystals that **do not possess a center of symmetry** in their unit cell. When a centrosymmetric crystal is deformed, the positive and negative charge centers remain coincident, producing no net dipole moment.

Common piezoelectric materials used in automotive applications:
- **Quartz ($SiO_2$):** Highly stable, used in precise timing and pressure sensors.
- **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT):** Highest piezoelectric coefficient, used in knock sensors and fuel injectors.
- **Barium Titanate ($BaTiO_3$):** Used in ultrasonic transducers.
- **Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF):** A flexible polymer film used in crash sensors and seat-belt pretensioners.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Constitutive Equations of Piezoelectricity

The complete mathematical description of a piezoelectric material is given by a coupled set of equations linking **electrical variables** (Electric field $E$, Electric displacement $D$) with **mechanical variables** (Stress $T$, Strain $S$). These are the **constitutive equations** of piezoelectricity and form the analytical heart of this topic.

In matrix form, the linear piezoelectric constitutive equations are:

$$D = d \cdot T + \varepsilon^T \cdot E$$

$$S = s^E \cdot T + d_t \cdot E$$

Where:
- $D$ = Electric displacement (C/m²)
- $T$ = Mechanical stress (N/m²)
- $E$ = Electric field (V/m)
- $S$ = Mechanical strain (dimensionless, m/m)
- $d$ = Piezoelectric strain coefficient (C/N or m/V)
- $d_t$ = Transpose of the piezoelectric coefficient matrix
- $\varepsilon^T$ = Permittivity at constant stress (F/m)
- $s^E$ = Elastic compliance at constant electric field (m²/N)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The first equation describes the **Direct Effect** (mechanical stress creates an electric displacement — used in sensors). The second equation describes the **Converse Effect** (electric field creates mechanical strain — used in actuators). Notice that **the same coefficient $d$ governs both effects** — this is a thermodynamic consequence of the **Maxwell reciprocity relation**.

---

## 2.2 The Piezoelectric Coefficient Matrix (Tensor Form)

The coefficient $d$ is not a single scalar — it is a **3 × 6 matrix** because the crystal has 3 electrical axes ($x_1, x_2, x_3$) and 6 independent mechanical stress/strain components. For a poled ferroelectric ceramic (used in 99% of automotive applications), the matrix simplifies to:

$$d = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & d_{15} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & d_{15} & 0 & 0 \\ d_{31} & d_{31} & d_{33} & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Where:
- $d_{33}$ = Charge generated per unit force applied along the poling (3) axis.
- $d_{31}$ = Charge generated per unit force applied perpendicular to the poling axis.
- $d_{15}$ = Shear-mode coefficient.

For a poled ceramic disc, the most commonly used coefficient is **$d_{33}$**, since force is typically applied along the poling direction.

---

## 2.3 Sensor Mode — Charge Generation Under Force

When a force $F$ is applied to a piezoelectric element of area $A$ and thickness $t$, the generated charge $Q$ is:

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot A \cdot \sigma$$

Where $\sigma = F/A$ is the applied mechanical stress. The corresponding open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ is:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F \cdot t}{\varepsilon_r \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Where $C_p$ is the capacitance of the piezoelectric element:** $C_p = \varepsilon_r \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot A / t$.
> This is the working principle of the **automotive knock sensor** in the engine block.

---

## 2.4 Actuator Mode — Strain Generation Under Voltage

When a voltage $V$ is applied across a piezoelectric element of thickness $t$:

$$S = d_{33} \cdot E = d_{33} \cdot \frac{V}{t}$$

The mechanical displacement $\Delta L$ is:

$$\Delta L = S \cdot L = d_{33} \cdot V \cdot \frac{L}{t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the working principle of **piezoelectric fuel injectors** in common-rail diesel engines — a stack of thin PZT discs applies a precise, repeatable mechanical push to the injector needle.

---

## 2.5 Resonance Frequency of a Piezoelectric Disc

For a thin disc of diameter $D$ vibrating in its radial mode, the resonance frequency is given by:

$$f_r = \frac{\eta}{D} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{\rho \cdot s_{11}^E}}$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = A dimensionless constant ($\eta \approx 2.05$ for the fundamental radial mode of a free disc).
- $\rho$ = Density of the piezoelectric material (kg/m³).
- $s_{11}^E$ = Elastic compliance at constant electric field (m²/N).

For a thickness-mode resonator (used in ultrasonic sensors), the resonance frequency is:

$$f_t = \frac{1}{2t} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{c_{33}^D}{\rho}}$$

Where $c_{33}^D$ is the elastic stiffness at constant electric displacement.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Variables** | **Application** | **Units** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ | $Q$: Charge, $F$: Force, $d_{33}$: Coefficient | Knock sensor, accelerometer | C, N, C/N |
| $V_{oc} = Q / C_p$ | $V_{oc}$: Open-circuit voltage, $C_p$: Capacitance | Sensor signal output | V, F |
| $C_p = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A / t$ | $A$: Area, $t$: thickness, $\varepsilon_r$: relative permittivity | Equivalent circuit | F |
| $S = d_{33} \cdot V / t$ | $S$: strain, $V$: voltage, $t$: thickness | Actuator displacement | dimensionless, V, m |
| $\Delta L = d_{33} \cdot V \cdot (L / t)$ | $L$: length, $t$: thickness | Injector needle stroke | m |
| $f_r = (\eta / D) \sqrt{1 / (\rho s_{11}^E)}$ | $D$: diameter, $\rho$: density | Radial resonance | Hz |
| $f_t = (1 / 2t) \sqrt{c_{33}^D / \rho}$ | $t$: thickness, $c_{33}^D$: stiffness | Thickness resonance | Hz |
| $k^2 = d_{33}^2 / (s_{33}^E \varepsilon_{33}^T)$ | $k$: coupling factor | Efficiency metric | dimensionless |
| $F_{max} = A \cdot \sigma_{max}$ | $\sigma_{max}$: max stress | Mechanical limit | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Strategy for KTU Exam:** Focus on the first **three formulas** in the table — they cover **~80% of marks** asked in piezoelectric numerical problems. The remaining formulas appear in derivation-type 14-mark questions.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Automotive Engineering Utility

The piezoelectric principle enables some of the most safety-critical and performance-critical systems in modern vehicles:

1. **Knock Sensor (KS):** Mounted on the engine block, detects abnormal combustion knock vibrations and feeds the signal to the ECU to retard ignition timing — improving fuel efficiency and preventing engine damage.
2. **Piezoelectric Fuel Injector:** Used in modern common-rail diesel systems (e.g., Bosch piezo injectors) for multiple precise injections per cycle.
3. **Crash Sensor (Airbag):** PVDF film sensors detect the rapid deceleration pulse during a frontal crash and trigger airbag deployment within **< 15 ms**.
4. **Parking Distance Sensor (PDS):** Ultrasonic piezoelectric transducers emit and receive 40–58 kHz sound waves to detect obstacles during reversing.
5. **Seat Belt Pretensioner:** A small pyrotechnic + piezo sensor combination tightens the belt in a crash.
6. **Engine Balance Shaft Actuator:** PZT stacks balance out engine vibrations for NVH refinement in luxury vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Open-Circuit Voltage of a Piezoelectric Sensor

**Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ generated by a piezoelectric disc of area $A$ and thickness $t$ when a force $F$ is applied along the poling axis (3-direction).

### Step 1 — Define the Mechanical Stress
The applied force $F$ acts on the cross-sectional area $A$ perpendicular to the poling axis. The mechanical stress $\sigma$ on the crystal is:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Direct Piezoelectric Effect
The direct piezoelectric effect gives the electric displacement $D_3$ generated along the poling axis as:

$$D_3 = d_{33} \cdot \sigma$$

Substituting Step 1:

$$D_3 = d_{33} \cdot \frac{F}{A}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Electric Displacement to Charge
The total charge $Q$ generated on the electrode face is the electric displacement multiplied by the area:

$$Q = D_3 \cdot A = d_{33} \cdot F$$

This is the fundamental **charge-force relationship** for a piezoelectric sensor.

### Step 4 — Determine the Capacitance of the Disc
A piezoelectric disc acts as a parallel-plate capacitor with the electrodes on its two faces. The capacitance is:

$$C_p = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{t}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Open-Circuit Voltage
For a charge $Q$ on a capacitor of capacitance $C_p$, the voltage is $V = Q / C_p$. Therefore:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{t}}$$

### Step 6 — Simplify
Multiplying numerator and denominator:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}$$

This is the **final expression** for the open-circuit sensor voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Voltage is directly proportional to the **force and thickness** and inversely proportional to the **area and permittivity**. This is why automotive knock sensors are designed as **thin, small-area discs** to maximize voltage sensitivity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mechanical Displacement of a Piezoelectric Actuator

**Problem Statement:** Show that the free mechanical displacement of a piezoelectric stack actuator of length $L$ and thickness per layer $t$ under applied voltage $V$ is $\Delta L = d_{33} \cdot V \cdot n$, where $n$ is the number of layers.

### Step 1 — Strain Generated by a Single Layer
The converse piezoelectric effect gives the strain in a single PZT layer of thickness $t$ under an applied electric field $E$:

$$S = d_{33} \cdot E$$

The electric field across a single layer of thickness $t$ with applied voltage $V_{layer}$ is:

$$E = \frac{V_{layer}}{t}$$

Therefore:

$$S = d_{33} \cdot \frac{V_{layer}}{t}$$

### Step 2 — Displacement of a Single Layer
The mechanical displacement of a single layer of thickness $t$ is:

$$\Delta t = S \cdot t = d_{33} \cdot V_{layer}$$

### Step 3 — Displacement of a Stack
A stack actuator consists of $n$ layers connected electrically in parallel (so each layer sees the same voltage $V_{layer}$), but mechanically in series. The total displacement is the sum of the displacements of all layers:

$$\Delta L = n \cdot \Delta t = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V_{layer}$$

### Step 4 — Voltage Distribution
In automotive injectors, all $n$ layers are driven by the same total voltage $V$, and each layer typically sees $V / n$. To maximize the engineering parameter, we often express the result in terms of the total applied voltage $V$ across the stack:

$$V_{layer} = \frac{V}{n}$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot \frac{V}{n} = d_{33} \cdot V$$

If all layers are connected in series electrically:

$$\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why stack actuators use many **thin** PZT layers — the displacement scales with the number of layers, and using thin layers allows a low driving voltage to generate a high electric field per layer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Radial Resonance Frequency of a Piezoelectric Disc

**Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for the radial-mode resonance frequency $f_r$ of a thin piezoelectric disc.

### Step 1 — Equation of Motion for Radial Vibration
The disc is modelled as a thin circular plate with diameter $D$ undergoing radial extensional vibration. The relevant elastic constant is the planar elastic compliance $s_{11}^E$ (at constant electric field). The wave equation in the radial direction is:

$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = \frac{1}{\rho \cdot s_{11}^E} \cdot \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial r^2}$$

Where $u(r,t)$ is the radial displacement and $\rho$ is the material density.

### Step 2 — Wave Speed
The longitudinal wave speed $v$ in the radial direction is:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\rho \cdot s_{11}^E}}$$

### Step 3 — Boundary Conditions
For a free-free disc, the boundary condition is **zero stress** at the circumference. Solving the wave equation with this boundary condition yields Bessel function solutions, and the fundamental mode satisfies the transcendental equation $J_1(\xi) = 0$ where $\xi = \pi D f_r / v$. The first non-trivial root is $\xi_1 \approx 2.05$ (denoted as $\eta$).

### Step 4 — Solve for Resonance Frequency
Setting $\xi_1 = \eta = 2.05$ and $v = 1 / \sqrt{\rho s_{11}^E}$:

$$\eta = \pi \cdot D \cdot f_r \cdot \sqrt{\rho \cdot s_{11}^E}$$

Solving for $f_r$:

$$f_r = \frac{\eta}{\pi \cdot D} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\rho \cdot s_{11}^E}}$$

A common engineering approximation is:

$$f_r \approx \frac{2.05}{D} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{\rho \cdot s_{11}^E}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Question:** A PZT-5A piezoelectric disc has the following specifications:
- Diameter $D = 10$ mm $= 0.01$ m
- Thickness $t = 1$ mm $= 0.001$ m
- $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N
- $\varepsilon_r = 1700$
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m

A force of $F = 50$ N is applied. Calculate:
(a) The charge generated.
(b) The capacitance of the disc.
(c) The open-circuit voltage.

### Part (a) — Charge Generated
$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F = (400 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 50$$

$$Q = 20{,}000 \times 10^{-12} = 2 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{C} = 20\ \text{nC}$$

### Part (b) — Capacitance
Area of the disc: $A = \pi D^2 / 4 = \pi (0.01)^2 / 4 = 7.854 \times 10^{-5}$ m²

$$C_p = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{t} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1700)(7.854 \times 10^{-5})}{0.001}$$

Numerator: $8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1700 = 1.505 \times 10^{-8}$
$1.505 \times 10^{-8} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5} = 1.182 \times 10^{-12}$

$$C_p = \frac{1.182 \times 10^{-12}}{0.001} = 1.182 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{F} = 1.182\ \text{nF}$$

### Part (c) — Open-Circuit Voltage
$$V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-8}}{1.182 \times 10^{-9}} = 16.92\ \text{V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always carry the units explicitly through the calculation. The final answer in Volts is a typical 3-mark sub-question in KTU 2024 scheme exams.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Piezoelectric Working Principle (Direct & Converse Effect)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Mechanical Force F] -->|Applied to Crystal| nodeB[Poled Piezoelectric Crystal]
    nodeB -->|Charge Displacement D = d33 x sigma| nodeC[Electric Charge Q on Electrodes]
    nodeC -->|Output Voltage Voc = Q / Cp| nodeD[Signal Conditioning Circuit]
    nodeD -->|Amplified and Filtered| nodeE[ECU Input]

    nodeF[External Voltage Source V] -->|Applied to Electrodes| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Strain S = d33 x E| nodeG[Mechanical Displacement deltaL]
    nodeG -->|Force Generation| nodeH[Mechanical Load]

    nodeA -.Direct Piezoelectric Effect.-> nodeC
    nodeF -.Converse Piezoelectric Effect.-> nodeH
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Automotive Knock Sensor System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Engine Block Vibration Source] -->|Mechanical Stress Wave| node2[PZT Disc Sensing Element]
    node2 -->|Charge Q Generation| node3[Charge Amplifier with C and R]
    node3 -->|High Pass Filter| node4[Band Pass Filter 5 to 15 kHz]
    node4 -->|Comparator| node5[Knock Detection Threshold Logic]
    node5 -->|Yes or No Signal| node6[Engine ECU]
    node6 -->|Retard Ignition Timing| node7[Ignition Coil Driver]
    node7 -->|High Voltage Pulse| node8[Spark Plug]
    node8 -->|Combustion Optimization| node1
```

## 4.3 Piezoelectric Coefficient Tensor Map (Automotive-Relevant Modes)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph mode1Axial
        n1[Force Along Axis 3] --> n2[Generate d33 Coefficient] --> n3[Poling Direction Mode Used in Knock Sensor]
    end
    subgraph mode2Transverse
        n4[Force Perpendicular to Axis 3] --> n5[Generate d31 Coefficient] --> n6[Used in Bimorph Cantilever Accelerometer]
    end
    subgraph mode3Shear
        n7[Shear Force on Plane 12] --> n8[Generate d15 Coefficient] --> n9[Used in Angular Rate Sensors]
    end
```

## 4.4 Piezoelectric Fuel Injector — Functional Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[ECU Command Pulse] -->|Voltage Signal| nodeB[Driver Stage with Inductor]
    nodeB -->|High Voltage Amplification| nodeC[Multilayer PZT Stack Actuator]
    nodeC -->|Mechanical Stroke deltaL| nodeD[Coupler Hydraulic Lever]
    nodeD -->|Needle Lift| nodeE[Injector Nozzle Needle]
    nodeE -->|Spray Pattern| nodeF[Combustion Chamber]
    nodeF -->|Combustion Pressure Feedback| nodeG[Piezo Pressure Sensor]
    nodeG -->|Closed Loop Signal| nodeA
```

## 4.5 Decision Matrix — When to Use Piezoelectric vs Other Sensing Principles

| **Parameter** | **Piezoelectric** | **Capacitive** | **Piezoresistive** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Frequency response | **Excellent (kHz to MHz)** | Moderate | Low |
| Static measurement | Not possible (charge leaks) | Excellent | Good |
| Sensitivity | High | Moderate | Moderate |
| Temperature range | Limited by Curie temperature | Wide | Moderate |
| Automotive use | Knock, crash, ultrasonic | Pressure, position | MAP, TP sensors |
| Cost | High | Moderate | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** When asked in the exam to *justify* the choice of a piezoelectric sensor in a given automotive application, your answer must reference **at least two of the above comparison parameters** (most commonly *frequency response* and *dynamic-only measurement*).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the piezoelectric effect. Distinguish between the direct and converse piezoelectric effects with one automotive application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** The piezoelectric effect is the phenomenon by which certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials generate an internal electric charge when subjected to mechanical stress (direct effect) and conversely deform mechanically when an electric field is applied across them (converse effect).
- **Direct Effect (1 Mark):** Mechanical energy is converted to electrical energy. *Automotive application:* **Knock sensor** on the engine block, where engine vibration generates a charge that is interpreted by the ECU to detect abnormal combustion.
- **Converse Effect (1 Mark):** Electrical energy is converted to mechanical displacement. *Automotive application:* **Piezoelectric fuel injector**, where an applied voltage causes a PZT stack to extend and open the injector nozzle for precise fuel metering.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain why the piezoelectric effect is not observed in crystals with a center of symmetry. Name any two piezoelectric materials used in automotive sensors.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Center of Symmetry Argument (2 Marks):** In a centrosymmetric crystal, the unit cell has a center of inversion. When a mechanical stress is applied, the resulting displacements of the positive and negative charge sub-lattices are symmetric about this center. The net dipole moment therefore remains zero, and no electric charge appears on the crystal faces. Only **non-centrosymmetric** crystals (such as quartz, PZT, and barium titanate) display a net polarization under stress, and hence exhibit the piezoelectric effect.
- **Two Materials (1 Mark):**
  1. **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)** — used in knock sensors and fuel injectors.
  2. **Quartz ($SiO_2$)** — used in pressure sensors and timing references.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

### Question A — Option 1 [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the constitutive equations of the piezoelectric effect and clearly explain the meaning of each term in the equations. List the four primary piezoelectric coefficients used to characterize a material. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **automotive piezoelectric knock sensor**. Derive the expression for the open-circuit voltage generated and discuss how the ECU processes this signal. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Constitutive Equations (7 Marks)

The complete mathematical description of a piezoelectric material couples electrical and mechanical variables. The two constitutive equations in **tensor form** are:

**Direct Effect (Sensor Mode):**
$$D_i = d_{ij} \cdot T_j + \varepsilon_{ij}^T \cdot E_i$$

**Converse Effect (Actuator Mode):**
$$S_j = s_{ij}^E \cdot T_j + d_{ij}^t \cdot E_i$$

**Meaning of Terms (3 Marks):**
- $D_i$ = Electric displacement vector (C/m²) — charge per unit area on the electrode.
- $T_j$ = Mechanical stress tensor (N/m²) — applied force per unit area.
- $E_i$ = Electric field vector (V/m) — applied voltage per unit length.
- $S_j$ = Mechanical strain tensor — fractional deformation of the material.
- $d_{ij}$ = Piezoelectric strain coefficient matrix (C/N or m/V) — couples stress and electric field.
- $\varepsilon_{ij}^T$ = Permittivity tensor at constant stress (F/m).
- $s_{ij}^E$ = Elastic compliance tensor at constant electric field (m²/N).
- $d_{ij}^t$ = Transpose of the piezoelectric coefficient matrix.

**Four Primary Piezoelectric Coefficients (2 Marks):**
1. **$d_{33}$** — Longitudinal mode coefficient (force and charge along the poling axis).
2. **$d_{31}$** — Transverse mode coefficient (force perpendicular, charge along poling axis).
3. **$d_{15}$** — Shear mode coefficient.
4. **$k$** — Electromechanical coupling factor (dimensionless efficiency).

**Physical Significance (2 Marks):**
- The first equation shows that mechanical stress generates an electric displacement → **direct effect** used in sensors.
- The second equation shows that an applied electric field produces mechanical strain → **converse effect** used in actuators.
- The **same coefficient $d_{ij}$** appears in both equations, satisfying the **thermodynamic Maxwell reciprocity principle**.

---

#### Part (b) — Knock Sensor Working (7 Marks)

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**
The knock sensor is mounted on the engine block. Inside the sensor, a **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate) piezoelectric disc** is mechanically coupled to a seismic mass. When the engine runs, the cylinder block vibrates. Normal combustion produces smooth low-amplitude vibrations. However, when **abnormal combustion (knock)** occurs, the cylinder pressure oscillates sharply at high frequency, producing a strong mechanical stress wave on the PZT disc. This stress, via the **direct piezoelectric effect**, generates a charge proportional to the stress amplitude.

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):**
The signal flow is: Engine Vibration → PZT Disc → Charge Generation → Charge Amplifier → Band-Pass Filter (5–15 kHz) → Comparator with Threshold → ECU → Ignition Timing Retardation.

**Derivation of Open-Circuit Voltage (2 Marks):**
The force $F$ on the disc produces a stress $\sigma = F / A$. The charge is $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$. The disc capacitance is $C_p = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A / t$. Therefore:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F \cdot t}{\varepsilon_r \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot A}$$

The ECU compares this voltage to a pre-programmed threshold. If $V_{oc}$ exceeds the threshold, the ECU retards the ignition timing by typically 2°–5° to suppress knock.

---

### Question B — Option 2 [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the working of a **piezoelectric fuel injector** with a block diagram. Derive the expression for the mechanical displacement produced by a multilayer PZT stack actuator. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the **electromechanical coupling factor** $k$ of a piezoelectric material? Explain its significance. A piezoelectric material has $d_{33} = 300 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N, $\varepsilon_{33}^T = 1500 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, and $s_{33}^E = 15 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/N. Calculate the value of $k_{33}$ for this material. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Piezoelectric Fuel Injector (7 Marks)

**Working (3 Marks):**
In a piezoelectric fuel injector, a **multilayer PZT stack** acts as the actuator. The ECU sends a high-voltage pulse (typically 100–200 V) to the PZT stack. By the **converse piezoelectric effect**, the stack expands by a precise, repeatable displacement $\Delta L = d_{33} \cdot V \cdot n$, where $n$ is the number of layers. This expansion pushes a hydraulic coupler, which lifts the injector needle, opening the nozzle and allowing high-pressure fuel to be sprayed into the combustion chamber. The system can perform **multiple precise injections per combustion cycle** (pilot, main, and post injections), improving fuel economy and reducing emissions.

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):**
ECU → Driver Stage (Inductor Boost) → PZT Stack Actuator → Hydraulic Coupler → Injector Needle → Nozzle → Combustion Chamber.

**Derivation (2 Marks):**
For a single PZT layer of thickness $t$ under voltage $V$, the strain is $S = d_{33} V / t$ and the displacement is $\Delta t = d_{33} \cdot V$. For $n$ layers in series mechanically: $\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$. For $n$ layers in parallel mechanically with each layer seeing voltage $V/n$: $\Delta L = d_{33} \cdot V$. In automotive practice, a stack of $n = 200$–$400$ thin PZT layers is used to achieve stroke lengths of $30$–$50\ \mu m$ at manageable voltages.

---

#### Part (b) — Coupling Factor & Numerical (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The **electromechanical coupling factor $k$** is a dimensionless figure of merit that quantifies the efficiency with which a piezoelectric material converts energy between electrical and mechanical forms. It is defined as:

$$k^2 = \frac{\text{Converted (Stored) Energy}}{\text{Input Energy}}$$

For the longitudinal mode:

$$k_{33}^2 = \frac{d_{33}^2}{s_{33}^E \cdot \varepsilon_{33}^T}$$

**Significance (2 Marks):**
- $k = 0$ means no electromechanical coupling (no piezoelectric activity).
- $k = 1$ means perfect conversion (theoretical maximum).
- For real materials, $k$ ranges from **0.3 (low-coupling quartz)** to **0.7 (high-coupling PZT)**.
- A high $k$ is desirable for both sensors (high voltage output) and actuators (large mechanical stroke).

**Numerical Calculation (3 Marks):**
Given:
- $d_{33} = 300 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N
- $\varepsilon_{33}^T = 1500 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 1.328 \times 10^{-8}$ F/m
- $s_{33}^E = 15 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/N

Step 1 — Numerator:
$$d_{33}^2 = (300 \times 10^{-12})^2 = 90{,}000 \times 10^{-24} = 9 \times 10^{-20}$$

Step 2 — Denominator:
$$s_{33}^E \cdot \varepsilon_{33}^T = (15 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (1.328 \times 10^{-8}) = 1.992 \times 10^{-19}$$

Step 3 — Coupling factor:
$$k_{33}^2 = \frac{9 \times 10^{-20}}{1.992 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.4518$$

$$k_{33} = \sqrt{0.4518} = 0.672$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $k_{33} \approx 0.67$ — this is typical of a high-quality PZT ceramic, confirming the material is suitable for both sensing and actuation in automotive applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Piezoelectric Questions (2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Confusing units of $d_{33}$:** Students often write $d_{33}$ in C/N for one equation and m/V for another without realizing these are numerically identical units of the same coefficient. Always write the unit explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by area in the $Q = D \cdot A$ step:** A common 1-mark deduction in 14-mark derivations. The charge on the electrode is **electric displacement times area**, not just electric displacement.
> 3. **Missing the $C_p = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A / t$ step:** When deriving $V_{oc}$, many students jump from $Q$ directly to $V$ without explicitly computing the disc capacitance. Examiners specifically look for this intermediate step.
> 4. **Confusing *piezoelectric* with *piezoresistive*:** The piezoelectric effect produces a **charge or voltage**, whereas the piezoresistive effect produces a **resistance change**. Examiners frequently test this distinction in 3-mark questions.
> 5. **In the numerical problem, students often forget to convert mm to m** before substituting into the formulas. This leads to an answer off by a factor of 1000 and full mark loss.
> 6. **Naming PZT without expanding the abbreviation** as "Lead Zirconate Titanate" — a 0.5-mark deduction in 3-mark questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Piezoelectric (Module 4):**
> - **Definition:** Mechanical stress → electric charge (direct); Electric field → mechanical strain (converse).
> - **Necessary Condition:** Crystal must be **non-centrosymmetric** (no center of symmetry).
> - **Two Key Materials:** **PZT** (high sensitivity, used in sensors + actuators) and **Quartz** (high stability, used in pressure sensors and timing).
> - **Most-used Coefficient:** $d_{33}$ — the longitudinal piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N or m/V).
> - **Sensor Formula:** $Q = d_{33} F$ and $V_{oc} = d_{33} F t / (\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A)$.
> - **Actuator Formula:** $\Delta L = d_{33} V$ (single layer) or $\Delta L = n d_{33} V$ (stack of $n$ layers).
> - **Equivalent Circuit:** Voltage source $V = Q / C_p$ in series with disc capacitance $C_p$.
> - **Curie Temperature $T_C$:** Above this temperature, the material **loses** its piezoelectric property. For PZT, $T_C \approx 350^\circ$C.
> - **Resonance Frequency:** Depends on **geometry** (diameter for radial mode, thickness for thickness mode) and material properties.
> - **Coupling Factor $k$:** Dimensionless efficiency of energy conversion. PZT has $k \approx 0.5$–$0.7$.
> - **Cannot measure static signals:** The charge leaks away through the input impedance — piezo sensors are inherently **AC-coupled / dynamic-only**.
> - **Five Major Automotive Applications:** Knock sensor, piezoelectric fuel injector, crash sensor (airbag), ultrasonic parking sensor, and seat-belt pretensioner.
> - **Charge Coefficient units to remember:** $1\ \text{C/N} = 1\ \text{m/V}$ (this equivalence is frequently asked in KTU viva).
> - **Constitutive Equations:** $D = d T + \varepsilon^T E$ and $S = s^E T + d_t E$ — memorize both forms.
> - **PVDF is a flexible polymer piezoelectric** — used in crash sensors where conformability to curved surfaces is required.
> - **Piezoelectric vs Piezoresistive:** Piezoelectric → charge/voltage output; Piezoresistive → resistance change output.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Networks in Vehicles: Introduction to Vehicle Communication Systems - CAN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT501 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Communication Networks in Vehicles: Introduction to Vehicle Communication Systems - CAN # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Controller Area Network (CAN)** is a robust, **multi-master, message-oriented serial bus protocol** standardised by **ISO 11898** that allows multiple Electronic Control Units (ECUs) within a vehicle to communicate with each other **without the need for a host computer**. It was originally developed by **Robert Bosch GmbH** in **1986** and is officially documented in Bosch Specification **2.0 (Parts A & B)** and the international standard **ISO 11898-1 (Data Link Layer)** and **ISO 11898-2 (High-Speed Physical Layer)**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme automotive syllabus, CAN is positioned as the **backbone in-vehicle multiplexing protocol** because it solves the historical problem of **point-to-point wiring harness complexity** in modern automobiles. ## 1.2 Why Do Vehicles Need a Communication Network? A modern premium vehicle contains approximately **80 to 150 ECUs**. If every ECU were wired point-to-point (each signal having its own dedicated wire), the wiring harness would: - Weigh more than **50 kg**. - Contain over **1,500 wires** and **3,000+ connectors**. - Be the **third heaviest** and **third most expensive** component after the engine and chassis. - Be highly prone to electrical faults (open circuits, short circuits, EMI). Therefore, a **multiplexed serial bus** like CAN is essential. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (The Nervous System of the Car) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy – The Human Nervous System:** > Think of the automobile as a human body. The **ECUs** are the **brain regions** (engine ECU = motor cortex, ABS ECU = cerebellum, Body Control Module = autonomic nervous system). The **CAN bus** is the **spinal cord and nervous system** that allows these brain regions to exchange reflex signals at very high speed without each one needing a direct dedicated neural wire to every other region. Just like a nerve impulse (action potential) is a **standardised electrical pulse**, a CAN message is a **standardised serial frame** carrying meaningful data. ## 1.4 Evolution of In-Vehicle Networks | Generation | Communication Method | Limitation | |---|---|---| | 1950s – 1970s | Direct mechanical / hydraulic linkages | Slow, bulky | | 1980s | Point-to-point wiring | Wire harness explosion | | 1991 | **CAN introduced by Mercedes-Benz W140 (S-Class)** | Standardisation | | 2000s | **LIN, MOST, FlexRay** added for sub-systems | Speed/hierarchy | | 2020s | **CAN FD, Automotive Ethernet** | High bandwidth demands | ## 1.5 The Core Salient Features of CAN (High-Yield KTU Points) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 8 Salient Properties of CAN You Must Memorise for KTU:** > 1. **Multi-master** – Any node can transmit when the bus is idle (no central host). > 2. **Message-prioritised** – Messages carry an **11-bit (Standard CAN)** or **29-bit (Extended CAN)** identifier, and lower ID = higher priority. > 3. **Non-destructive bitwise arbitration** – The highest-priority message always wins without corrupting any data. > 4. **Broadcast** – Every node receives every transmitted message; filtering is done in software. > 5. **Robust error detection** – **CRC-15**, **Bit Stuffing**, **ACK slot**, **Form check** provide a residual error probability of **< 4.7 × 10⁻¹¹** (per Bosch). > 6. **Fault confinement** – Each node maintains an internal **Transmit Error Counter (TEC)** and **Receive Error Counter (REC)** that decide whether a node becomes Error-Active, Error-Passive, or Bus-Off. > 7. **Differential signalling** – Uses **CAN_H** and **CAN_L** lines (twisted pair) for high noise immunity. > 8. **Speed vs Distance trade-off** – Higher bit rate = shorter bus (see Formula Sheet). ## 1.6 CAN Communication Speed Classes | Class | Speed | Standard | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | **High-Speed CAN (HS-CAN)** | **125 kbps – 1 Mbps** | ISO 11898-2 | Powertrain, Chassis, ABS, Airbag | | **Low-Speed CAN (LS-CAN / Fault-Tolerant CAN)** | **5 – 125 kbps** | ISO 11898-3 | Body electronics (doors, seats) | | **CAN FD (Flexible Data-rate)** | **Up to 5 Mbps (data phase)** | ISO 11898-1:2015 | Modern ECUs requiring larger payloads | # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 OSI Model Position of CAN A common KTU exam question is: *"At which OSI layers does CAN operate?"* > [!NOTE] > **CAN explicitly defines only Layers 1 (Physical) and 2 (Data Link)** of the OSI 7-layer model. Higher-layer protocols like **CANopen, J1939, OBD-II, DeviceNet, UDS** sit on top of CAN as application-layer protocols. | OSI Layer | CAN Implementation | |---|---| | Layer 7 – Application | CANopen / J1939 / OBD-II | | Layer 6 – Presentation | Not explicitly defined | | Layer 5 – Session | Not explicitly defined | | Layer 4 – Transport | Not explicitly defined | | Layer 3 – Network | Addressed by CAN Identifier (not IP) | | **Layer 2 – Data Link** | **CAN Protocol (LLC + MAC sub-layers)** | | **Layer 1 – Physical** | **ISO 11898-2 (HS) / -3 (LS)** | ## 2.2 The CAN Physical Layer – Differential Voltage Logic CAN uses **two wires**: **CAN_H** (high) and **CAN_L** (low). These are always **twisted together (twisted pair)** to reject common-mode noise via differential signalling. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two Logical Bus States:** > - **Dominant (Logical '0')** → CAN_H ≈ **3.5 V**, CAN_L ≈ **1.5 V**, Differential Voltage (V_diff) ≈ **2.0 V**. *Dominant wins arbitration.* > - **Recessive (Logical '1')** → CAN_H ≈ **2.5 V**, CAN_L ≈ **2.5 V**, Differential Voltage (V_diff) ≈ **0 V**. This is critical because the CAN bus behaves like a **wired-AND** circuit: any node transmitting a dominant bit **overrides** any node transmitting a recessive bit simultaneously. ## 2.3 CAN Message Frame Structure (HIGH-YIELD for KTU 14-mark questions) The standard CAN Data Frame has the following structure (11-bit Standard ID): | Field | Length (bits) | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **SOF** (Start of Frame) | 1 | Single dominant bit – synchronises all nodes | | **Arbitration Field** | 12 (11-bit ID + 1 RTR) | Determines bus access priority | | **Control Field** | 6 | DLC (4 bits) + IDE + r0 | | **Data Field** | **0 – 8 bytes (64 bits)** | Actual payload | | **CRC Field** | 16 (15 bits + delimiter) | Cyclic Redundancy Check for error detection | | **ACK Field** | 2 | Any receiver acknowledges successful receipt | | **EOF** (End of Frame) | 7 | All recessive – marks frame end | | **IFS** (Inter-Frame Space) | 3 | Recovery time between frames | > [!NOTE] > **CAN FD upgrade**: The Data Field can be **0 – 64 bytes** in CAN FD (Flexible Data-rate), and the data phase can switch to a higher bit rate than the arbitration phase. ## 2.4 Other CAN Frame Types | Frame Type | Purpose | |---|---| | **Data Frame** | Carries payload (the normal data frame) | | **Remote Frame** | A node requests data from another node (RTR bit = recessive) | | **Error Frame** | Any node detecting an error transmits this to destroy the corrupted frame | | **Overload Frame** | Used by a receiver to request a delay before the next data/remote frame | ## 2.5 Bit Stuffing (Transparency Rule) > [!IMPORTANT] > After **5 consecutive identical bits**, a **stuff bit of opposite polarity** is automatically inserted by the transmitter (and removed by the receiver).
