# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Bank]**

> **Q:** Differentiate between the **Static Segment** and **Dynamic Segment** of a FlexRay communication cycle. Mention any **two** functional differences.

### Model Answer (3 marks):
1. **Static Segment** uses **TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access)** with fixed, pre-assigned slots, where each node transmits in its reserved slot regardless of having data. This gives **strict determinism** (hard real-time). Dynamic Segment uses **FTDMA** with **mini-slots** that expand into full slots only on demand, providing **bounded but soft real-time latency**. *(2 marks)*
2. The Static Segment is suitable for **safety-critical, time-triggered data** (e.g., x-by-wire), while the Dynamic Segment is used for **best-effort, event-triggered traffic** (e.g., diagnostics, body-control). *(1 mark)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

> **Q:** What is the **maximum payload size** of a FlexRay frame? How does this compare with **CAN**? Why is this difference significant for chassis-domain applications?

### Model Answer (3 marks):
1. FlexRay allows a **variable payload of 0 to 254 bytes** per frame, whereas **CAN** is limited to a **fixed 8-byte payload**. *(1 mark)*
2. This 30× larger payload enables transmission of **multi-axis sensor arrays, complex control vectors, and calibration blocks** in a single frame, reducing protocol overhead. *(1 mark)*
3. For **chassis-domain** applications (adaptive dampers, drive-by-wire), this bandwidth headroom supports time-critical control without fragmentation, which CAN cannot provide at higher data volumes. *(1 mark)*

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [Choice 1]

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Past Pattern Adapted]**
>
> **A (a)** Explain the **FlexRay communication cycle architecture** in detail with a clearly labeled diagram. Discuss the role of each segment. *(7 marks)*
>
> **B (b)** A FlexRay cluster operates at **10 Mbps**. The static segment has **24 slots**, each with **gdStaticSlot = 50 MT**, where **1 MT = 1 µs**. Header = 5 bytes, Trailer = 3 bytes, Payload = 18 bytes, Channel idle = 11 bit times.
>   (i) Calculate the frame transmission time in microseconds.
>   (ii) Calculate the static-segment duration.
>   (iii) If the symbol window = 1 MT, NIT = 2 MT, and dynamic segment = 100 MT, compute the total communication cycle duration in microseconds. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

The FlexRay communication cycle is the fundamental repeating time structure on the bus. *(0.5 mark)*

| Segment | Function |
|---------|----------|
| **Static Segment** | TDMA-based, fixed slots for time-critical data; deterministic. *(1.5 marks)* |
| **Dynamic Segment** | FTDMA/mini-slot-based; dynamic bandwidth allocation for event-triggered data. *(1.5 marks)* |
| **Symbol Window** | Reserved for control symbols (Wake-up, Test, MTS). *(1 mark)* |
| **Network Idle Time** | Used for distributed clock synchronization. *(1 mark)* |

*(Refer to the cycle structure Mermaid diagram for full-mark visualization. 1 mark for labeled diagram.)*

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**(i) Frame transmission time** *(2 marks)*

$$
T_{frame} \;=\; (5 + 18 + 3) \times 8 \;+\; 11
$$

$$
=\; 26 \times 8 + 11 \;=\; 208 + 11 \;=\; 219 \;\text{bits}
$$

$$
T_{frame} \;=\; 219 \times 100 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{s} \;=\; 21.9 \;\mu s
$$

**(ii) Static-segment duration** *(2 marks)*

$$
T_{SS} \;=\; 24 \times 50 \;\text{MT} \;=\; 1200 \;\text{MT} \;=\; 1200 \;\mu s
$$

**(iii) Total cycle duration** *(3 marks)*

$$
T_{cycle} \;=\; T_{SS} \;+\; T_{DS} \;+\; T_{SW} \;+\; T_{NIT}
$$

$$
=\; 1200 + 100 + 1 + 2 \;=\; 1303 \;\text{MT} \;=\; 1303 \;\mu s \;\approx\; 1.303 \;\text{ms}
$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** [Choice 2]

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Past Pattern Adapted]**
>
> **B (a)** Draw and explain the **FlexRay frame structure** with header, payload, and trailer details. Mention the role of the **Header CRC** and the **Cycle Count** field. *(7 marks)*
>
> **B (b)** Compare **FlexRay, CAN, and LIN** in terms of **maximum data rate, topology, determinism, and typical application domain**. Tabulate the answer. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

A FlexRay frame has three segments plus physical preambles:

1. **Header Segment (5 bytes)** — contains:
   - 5 reserved/indicator bits (Reserved, Payload Preamble, Null Frame, Sync, Startup)
   - **Frame ID (11 bits)** — slot identifier (0–2047) *(0.5 mark)*
   - **Payload Length (7 bits)** — 0–254 bytes *(0.5 mark)*
   - **Header CRC (11 bits)** — protects the header; uses CRC-11 polynomial. Detects bit errors in header before payload is accepted. *(1 mark)*
   - **Cycle Count (6 bits)** — values 0–63, identifies which cycle within the repeating 64-cycle counter. Used to support multi-cycle schedules (e.g., a signal transmitted every 4 cycles). *(1 mark)*

2. **Payload Segment (0–254 bytes)** — application data. *(0.5 mark)*

3. **Trailer Segment (3 bytes)** — contains:
   - **16-bit Frame CRC** (CRC-16 polynomial) — protects the payload and is computed over Header + Payload. *(0.5 mark)*
   - **8-bit Boundary Delimiter** — confirms frame end. *(0.5 mark)*

*(2 marks for clear labeled diagram — refer to Frame Structure Mermaid block.)*

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

| Parameter | CAN | LIN | FlexRay |
|-----------|-----|-----|---------|
| **Max data rate** | 1 Mbps (CAN 2.0) / 5 Mbps (CAN-FD) | 20 kbps | 10 Mbps per channel (20 Mbps dual) |
| **Topology** | Bus | Bus (single master) | Bus, Star, Hybrid |
| **Access method** | CSMA/CR + arbitration | Master/slave polling | TDMA + FTDMA hybrid |
| **Determinism** | Non-deterministic (best-effort) | Deterministic (scheduled polling) | Strictly deterministic in static segment |
| **Payload size** | 0–8 bytes | 0–8 bytes | 0–254 bytes |
| **Error handling** | CRC-15 + ACK + retransmit | Checksum | Header CRC-11 + CRC-16, dual-channel redundancy |
| **Application domain** | Powertrain, body, chassis mid-speed | Body electronics (mirrors, seats) | Chassis high-speed, x-by-wire, ADAS |
| **Cable** | Twisted pair | Single wire | Twisted pair / POF |

*(0.5 mark per fully-correct row × 8 rows = 4 marks; 3 marks for concluding statement.)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU evaluations:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 11-bit channel idle time** in frame duration calculations — examiners specifically test this.
> 2. **Confusing microticks vs. macroticks** — microticks are oscillator-dependent; macroticks are the cluster-wide integer multiple.
> 3. **Claiming CAN is deterministic** — CAN is non-deterministic due to CSMA/CR; FlexRay's static segment is deterministic.
> 4. **Wrong payload size** — FlexRay = 0–254 bytes (NOT 8 like CAN).
> 5. **Mixing up TDMA and CSMA** — write clearly: "FlexRay Static Segment uses TDMA" — do not write "FlexRay uses TDMA only" because dynamic segment is FTDMA.
> 6. **Cycle Count field** is 6 bits → cycles 0 to 63. Frame ID is 11 bits → 0 to 2047.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FlexRay = Time-Triggered + Event-Triggered** hybrid protocol; static segment = TDMA (deterministic), dynamic segment = FTDMA (bounded).
- **Bit rate = 10 Mbps per channel; 20 Mbps aggregate** when both channels active.
- **Communication Cycle = SS + DS + SW + NIT**; each cycle repeats with a 6-bit cycle counter (0–63).
- **Frame = 5-byte header + 0–254 byte payload + 3-byte trailer**; total minimum frame = 8 bytes.
- **Header contains**: 5 indicator bits, Frame ID (11 bits), Payload Length (7 bits), Header CRC (11 bits), Cycle Count (6 bits).
- **Trailer contains**: 16-bit CRC, 8-bit boundary delimiter.
- **Channel idle time** after every frame = **11 bit times** (electrical physical layer).
- **Slot duration**: $gdSlot = gdActionPointOffset + gdStaticSlotLength + gdChannelIdleTime$.
- **Cycle duration**: $T_{cycle} = T_{SS} + T_{DS} + T_{SW} + T_{NIT}$.
- **Topologies**: Bus, Star, Hybrid (multi-star) — FlexRay supports all three.
- **Physical media**: Twisted-pair differential copper, or Plastic Optical Fiber (POF).
- **Synchronization**: Distributed, less than 1 µs accuracy across the cluster; uses sync frames transmitted in the static segment; correction applied in NIT.
- **Wake-up**: Cluster starts only after a wake-up symbol on at least one channel, followed by a startup frame from a designated **cold-start node**.
- **Dual channels** (Channel A and Channel B) provide **redundancy** — critical for x-by-wire safety.
- **FlexRay standards**: ISO 17458-1 through ISO 17458-5 (parts cover general info, electrical physical layer, optical physical layer, data link layer, conformance test).
- **Application areas**: Steer-by-wire, brake-by-wire, adaptive cruise control, active suspension, and as a gateway between CAN/LIN sub-buses.
- **Comparison anchors** (must memorize):
  - FlexRay > CAN (CAN) > LIN (LIN) in speed
  - FlexRay = strictly deterministic; CAN = best-effort; LIN = scheduled polling.
  - FlexRay = 254 bytes; CAN = 8 bytes; LIN = 8 bytes.

> **Final KTU Tip:** Always quote **numeric values** (10 Mbps, 254 bytes, 5+3 byte frame overhead) in 14-mark answers — they are easy marks and signal to the examiner that you have memorized the protocol specifics.
